| Deprecated option                        | Warning condition                    | Nonfatal but visible                 | Option must be rejected                          |
| File/resource lifetime                   | `with-*` macro                       | Cleanup on all exits                 | Custom resource protocol needed                  |
| Temporary global/contextual setting      | Dynamic binding                      | Scoped effect                        | Ordinary parameter would be clearer              |
| Implementation-specific operation        | Wrapper + reader conditional         | Isolates non-portability             | Need multi-implementation support                |
| Foreign call                             | Narrow wrapper + condition           | Safety and conversion boundary       | Performance requires batching                    |
| Shared mutable state                     | Lock/queue/ownership protocol        | Prevent races                        | Need actor/task architecture                     |
| Thread/resource cleanup                  | `unwind-protect` + library lifecycle | Nonlocal exit safety                 | Implementation has special termination semantics |

### Common boundary anti-patterns — `package-module confusion`, `string errors`, `eval parser`, `hidden state`

| Anti-pattern                         | Why it is wrong or incomplete                 | Better pattern                                    |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| “Package equals module”              | Packages manage symbols, not files or loading | Use packages + ASDF systems distinctly            |
| Export everything                    | Removes API/internal distinction              | Export stable constructors, functions, conditions |
| Use `pkg::internal` in normal code   | Couples to implementation detail              | Request/export API or isolate dependency          |
| Manual `load` chain for project      | Fragile and non-reproducible                  | ASDF system                                       |
| Top-level startup side effects       | Loading becomes execution                     | Explicit start function                           |
| Bare string errors in public API     | Not type-handleable                           | Define condition classes                          |
| Catch broad `error` and return `nil` | Destroys diagnostics                          | Catch specific conditions                         |
| Use `read` for user input            | Reader is not a safe generic parser           | Use format-specific parser and validation         |
| Use `eval` for dynamic lookup        | Code execution and environment confusion      | Use functions, closures, maps, or macros          |
| Rely on GC for resources             | GC does not close protocols deterministically | `with-*` / `unwind-protect`                       |
| Mutate special variables globally    | Leaks context across calls                    | Dynamic `let` binding                             |
| Scatter `#+sbcl` everywhere          | Non-portability infects domain code           | Adapter package                                   |
| Depend on live REPL state            | Clean load may fail                           | Restart image and load ASDF system                |
| Change macro syntax casually         | Macros are source-level API                   | Deprecate and support migration                   |

### Part 5 practical review artifact — `boundary-first design`

| Question                                     | If yes                                                   | If no                                                 |
| -------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Is this symbol public?                       | Export it and document the contract.                     | Keep it unexported or internal.                       |
| Does this code need file/load ordering?      | Put it in ASDF components.                               | A simple file/load may be enough for experiments.     |
| Can callers handle this failure differently? | Define a condition type.                                 | A simple `error` or `nil` may be enough.              |
| Can `nil` be a valid result?                 | Return a presence value too.                             | Single `nil` absence may be acceptable.               |
| Is recovery policy context-dependent?        | Offer restarts.                                          | Choose local default or signal condition.             |
| Does a resource need cleanup?                | Use `with-*` or `unwind-protect`.                        | GC-managed memory alone may be enough.                |
| Does the function mutate input?              | Document ownership and return convention.                | Prefer non-destructive naming/behavior.               |
| Is input external or untrusted?              | Parse and validate; do not `eval`.                       | Trusted internal data may use richer Lisp mechanisms. |
| Is behavior implementation-specific?         | Isolate behind wrapper/feature condition.                | Keep code portable ANSI/Common Lisp.                  |
| Is dynamic context appropriate?              | Use special variables with earmuffs and dynamic binding. | Pass explicit arguments.                              |
| Will changing this break callers?            | Treat it as compatibility boundary.                      | Refactor freely inside internal code.                 |

## PART 6 — Standard Library and Core Ecosystem Reference by Task Pattern

### Standard library and ecosystem orientation — `ANSI Common Lisp`, `ASDF`, `Quicklisp`, `UIOP`, ecosystem conventions

Common Lisp has a large standard language, but not a modern all-inclusive standard platform. The standard gives substantial support for data structures, sequences, numbers, symbols, packages, pathnames, streams, printing, reading, CLOS, conditions, and many utility functions. Modern application work usually adds ecosystem libraries for regular expressions, JSON, HTTP, testing, logging, command-line interfaces, portability, dates, threads, and deployment.

This means Part 6 must be read with a layered model:

| Layer                              | Role                                               | Examples                                                                         | Practical rule                                                                     |
| ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ANSI Common Lisp standard library  | Portable core language facilities                  | sequences, hash tables, pathnames, streams, conditions, CLOS, `format`, packages | Learn this deeply first.                                                           |
| Implementation-provided facilities | Compiler/runtime/OS/threads/FFI extensions         | SBCL-specific packages, implementation debugger/profiler/thread APIs             | Use deliberately and isolate when portability matters.                             |
| ASDF                               | System definition, compilation/loading, operations | `.asd` files, `asdf:load-system`, test operations                                | Treat as the standard practical project layer.                                     |
| UIOP                               | Portability utilities distributed with ASDF        | paths, files, environment, subprocess, image utilities                           | Use for practical OS/path utilities instead of reinventing.                        |
| Quicklisp                          | Library acquisition/loading workflow               | `ql:quickload`                                                                   | Convenient for development, but production reproducibility needs extra discipline. |
| Community libraries                | Domain-specific tools                              | regex, JSON, HTTP, tests, logging, CLI, web                                      | Choose by maturity, maintenance, portability, and project needs.                   |

Common Lisp professional use is therefore not “standard library only” and not “library ecosystem first.” The correct order is:

1. Know the standard mechanisms.
2. Use ASDF to organize systems.
3. Use UIOP for common portability tasks.
4. Use Quicklisp to acquire libraries during development.
5. Choose external libraries deliberately.
6. Isolate implementation-specific behavior.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is importing expectations from Python or Node and assuming one dominant package manager, one canonical HTTP stack, one canonical JSON library, and one universal deployment workflow. Common Lisp’s ecosystem is smaller, older, more implementation-sensitive, and more library-by-library. This gives control, but requires more evaluation.

### System loading and dependency workflow — `ASDF`, `Quicklisp`, `load-system`, `quickload`, `.asd`

For real projects, the first ecosystem skill is loading systems correctly.

ASDF system definition:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:example
  :description "Example system."
  :depends-on (#:alexandria #:cl-ppcre)
  :components
  ((:file "packages")
   (:module "src"
    :serial t
    :components
    ((:file "text")
     (:file "users")
     (:file "main")))))
```

Load with ASDF:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
```

Load a Quicklisp-available system:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :alexandria)
```

Run tests if the system defines a test operation:

```lisp
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

| Task                                     | Tool/API                | Canonical use                 | Caveat                                                                       |
| ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Define a system                          | `asdf:defsystem`        | `.asd` file                   | Not the same as defining a package.                                          |
| Declare dependencies                     | `:depends-on`           | `:depends-on (#:alexandria)`  | Must match actual system names, not necessarily package names.               |
| Load own system                          | `asdf:load-system`      | `(asdf:load-system :example)` | Assumes ASDF can find the system.                                            |
| Load external library during development | `ql:quickload`          | `(ql:quickload :cl-ppcre)`    | Quicklisp convenience is not full reproducible dependency locking by itself. |
| Compile components                       | ASDF operations         | usually implicit through load | Compile/load phases can matter for macros.                                   |
| Test system                              | `asdf:test-system`      | project-specific test op      | Requires test system/op setup.                                               |
| Inspect system organization              | ASDF system definitions | read `.asd`                   | Dependency graph is part of project architecture.                            |

**Design meaning:** ASDF is not merely a loader. It is the system graph. It records how files, components, and dependencies fit together. Quicklisp is a library acquisition layer that can call ASDF to load systems.

**Failure-first explanation: Quicklisp loading is not the same as package use**

The tempting model is:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :some-library)
```

and then assuming all names are magically available.

The surprising behavior is that loading a system does not necessarily import its symbols into the current package. The library may define one or more packages, and the caller still needs to use package-qualified symbols, `:use`, or `:import-from`.

The correct explanation is that Quicklisp/ASDF loads systems; packages control symbol accessibility.

The professional rule of thumb is: **load systems with ASDF/Quicklisp; access APIs through packages.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is testing code only in a REPL where many libraries have already been loaded. In a clean image, missing ASDF dependencies or missing package imports will surface. Test from a clean load regularly.

### Files, paths, and directories — `pathname`, `merge-pathnames`, `probe-file`, `ensure-directories-exist`, `UIOP`

Common Lisp has standard pathnames, but practical filesystem work often benefits from UIOP utilities. Pathnames are structured path objects, not merely strings. This abstraction is portable in intent, but it also has historical complexity.

Standard examples:

```lisp
#P"data/config.sexp"
```

```lisp
(probe-file #P"data/config.sexp")
```

```lisp
(merge-pathnames "config.sexp" #P"/etc/example/")
```

Ensure directories exist:

```lisp
(ensure-directories-exist #P"output/reports/")
```

UIOP provides practical path utilities commonly used in ASDF-based projects:

```lisp
(uiop:ensure-pathname "data/config.sexp")
```

```lisp
(uiop:directory-exists-p #P"data/")
```

```lisp
(uiop:file-exists-p #P"data/config.sexp")
```

| Task                      | Standard CL                       | UIOP / ecosystem tendency   | Caveat                                        |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Represent path            | pathname, namestring              | `uiop:ensure-pathname`      | Strings are not pathnames.                    |
| Check file existence      | `probe-file`                      | `uiop:file-exists-p`        | Be clear whether file vs directory matters.   |
| Check directory existence | implementation/pathname handling  | `uiop:directory-exists-p`   | Directory behavior varies more than expected. |
| Merge paths               | `merge-pathnames`                 | UIOP helpers                | Defaults matter.                              |
| Create directories        | `ensure-directories-exist`        | UIOP helpers                | Pathname must denote intended directory/file. |
| List directory            | implementation-specific patterns  | UIOP directory utilities    | Portability details matter.                   |
| Temporary paths           | not a single universal core story | UIOP/library/implementation | Needs lifecycle and cleanup.                  |

Example: reading a project-relative file with a system-relative path is usually better than relying on current working directory. ASDF/UIOP can help with system-relative path patterns, though exact project conventions vary.

**Design meaning:** filesystem code is a boundary between Common Lisp’s pathname abstraction and the host operating system. Avoid ad hoc string concatenation for paths.

**Failure-first explanation: current working directory is a fragile dependency**

The tempting model is:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream "config.sexp")
  ...)
```

This works only if the process current directory is what the programmer expects.

The correct explanation is that relative paths are resolved against runtime defaults, not automatically against the source file or ASDF system.

The professional rule of thumb is: **make path origins explicit: user-provided path, current working directory, system-relative path, or configuration path.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is concatenating path strings manually:

```lisp
(concatenate 'string directory "/" filename)
```

This is non-portable and error-prone. Use pathname operations or UIOP helpers.

### Streams and I/O — `stream`, `with-open-file`, `read-line`, `write-line`, `format`, binary I/O

Streams are Common Lisp’s abstraction for input and output. Files, strings, terminal output, and other sources/sinks are represented through stream protocols.

Read a line:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream #P"input.txt" :direction :input)
  (read-line stream nil nil))
```

Write lines:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream #P"output.txt"
                        :direction :output
                        :if-exists :supersede
                        :if-does-not-exist :create)
  (write-line "hello" stream))
```

Read all lines:

```lisp
(defun read-lines (path)
  (with-open-file (stream path :direction :input)
    (loop for line = (read-line stream nil nil)
          while line
          collect line)))
```

Write generated text:

```lisp
(defun write-report (path users)
  (with-open-file (stream path
                          :direction :output
                          :if-exists :supersede
                          :if-does-not-exist :create)
    (dolist (user users)
      (format stream "~A <~A>~%"
              (user-name user)
              (user-email user)))))
```

String output stream:

```lisp
(defun report-string (users)
  (with-output-to-string (stream)
    (dolist (user users)
      (format stream "~A~%" (user-name user)))))
```

| Task                        | API                                   | Canonical pattern                  | Caveat                                              |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| Read one object             | `read`                                | trusted Lisp data                  | Do not use unrestricted `read` for untrusted input. |
| Read one line               | `read-line`                           | text files                         | EOF handling must be explicit.                      |
| Write string with newline   | `write-line`                          | simple text output                 | Does not format objects like `format`.              |
| Write formatted text        | `format`                              | reports/log output                 | Format strings are a mini-language.                 |
| Write readable Lisp object  | `write`, `prin1`                      | Lisp-readable output when possible | Not all objects print readably.                     |
| Write human-readable object | `princ`, `print`                      | debugging/display                  | May not be readable as data.                        |
| Build string                | `with-output-to-string`               | efficient text construction        | Avoid repeated concatenation loops.                 |
| Open file safely            | `with-open-file`                      | scoped stream                      | Do not return stream outside scope.                 |
| Binary I/O                  | element-type options, read/write-byte | byte streams                       | Implementation and element type choices matter.     |

EOF handling:

```lisp
(read-line stream nil :eof)
```

Here `nil` suppresses EOF error, and `:eof` is the EOF value.

**Design meaning:** streams allow functions to be more composable. A function that writes to a stream can write to a file, string, terminal, or custom stream-like object.

**Failure-first explanation: returning a stream from `with-open-file` is a lifetime bug**

The tempting model is:

```lisp
(defun get-stream (path)
  (with-open-file (stream path)
    stream))
```

The stream is closed when the body exits. The caller receives a resource outside its lifetime.

Correct pattern:

```lisp
(defun read-file-first-line (path)
  (with-open-file (stream path)
    (read-line stream nil nil)))
```

or design an explicit open/close ownership protocol.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is reading with `read` when the file contains ordinary text. `read` reads Lisp objects. Use `read-line`, character functions, or a format-specific parser for non-Lisp text.

Another pitfall is writing output functions that always print to `t`. Prefer accepting a stream parameter or using `*standard-output*` deliberately.

### Printing and formatting — `format`, `write`, `prin1`, `princ`, `print`, pretty printing

Common Lisp’s output facilities are powerful. `format` is a full mini-language and one of the most important standard tools.

Basic `format`:

```lisp
(format t "Hello, ~A!~%" name)
```

Return a string instead of writing to a stream:

```lisp
(format nil "Hello, ~A!" name)
```

Common directives:

| Directive       | Meaning                                    | Example                              |
| --------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| `~A`            | Aesthetic output, like `princ`             | user-facing display                  |
| `~S`            | Standard/readable-ish output, like `prin1` | debugging/data-ish display           |
| `~%`            | Newline                                    | line break                           |
| `~&`            | Fresh line                                 | newline if not already at line start |
| `~D`            | Decimal integer                            | counts                               |
| `~X`            | Hexadecimal integer                        | byte/ID display                      |
| `~F`            | Fixed-format float                         | numeric display                      |
| `~{...~}`       | Iteration over list                        | repeated formatting                  |
| `~@[...~]`      | Conditional if argument non-`nil`          | optional text                        |
| `~:[...~;...~]` | Conditional false/true selection           | pluralization/simple branch          |

Examples:

```lisp
(format nil "~A has ~D message~:P." name count)
```

```lisp
(format t "~{~A~^, ~}~%" '("Ada" "Grace" "Edsger"))
;; Ada, Grace, Edsger
```

`write`, `prin1`, `princ`, and `print` differ in readability and formatting intent.

| Function | Intent                                   | Example use                       | Caveat                                |
| -------- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `write`  | General object writing with options      | configurable printing             | Many keyword options                  |
| `prin1`  | Print object readably when possible      | data/debug output                 | Not all objects readable              |
| `princ`  | Aesthetic/human output                   | user-facing strings               | Strings printed without quotes        |
| `print`  | Like `prin1` with newline/space behavior | quick debugging                   | Adds formatting                       |
| `pprint` | Pretty print                             | readable structured output        | Controlled by pretty printer settings |
| `format` | Formatted output                         | reports, messages, generated text | Mini-language can become unreadable   |

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp distinguishes human-facing output from machine-readable-ish output. `~A` and `~S` encode that distinction in `format`.

**Failure-first explanation: `~A` and `~S` are not interchangeable**

The tempting model is: “Both print the object.”

Example:

```lisp
(format nil "~A" "hello")
;; "hello" displayed without quotes

(format nil "~S" "hello")
;; "\"hello\"" style readable representation
```

The correct explanation is that `~A` is aesthetic; `~S` is more representation-oriented.

The professional rule of thumb is: **use `~A` for user-facing text; use `~S` for debugging, diagnostics, and values whose printed representation matters.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is writing extremely complex `format` strings. `format` can express loops, conditionals, alignment, pluralization, and pretty printing. That power can harm readability. If the format string becomes a program, consider helper functions or clearer construction.

### Reading and writing Lisp data — `read`, `write`, `*print-readably*`, `*read-eval*`, trust boundary

Common Lisp can read and write Lisp objects, but this is not the same as general safe serialization.

Write a Lisp object:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream #P"data.sexp"
                        :direction :output
                        :if-exists :supersede)
  (write '(:host "localhost" :port 8080)
         :stream stream
         :pretty t))
```

Read it back:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream #P"data.sexp" :direction :input)
  (read stream))
```

This pattern is useful for trusted internal data. It is not automatically safe for untrusted input.

Printer variables:

```lisp
(let ((*print-readably* t)
      (*print-circle* t))
  (write object :stream stream))
```

Reader-related safety variable:

```lisp
(let ((*read-eval* nil))
  (read stream))
```

`*read-eval*` controls `#.` read-time evaluation, but disabling it does not by itself turn the reader into a complete safe parser for arbitrary hostile input.

| Task                               | Use                                   | Caveat                             |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Save trusted symbolic config       | `write` / `read` with validation      | Developer-controlled only          |
| Debug print object                 | `prin1`, `pprint`, `format ~S`        | May not be readable                |
| Human display                      | `princ`, `format ~A`                  | Not machine-readable               |
| Read user input                    | format-specific parser                | Avoid unrestricted `read`          |
| Read Lisp data with less risk      | bind `*read-eval*` to `nil`, validate | Still a Lisp reader boundary       |
| Preserve circular/shared structure | `*print-circle*`                      | Reading requires compatible syntax |

**Design meaning:** Lisp-readable data is powerful because code and data share representation conventions. This is also why it is a trust boundary.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is assuming `*print-readably*` guarantees every object can be serialized and restored. Some objects cannot be printed readably. Another is assuming `*read-eval* nil` is sufficient for all untrusted input scenarios. It is a mitigation, not a complete validation strategy.

### Text processing — `string`, `character`, `search`, `subseq`, `string-trim`, `format`

Standard Common Lisp has useful string and character operations, but it does not standardize a regex library. For many tasks, standard string functions are enough.

| Task                  | Standard API                                            | Example                          |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Length                | `length`                                                | `(length "abc")`                 |
| Index character       | `char`, `aref`                                          | `(char "abc" 0)`                 |
| Substring             | `subseq`                                                | `(subseq "abcdef" 1 4)`          |
| Search substring      | `search`                                                | `(search "bc" "abcdef")`         |
| Find character        | `position`                                              | `(position #\@ email)`           |
| Trim characters       | `string-trim`, `string-left-trim`, `string-right-trim`  | `(string-trim " " input)`        |
| Change case           | `string-upcase`, `string-downcase`, `string-capitalize` | `(string-downcase name)`         |
| Compare exact         | `string=`, `string<`                                    | `(string= a b)`                  |
| Compare ignoring case | `string-equal`, `string-lessp`                          | `(string-equal a b)`             |
| Build string          | `with-output-to-string`, `format nil`                   | `(format nil "~A:~A" host port)` |

Examples:

```lisp
(defun normalize-name (name)
  (string-downcase
   (string-trim '(#\Space #\Tab #\Newline) name)))
```

```lisp
(defun email-domain (email)
  (let ((pos (position #\@ email)))
    (when pos
      (subseq email (1+ pos)))))
```

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp strings are sequences and arrays of characters. Many sequence functions therefore apply to strings, but text processing still needs attention to case, character sets, encodings, and external library behavior.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is using `eq` or `eql` to compare strings. Use `string=` for exact string content. Another pitfall is assuming empty string is false. In Common Lisp, `""` is true.

### Regular expressions — `cl-ppcre`, pattern matching, text validation

Common Lisp does not include regular expressions in the ANSI standard. The de facto ecosystem answer is commonly a regex library such as `cl-ppcre`.

Example style:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :cl-ppcre)
```

```lisp
(cl-ppcre:scan "^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$" username)
```

Extracting matches depends on the API used:

```lisp
(cl-ppcre:register-groups-bind (local domain)
    ("^([^@]+)@([^@]+)$" email)
  (list local domain))
```

| Task                   | Standard CL? | Ecosystem tool                   | Caveat                                 |
| ---------------------- | ------------ | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Exact substring search | Yes          | `search`                         | No regex needed.                       |
| Character position     | Yes          | `position`                       | No regex needed.                       |
| Simple validation      | Sometimes    | `cl-ppcre` if pattern-based      | Regex can become unreadable.           |
| Tokenization           | Partly       | `cl-ppcre:split` or parser       | Regex may be insufficient for grammar. |
| Complex parsing        | No           | parser combinators/custom parser | Do not force regex beyond its fit.     |

**Design meaning:** regex is an ecosystem feature, not a Common Lisp core syntax feature. Use it when the task is genuinely regular-pattern text matching.

**Failure-first explanation: regex is not a parser for every text format**

The tempting model is to validate structured formats with a growing regex. The surprising result is fragile unreadable patterns.

The correct explanation is that regular expressions are best for regular lexical patterns, not arbitrary nested grammars.

The professional rule of thumb is: **use standard string functions for simple text, regex for regular patterns, and a parser for structured grammars.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is using regex for tasks that `position`, `search`, or `string=` solve more clearly. Another is embedding large regex strings without tests or comments.

### Sequence and collection utilities — `sequence`, `map`, `reduce`, `sort`, `remove`, `substitute`

Standard Common Lisp’s sequence library is substantial. It supports lists, vectors, and strings through common operations.

| Task                        | Function                              | Notes                                             |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Transform                   | `map`, `mapcar`                       | `mapcar` returns list; `map` result type explicit |
| Filter                      | `remove-if`, `remove-if-not`          | Non-destructive                                   |
| Delete/filter destructively | `delete`, `delete-if`                 | May modify input                                  |
| Find                        | `find`, `find-if`                     | Returns item or `nil`                             |
| Locate                      | `position`, `position-if`             | Returns index or `nil`                            |
| Count                       | `count`, `count-if`                   | Common summary operation                          |
| Substitute                  | `substitute`, `substitute-if`         | Non-destructive                                   |
| Sort                        | `sort`, `stable-sort`                 | Destructive permission matters                    |
| Merge                       | `merge`                               | For sorted sequences                              |
| Reduce                      | `reduce`                              | Aggregation                                       |
| Test predicates             | `every`, `some`, `notany`, `notevery` | Predicate logic                                   |

Examples:

```lisp
(remove-if-not #'evenp '(1 2 3 4))
;; => (2 4)
```

```lisp
(position "Ada" names :test #'string=)
```

```lisp
(sort users #'string< :key #'user-name)
```

`sort` is destructive. If the original sequence must be preserved, copy first:

```lisp
(sort (copy-seq users) #'string< :key #'user-name)
```

| Common keyword   | Meaning                                              | Example                              |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `:key`           | Extract comparison value                             | `:key #'user-name`                   |
| `:test`          | Equality predicate                                   | `:test #'equal`                      |
| `:test-not`      | Negated test; generally less favored in modern style | prefer positive tests where possible |
| `:start`, `:end` | Sequence bounds                                      | process subsequence                  |
| `:from-end`      | Search/count from end                                | last match behavior                  |
| `:count`         | Limit number of changes/removals                     | partial operations                   |

**Design meaning:** sequence functions are the standard value-level collection abstraction. They should be preferred when they make the operation clearer than manual traversal.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is forgetting destructive behavior in functions such as `sort`, `delete`, `nreverse`, and `nsubstitute`. Another is using list-specific functions where sequence functions would allow strings or vectors too.

### Hash-table utilities — `make-hash-table`, `gethash`, `maphash`, equality test

Hash tables are standard and essential for mapping tasks.

```lisp
(defparameter *users-by-id*
  (make-hash-table :test 'equal))
```

Add/update:

```lisp
(setf (gethash "u-1" *users-by-id*) user)
```

Lookup:

```lisp
(multiple-value-bind (user present-p)
    (gethash "u-1" *users-by-id*)
  (when present-p
    user))
```

Iterate:

```lisp
(maphash (lambda (id user)
           (format t "~A => ~A~%" id (user-name user)))
         *users-by-id*)
```

Remove:

```lisp
(remhash "u-1" *users-by-id*)
```

Clear:

```lisp
(clrhash *users-by-id*)
```

| Task          | API                                | Caveat                                |
| ------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Create table  | `make-hash-table`                  | Choose `:test` correctly.             |
| Lookup        | `gethash`                          | Use second value when `nil` is valid. |
| Insert/update | `(setf (gethash key table) value)` | Mutates table.                        |
| Remove key    | `remhash`                          | Returns generalized boolean.          |
| Clear table   | `clrhash`                          | Destructive.                          |
| Count entries | `hash-table-count`                 | Does not imply iteration order.       |
| Iterate       | `maphash`                          | Order unspecified.                    |

**Failure-first explanation: hash table order is not a stable data model**

The tempting model is: “I inserted keys in this order, so iteration will follow it.”

The correct explanation is that standard hash tables are mappings, not ordered maps. Iteration order should not be part of program semantics.

The professional rule of thumb is: **if order matters, store order separately or use a representation that preserves order.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is using the wrong equality test. For string keys, use `:test 'equal` or another appropriate content comparison. `eq` is usually wrong for string keys.

### List utilities and utility libraries — `car`, `cdr`, `destructuring-bind`, `alexandria`, `serapeum`

Common Lisp has many list functions, and utility libraries often add convenience operations. The most widely used utility layer in many projects is `Alexandria`; some projects also use broader utility libraries such as `Serapeum`.

Standard list operations:

| Task               | Standard function/form  | Example                        |
| ------------------ | ----------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| First element      | `car`, `first`          | `(first xs)`                   |
| Rest of list       | `cdr`, `rest`           | `(rest xs)`                    |
| Construct          | `cons`, `list`, `list*` | `(cons x xs)`                  |
| Append             | `append`                | `(append xs ys)`               |
| Destructive append | `nconc`                 | owned lists only               |
| Reverse            | `reverse`, `nreverse`   | destructive vs non-destructive |
| Membership         | `member`, `member-if`   | use `:test`                    |
| Association lookup | `assoc`, `rassoc`       | alists                         |
| Destructure        | `destructuring-bind`    | structured list binding        |
| Tree substitution  | `subst`, `sublis`       | symbolic trees                 |

`destructuring-bind`:

```lisp
(destructuring-bind (name age role)
    '("Ada" 36 :researcher)
  (list :name name :age age :role role))
```

Nested destructuring:

```lisp
(destructuring-bind ((x y) (width height))
    '((10 20) (640 480))
  (list x y width height))
```

Alexandria is often used for utility functions that many Common Lisp programmers expect in practical projects, but it is not part of ANSI Common Lisp.

```lisp
(ql:quickload :alexandria)
```

Examples of utility-library roles include hash-table helpers, list helpers, control utilities, symbol utilities, and convenience macros. Exact function choice should be checked against the library documentation rather than assumed.

| Need                                   | Standard first? | Ecosystem utility when useful          | Caveat                                             |
| -------------------------------------- | --------------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Basic list operations                  | Yes             | Rarely needed                          | Learn standard operations first.                   |
| Common utilities missing from standard | Sometimes       | Alexandria                             | Adds dependency; know what is standard vs library. |
| More opinionated utility layer         | Sometimes       | Serapeum or project-specific utilities | Utility style may shape codebase.                  |
| Destructuring                          | Yes             | Additional pattern libraries optional  | Do not overcomplicate simple binding.              |

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp’s standard library is large but historically shaped. Utility libraries smooth common rough edges, but they should not replace learning the standard vocabulary.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is writing custom utilities for everything before checking standard Common Lisp. Another is depending on a utility library without distinguishing library functions from standard functions in documentation or teaching code.

### Arrays and numeric storage — `make-array`, `element-type`, specialized array, vector operations

Arrays matter for indexed data, numeric data, buffers, grids, and performance-sensitive storage.

Create a vector:

```lisp
(make-array 10 :initial-element 0)
```

Create a specialized numeric vector:

```lisp
(make-array 100
            :element-type 'double-float
            :initial-element 0.0d0)
```

Create a two-dimensional array:

```lisp
(make-array '(3 3) :initial-element 0)
```

Access:

```lisp
(aref array i)
(setf (aref array i) value)
```

Adjustable vector with fill pointer:

```lisp
(defparameter *items*
  (make-array 0 :adjustable t :fill-pointer 0))

(vector-push-extend "first" *items*)
```

| Task                       | API                             | Caveat                                                                |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Fixed-size indexed storage | `make-array`, `aref`            | Choose dimensions and element type.                                   |
| Growable vector buffer     | adjustable array + fill pointer | More complex than list accumulation.                                  |
| Numeric vector             | specialized array               | Implementation optimization depends on declarations and element type. |
| Multidimensional grid      | multidimensional array          | Access pattern and storage order matter.                              |
| Copy sequence              | `copy-seq`                      | Shallow element copy.                                                 |
| Replace region             | `replace`                       | Mutates target sequence.                                              |
| Generic element access     | `elt`                           | More generic, possibly less precise.                                  |

**Design meaning:** arrays and vectors are the right abstraction when indexed access or compact storage matters. Lists are not inherently more “Lispy” when the task is array-shaped.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is expecting `:element-type` alone to guarantee ideal performance. Implementations may optimize specialized arrays, but performance-sensitive numeric code also needs declarations, profiling, and attention to boxing/unboxing and compiler notes.

### Dates and time — `get-universal-time`, `decode-universal-time`, `local-time`

ANSI Common Lisp has universal time functions, but modern date/time work often benefits from ecosystem libraries.

Standard universal time:

```lisp
(get-universal-time)
```

Decode:

```lisp
(multiple-value-bind (second minute hour date month year day daylight-p zone)
    (decode-universal-time (get-universal-time))
  (list year month date hour minute second zone))
```

Encode:

```lisp
(encode-universal-time 0 30 9 12 5 2026)
```

| Task                         | Standard CL             | Ecosystem tendency           | Caveat                                      |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Current universal time       | `get-universal-time`    | standard enough              | Universal time is not a full date/time API. |
| Decode time                  | `decode-universal-time` | standard                     | Time zone handling needs care.              |
| Encode time                  | `encode-universal-time` | standard                     | Arguments are easy to mix up.               |
| Sleep                        | `sleep`                 | standard                     | Not scheduling infrastructure.              |
| Modern timestamps/time zones | limited                 | `local-time`-style libraries | Choose library deliberately.                |
| Duration/date arithmetic     | limited                 | external library             | Calendar arithmetic is subtle.              |

**Design meaning:** the standard time API is useful but basic. Serious time-zone, timestamp, parsing, formatting, and calendar logic often needs a library.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is hand-rolling date/time arithmetic. Time zones, daylight saving, leap behavior, and formatting conventions are more complex than they appear. Use a library for serious time work.

### Serialization and data formats — `S-expression`, JSON libraries, validation boundary

Common Lisp can read/write S-expressions naturally, but modern systems often need JSON, YAML, CSV, MessagePack, or other formats. These are ecosystem choices, not ANSI-standard features.

| Format/task                 | Standard CL?             | Typical approach                                                      | Caveat                      |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Lisp-readable internal data | Yes, with `read`/`write` | Trusted S-expressions                                                 | Not untrusted generic input |
| JSON                        | No                       | Libraries such as YASON, Jonathan, Jzon, cl-json depending on project | Representation choices vary |
| CSV                         | No                       | Library or custom parser                                              | Escaping rules matter       |
| YAML/TOML                   | No                       | Ecosystem library                                                     | Evaluate library maturity   |
| Binary formats              | No general standard      | library-specific                                                      | Endianness/type mapping     |
| Custom protocol             | No                       | parser/encoder functions                                              | Need validation and tests   |

Example internal S-expression config:

```lisp
'(:host "localhost" :port 8080 :debug nil)
```

Validation step:

```lisp
(defun parse-config (data)
  (let ((host (getf data :host))
        (port (getf data :port)))
    (unless (stringp host)
      (error 'invalid-config :reason "HOST must be a string."))
    (unless (typep port '(integer 1 65535))
      (error 'invalid-config :reason "PORT must be a valid port."))
    (make-config :host host :port port)))
```

**Design meaning:** serialization is a boundary. The external representation should be converted into internal domain objects rather than leaking raw JSON/plist/hash structures everywhere.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is choosing a JSON library and then letting its default representation become the application’s domain model. Convert at the boundary.

Another pitfall is treating S-expression config as safe for all users. It is convenient for trusted developer-controlled configuration; untrusted data needs strict reader controls or a separate parser.

### Testing — `FiveAM`, `Parachute`, `Rove`, `ASDF test-op`, assertions

Common Lisp does not standardize a unit-testing framework. Testing is therefore an ecosystem choice. Several libraries are used in practice, including `FiveAM`, `Parachute`, and `Rove`. The important point is not that one framework is universally correct, but that tests should be connected to the ASDF system workflow and should run from a clean image.

A common pattern is to define a separate test system:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:example/test
  :depends-on (#:example #:fiveam)
  :components
  ((:module "test"
    :serial t
    :components
    ((:file "packages")
     (:file "users-test"))))
  :perform (asdf:test-op (op c)
             (declare (ignore op c))
             (symbol-call '#:fiveam '#:run! '#:example-tests)))
```

A simple FiveAM-style test file may look like:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.test
  (:use #:cl #:fiveam)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:make-user
                #:user-name))

(in-package #:example.test)

(def-suite example-tests)
(in-suite example-tests)

(test make-user-test
  (let ((user (make-user :name "Ada")))
    (is (string= "Ada" (user-name user)))))
```

| Task                        | Common tool/pattern                              | Purpose                                         | Caveat                                           |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Unit testing                | FiveAM / Parachute / Rove                        | Test functions, data models, macros, conditions | Framework APIs differ.                           |
| System-level test operation | ASDF `test-op`                                   | Run tests through system workflow               | Must be configured explicitly.                   |
| Internal assertion          | `assert`                                         | Check internal invariants                       | Not a replacement for user-facing validation.    |
| Type/domain check           | `check-type`, predicates                         | Validate assumptions                            | Usually too narrow for full business validation. |
| Macro testing               | `macroexpand-1` + behavioral tests               | Check expansion and runtime behavior            | Expansion tests can become brittle if too exact. |
| Condition testing           | framework condition assertions or `handler-case` | Verify error protocol                           | Do not only test error strings.                  |
| Clean-image testing         | restart implementation, load system, run tests   | Detect hidden REPL state dependencies           | Slower but more reliable.                        |

Testing conditions should usually check condition type and slots, not printed messages.

```lisp
(handler-case
    (parse-port 99999)
  (invalid-port (condition)
    (is (= 99999 (invalid-port-value condition)))))
```

Macro tests should usually test behavior and only selectively test expansion shape.

```lisp
(test when-let-test
  (is (= 10
         (when-let (x 10)
           x))))
```

Expansion inspection is still useful:

```lisp
(macroexpand-1
 '(when-let (x (find-user id))
    (notify x)))
```

**Design meaning:** because Common Lisp is dynamic and macro-capable, tests are not merely regression tools. They are part of the discipline that compensates for fewer static guarantees and for powerful syntactic abstraction.

**Failure-first explanation: REPL success is not test success**

The tempting model is: “I evaluated the function in the REPL and it worked.”

The surprising failure appears when a clean image cannot load the system, a package was only created interactively, a macro was redefined without recompiling dependents, or test-only dependencies were already present by accident.

The correct explanation is that the live image contains state not necessarily represented in source files and ASDF system definitions.

The professional rule of thumb is: **tests should run from source through ASDF, not only from the current REPL state.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is testing only final values and not testing error contracts. In Common Lisp, conditions, restarts, multiple values, and mutation policies are part of API behavior. Another pitfall is writing tests that depend on hash-table iteration order or printed object representation when those are not contractual.

### Debugging — `debugger`, `break`, `trace`, `step`, `inspect`, compiler notes

Common Lisp debugging is shaped by interactive development. The debugger is not only a crash display; it is part of the condition system and often exposes restarts. A serious workflow uses the debugger, inspector, tracing, compiler notes, and macro expansion together.

| Task                         | Tool/API                                  | Use                                    | Caveat                                                  |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Enter debugger intentionally | `break`                                   | Stop at a point and inspect            | Do not leave in production paths.                       |
| Signal error                 | `error`                                   | Enter debugger if unhandled            | Use structured conditions for APIs.                     |
| Trace function calls         | `trace`, `untrace`                        | Observe arguments/results              | Can produce noise and affect timing.                    |
| Step evaluation              | implementation/editor support             | Walk through execution                 | Support varies.                                         |
| Inspect objects              | inspector via SLIME/SLY or implementation | Examine structures/classes/hash tables | Live object state may differ from source assumptions.   |
| Inspect macro expansion      | `macroexpand-1`, editor macroexpand       | Debug macros                           | Expansion may be implementation-expanded further later. |
| Read compiler notes          | SBCL compiler output                      | Performance/type diagnostics           | Implementation-specific.                                |
| Disassemble                  | `disassemble`                             | Low-level performance investigation    | Expert-level; do not start here.                        |

Basic trace:

```lisp
(trace parse-config)
(parse-config data)
(untrace parse-config)
```

Intentional break:

```lisp
(defun suspicious-function (x)
  (break "Inspecting X: ~S" x)
  (process x))
```

Macro debugging:

```lisp
(macroexpand-1
 '(with-open-file (stream path)
    (read-line stream)))
```

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp debugging is interactive and semantic. One can inspect values, invoke restarts, redefine functions, and continue. This is powerful, but it can also hide the difference between source-level correction and live-image patching.

**Failure-first explanation: fixing the image is not fixing the program**

The tempting model is: “I redefined the function in the REPL, so the bug is fixed.”

The surprising failure is that restarting the image and loading from source brings the bug back, because the source file was not updated or dependent code was not recompiled.

The correct explanation is that the image is the running environment; the source and ASDF systems are the durable program.

The professional rule of thumb is: **use the REPL to diagnose and experiment; commit the fix to source and verify with a clean load/test.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is tracing too much and drowning in output. Another is ignoring compiler warnings or SBCL notes. Compiler output often reveals type uncertainty, unreachable code, undefined functions, package mistakes, or performance problems.

### Logging and observability — `format`, streams, conditions, logging libraries

Common Lisp does not standardize a full logging framework. Basic logging can be done with streams and `format`, but serious applications usually use a library or define a thin logging layer.

Simple logging function:

```lisp
(defparameter *log-stream* *error-output*)

(defun log-message (level control-string &rest args)
  (format *log-stream* "~&[~A] " level)
  (apply #'format *log-stream* control-string args)
  (terpri *log-stream*))
```

Usage:

```lisp
(log-message :info "Loaded ~D users." count)
```

A slightly more disciplined version uses dynamic binding:

```lisp
(let ((*log-stream* stream))
  (run-task))
```

| Task                     | Basic CL mechanism       | Ecosystem tendency          | Caveat                                 |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------ | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Print simple diagnostic  | `format *error-output*`  | enough for scripts/tools    | Not structured.                        |
| Build log string         | `format nil`             | useful for adapters         | Avoid repeated concatenation loops.    |
| Dynamic log destination  | special stream variable  | common CL style             | Hidden dependency if undocumented.     |
| Structured logging       | library or project layer | application-level need      | Choose format and fields deliberately. |
| Warning as observability | `warn` / condition       | nonfatal abnormal situation | Better than manual warning text.       |
| Error event              | condition object         | structured diagnostic       | Do not only log and continue blindly.  |

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp already has streams and conditions, so logging design should integrate with them. Warnings and conditions can be more structured than raw text logs.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is using logging as error handling. Logging an error and returning `nil` may hide failure from the caller. Another is writing directly to `*standard-output*` in library code. Prefer a stream parameter, a dynamic variable, or a logging abstraction.

### Configuration — `special variables`, files, environment variables, command-line arguments

Configuration enters a Common Lisp program through several channels: special variables, files, environment variables, command-line arguments, ASDF/system configuration, and application-specific data formats.

| Configuration source             | Mechanism                            | Use when                      | Caveat                                  |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Dynamic setting                  | special variable                     | temporary scoped behavior     | Hidden dependency if overused           |
| Developer-controlled config file | S-expression, JSON, TOML, etc.       | persistent settings           | Parse and validate                      |
| Environment variable             | UIOP or implementation API           | deployment configuration      | Values are strings                      |
| CLI argument                     | implementation/UIOP/library          | command-line tools            | Parsing required                        |
| ASDF system setting              | system definition                    | build-time/system composition | Not runtime app config                  |
| Defaults in code                 | keyword defaults, constants          | stable defaults               | Do not make deployment values hardcoded |
| Runtime override                 | dynamic binding / explicit parameter | tests, request context        | Must be documented                      |

Example: environment variable boundary using UIOP:

```lisp
(defun getenv-required (name)
  (or (uiop:getenv name)
      (error "Missing environment variable: ~A" name)))
```

Parsing:

```lisp
(defun getenv-port (name)
  (let ((raw (getenv-required name)))
    (parse-integer raw :junk-allowed nil)))
```

Better with validation:

```lisp
(defun getenv-port (name)
  (let ((port (parse-integer (getenv-required name)
                             :junk-allowed nil)))
    (unless (typep port '(integer 1 65535))
      (error "Invalid port in environment variable ~A: ~S" name port))
    port))
```

**Design meaning:** configuration is external input. It should be converted into internal domain objects early.

**Failure-first explanation: environment variables are not typed**

The tempting model is: “The environment variable `PORT` is a port.”

The correct explanation is that environment variables are strings. They become domain values only after parsing and validation.

The professional rule of thumb is: **parse configuration at the boundary; do not pass raw strings through the program.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is reading environment variables deep inside domain logic. That hides dependencies and makes tests harder. Prefer reading configuration at startup or boundary code, then passing a validated configuration object.

### Command-line interfaces — `uiop:command-line-arguments`, option libraries, scripts

Common Lisp can be used for command-line tools, but CLI support is partly implementation- and ecosystem-mediated. UIOP provides basic access to command-line arguments. More sophisticated option parsing usually uses a library.

Basic argument access:

```lisp
(defun main ()
  (let ((args (uiop:command-line-arguments)))
    (format t "Arguments: ~S~%" args)))
```

Argument parsing should convert strings into validated domain values.

```lisp
(defun parse-limit-argument (arg)
  (let ((n (parse-integer arg :junk-allowed nil)))
    (unless (typep n '(integer 1 *))
      (error "Limit must be a positive integer: ~S" arg))
    n))
```

| Task                         | Basic option                          | Ecosystem option            | Caveat                                    |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Read raw args                | `uiop:command-line-arguments`         | implementation APIs         | Raw strings only.                         |
| Parse simple positional args | custom parser                         | enough for small tools      | Validate all values.                      |
| Parse options/flags          | library                               | unix-style CLI tools        | Library APIs differ.                      |
| Print usage                  | custom or library                     | CLI library support         | Keep errors clear.                        |
| Exit status                  | UIOP/implementation                   | depends on delivery/runtime | Isolate implementation details if needed. |
| Build executable/script      | implementation/Roswell-style workflow | ecosystem/deployment layer  | More Part 9 than core library.            |

**Design meaning:** CLI code is boundary code. It should be thin: parse strings, validate, construct configuration/domain objects, call core functions.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is letting command-line parsing shape the whole application architecture. Keep CLI-specific names and formats at the edge.

### Subprocess and OS interaction — `UIOP`, implementation APIs, portability

ANSI Common Lisp does not standardize a full modern OS API. Practical code often uses UIOP or implementation-specific functions for subprocesses, environment variables, filesystem operations, and image utilities.

Example conceptual UIOP subprocess use:

```lisp
(uiop:run-program '("echo" "hello")
                  :output :string)
```

Running external programs requires explicit handling of output, error output, exit status, environment, and shell interpretation.

| Task                     | Practical mechanism                      | Boundary concern             |
| ------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Run program              | `uiop:run-program` or implementation API | command quoting, exit status |
| Get environment variable | `uiop:getenv`                            | missing/malformed values     |
| Current directory        | UIOP/pathname utilities                  | relative path fragility      |
| Temporary files          | UIOP/library/implementation              | cleanup                      |
| Exit program             | UIOP/implementation                      | delivery/runtime behavior    |
| OS-specific feature      | reader conditionals/wrapper              | portability                  |

**Design meaning:** OS interaction is not core portable Lisp in the same way that list processing or CLOS is. Wrap it.

**Failure-first explanation: subprocess strings can create shell ambiguity**

The tempting model is to build one command string. This risks quoting and shell interpretation problems.

Prefer passing a list of program and arguments where the API supports it:

```lisp
(uiop:run-program (list "echo" "hello")
                  :output :string)
```

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is ignoring subprocess exit status. Another is mixing shell-specific syntax into code intended to be portable across systems.

### Networking and HTTP — ecosystem libraries, streams, JSON boundary

Networking is not a major ANSI Common Lisp standard-library area. Practical networking, HTTP clients, servers, WebSocket support, and TLS rely on ecosystem libraries and implementation support.

| Task             | Standard CL?         | Ecosystem approach                                                  | Caveat                             |
| ---------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Raw sockets      | No portable ANSI API | implementation/library                                              | Portability boundary               |
| HTTP client      | No                   | libraries such as Dexador/Drakma-style choices depending on project | Check maintenance and TLS behavior |
| HTTP server      | No                   | Hunchentoot/Lack-style ecosystem choices                            | Framework conventions vary         |
| JSON payloads    | No                   | JSON library                                                        | Validate boundary data             |
| TLS              | No                   | library/implementation/OpenSSL bindings                             | Deployment-sensitive               |
| Async networking | No single standard   | implementation/library model                                        | Concurrency model matters          |

**Design rule:** keep networking code at the boundary. Convert HTTP/JSON/socket data into internal structures or classes before business logic sees it.

Example conceptual shape:

```lisp
(defun fetch-user-json (id)
  ;; HTTP library call would live here.
  ;; Return parsed external representation.
  ...)

(defun fetch-user (id)
  (parse-user (fetch-user-json id)))
```

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is allowing HTTP response hash tables or JSON library data shapes to become internal domain objects. Another is ignoring timeout and retry policy. External calls fail; model that failure with conditions or explicit result protocols.

### Concurrency utilities — implementation threads, `Bordeaux-Threads`, queues, locks

ANSI Common Lisp does not define one portable threading model. Implementations such as SBCL provide threads, and portability libraries such as `Bordeaux-Threads` are commonly used to write more portable threaded code.

| Task                         | Common practical mechanism             | Caveat                                                           |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Create thread                | implementation API or Bordeaux-Threads | Semantics and support vary.                                      |
| Lock critical section        | mutex + `with-lock-held` style macro   | Avoid manual lock/unlock.                                        |
| Coordinate work              | queues/channels from libraries         | Choose abstraction deliberately.                                 |
| Protect hash table/state     | locks or ownership model               | Standard hash tables are not magically safe for shared mutation. |
| Dynamic variables in threads | implementation-specific details        | Understand binding behavior.                                     |
| Parallel CPU work            | implementation/library                 | Performance and safety need measurement.                         |
| Async/event loop             | library/framework-specific             | Not a standard CL core model.                                    |

Conceptual lock pattern:

```lisp
(with-lock-held (*cache-lock*)
  (setf (gethash key *cache*) value))
```

The exact macro and lock type depend on the threading library.

**Design meaning:** Common Lisp concurrency is practical but not standardized in a single ANSI abstraction. Treat it as an implementation/ecosystem layer and design shared state boundaries explicitly.

**Failure-first explanation: dynamic language does not mean dynamically safe shared state**

The tempting model is: “The runtime manages memory, so shared objects are safe.”

The correct explanation is that garbage collection prevents many memory-lifetime errors but does not prevent logical races, lost updates, inconsistent reads, or deadlocks.

The professional rule of thumb is: **use ownership, locks, queues, or immutable-by-convention data when sharing across threads.**

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is mutating a shared hash table from multiple threads without a policy. Another is holding locks while calling arbitrary generic functions, which may invoke user-defined methods and create deadlock or reentrancy problems.

### Functional utilities — `map`, `reduce`, predicates, `Alexandria`, composition

Functional style is well-supported by Common Lisp, but the standard library’s functional utility set is not identical to Scheme, Clojure, Haskell, or ML.

Standard functional tools include:

| Task                 | Standard construct | Example                                   |
| -------------------- | ------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
| Anonymous function   | `lambda`           | `(lambda (x) (* x x))`                    |
| Pass named function  | `#'name`           | `#'string-downcase`                       |
| Call function object | `funcall`          | `(funcall f x)`                           |
| Apply list arguments | `apply`            | `(apply #'+ xs)`                          |
| Map list             | `mapcar`           | `(mapcar #'1+ xs)`                        |
| Map sequence         | `map`              | `(map 'vector #'1+ xs)`                   |
| Reduce               | `reduce`           | `(reduce #'+ xs)`                         |
| Predicate all/some   | `every`, `some`    | `(every #'valid-p xs)`                    |
| Remove/filter        | `remove-if-not`    | `(remove-if-not #'active-p xs)`           |
| Sort with key        | `sort` with `:key` | `(sort users #'string< :key #'user-name)` |

Alexandria and similar utility libraries may provide additional functional conveniences, but standard Common Lisp should be learned first.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is importing point-free or pipeline-heavy expectations from other languages. Common Lisp supports higher-order programming well, but idiomatic code often uses explicit `let`, local functions, and clear sequence operations rather than maximizing abstraction density.

### Package and dependency utility ecosystem — `Alexandria`, `Bordeaux-Threads`, `UIOP`, `trivial-*`

The Common Lisp ecosystem has a pattern of small portability or utility libraries. Names vary, but many libraries exist to smooth differences across implementations or provide missing common utilities.

| Library category                | Example style                             | Role                                          | Caveat                                           |
| ------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| General utilities               | Alexandria                                | Common utility functions/macros               | Not standard CL; document dependency.            |
| Broader utility layer           | Serapeum                                  | More opinionated utilities                    | Can shape project style strongly.                |
| Thread portability              | Bordeaux-Threads                          | Cross-implementation thread API               | Does not erase all concurrency differences.      |
| Implementation feature wrappers | `trivial-*` libraries                     | Small portability shims                       | Quality/scope varies by library.                 |
| System utilities                | UIOP                                      | Paths, environment, subprocess, image support | Comes with ASDF, but still a specific API layer. |
| Regex                           | CL-PPCRE                                  | Pattern matching                              | Not a parser replacement.                        |
| JSON                            | YASON/Jzon/Jonathan/cl-json style choices | Serialization boundary                        | Representation conventions differ.               |
| HTTP                            | Drakma/Dexador/Hunchentoot/Lack ecosystem | Client/server                                 | Maintenance and TLS/deployment matter.           |

**Design meaning:** many Common Lisp ecosystem libraries are small because they wrap one portability or utility concern. This is different from ecosystems dominated by large frameworks.

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is depending on many tiny libraries without tracking maintenance or portability. Another is rewriting well-known portability utilities by hand. The right decision depends on project longevity, deployment constraints, and dependency tolerance.

### Task-to-tool decision table — standard vs ecosystem

| Task                | Start with standard CL                     | Add ecosystem when                        | Common mistake                        |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Lists/sequences     | `map`, `reduce`, `remove`, `find`, `loop`  | Utility convenience needed                | Writing custom traversal too early    |
| Hash maps           | `make-hash-table`, `gethash`               | Ordered/persistent/special maps needed    | Wrong equality test                   |
| Text basics         | string functions, `search`, `position`     | Regex or parsing needed                   | Regex for simple substring            |
| Regex               | none standard                              | Pattern language needed                   | Regex for nested grammar              |
| Files               | pathnames, streams                         | Practical portability/path helpers needed | String path concatenation             |
| Directories/OS      | limited standard                           | UIOP/implementation APIs                  | Assuming ANSI covers OS fully         |
| JSON/config formats | no standard JSON                           | External data format required             | Letting raw parsed shape leak inward  |
| Testing             | no standard framework                      | Any serious project                       | REPL-only testing                     |
| Logging             | streams/format/warn                        | Structured application logging needed     | Logging instead of error handling     |
| Threads             | no ANSI standard model                     | Concurrency needed                        | Shared mutation without policy        |
| HTTP/network        | no ANSI standard model                     | External service/server needed            | No timeout/error model                |
| CLI                 | UIOP raw args/basic implementation support | Real flags/options needed                 | Domain logic depending on raw strings |
| Deployment          | implementation-specific                    | Executable/image/container needed         | Treating REPL workflow as deployment  |

### Standard library cost notes — `allocation`, `destructive operation`, `sequence result`, `format`

Part 7 will give a deeper cost model, but Part 6 needs practical library-level warnings.

| Operation/pattern       | Usual cost                       | Hidden cost                       | When it matters                |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| `mapcar`                | Allocates result list            | Wasteful if result ignored        | Large traversals, side effects |
| `remove-if-not`         | Allocates filtered sequence/list | Non-destructive copy              | Large sequences                |
| `delete-if`             | May destructively modify         | Caller-owned data may change      | Shared data                    |
| `append` in loop        | Repeated traversal               | Can become quadratic              | Building large lists           |
| `push` + `nreverse`     | Efficient list accumulation      | Destructive reverse of owned list | Good local pattern             |
| `format nil`            | Allocates string                 | Format parsing/work               | Hot loops                      |
| `with-output-to-string` | Allocates growing string         | Large output memory               | Reports, serialization         |
| Hash table lookup       | Expected efficient               | Test function cost                | Hot lookup paths               |
| `equalp` hash test      | More permissive comparison       | Potentially more expensive        | Large keys/tables              |
| `sort`                  | Destructive                      | Input order changed               | Caller expects original order  |
| `copy-seq`              | Shallow copy                     | Elements shared                   | Nested mutable data            |
| `read`                  | Reader work and trust boundary   | Interning/reader behavior         | Untrusted input                |

**Common Pitfalls**

A common pitfall is optimizing away clarity before measuring. Another is ignoring obvious allocation patterns such as `mapcar` for effects or repeated `append`. Learn the standard cost traps first; profile before rewriting architecture.

### Part 6 quick-reference artifact — task-oriented library map

| Practical task        | First place to look                                  | Common ecosystem addition        | Key caveat                             |
| --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Define/load project   | ASDF                                                 | Quicklisp for dependencies       | Systems are not packages.              |
| Use dependency        | Quicklisp + package imports                          | local-projects/pinning workflow  | Loading does not import symbols.       |
| Work with paths       | pathnames, UIOP                                      | implementation helpers if needed | Avoid string concatenation.            |
| Read/write text files | streams, `with-open-file`, `read-line`, `write-line` | encoding/path helpers            | Scope stream lifetime.                 |
| Build text            | `format`, `with-output-to-string`                    | templating library if needed     | Complex `format` can become opaque.    |
| Search text           | `search`, `position`                                 | CL-PPCRE for regex               | Regex is not full parsing.             |
