| Application              | Config boundary, startup function, logging, deployment plan | Integration tests, dependency pinning, monitoring   |
| Framework                | Macro/CLOS protocol documentation                           | Extension tests, method-combination discipline      |
| Numeric/performance code | Tests, representation choices                               | Compiler notes, declarations, profiling             |
| FFI/system code          | Wrapper package, cleanup protocol                           | Portability and security review                     |
| Concurrent service       | State ownership, locks/queues, lifecycle                    | Stress tests, logging, restart policy               |

### Part 9 practical artifact — `professional Common Lisp checklist`

| Question                                      | Required answer                                         |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Can the system load from a fresh image?       | Yes, through ASDF.                                      |
| Are dependencies declared?                    | Yes, in `.asd`; Quicklisp loading alone is not enough.  |
| Are packages defined before use?              | Yes, predictably.                                       |
| Are exported symbols intentional?             | Yes, each is API.                                       |
| Are internal symbols hidden?                  | Yes, no normal external `::` use.                       |
| Are public failures structured?               | Yes, with condition classes when callers need handling. |
| Are resources scoped?                         | Yes, through `with-*` or `unwind-protect`.              |
| Is untrusted input parsed safely?             | Yes, no casual `read`/`eval`.                           |
| Are macros justified?                         | Yes, only for syntax/evaluation/binding needs.          |
| Are generic functions documented?             | Yes, as protocols.                                      |
| Is mutation policy clear?                     | Yes, ownership/destructive behavior documented.         |
| Are tests clean-image compatible?             | Yes, through ASDF test system.                          |
| Are compiler warnings/notes reviewed?         | Yes, especially in SBCL-focused code.                   |
| Is implementation-specific behavior isolated? | Yes, behind wrappers or feature branches.               |
| Is deployment explicit?                       | Yes, with startup function and dependency policy.       |

## PART 10 — Expert Integration, Diagnostic Judgment, and Final Reference Artifacts

### What mastery means in Common Lisp — `semantic judgment`, `representation judgment`, `phase judgment`

Common Lisp mastery is not mainly the ability to remember many functions. It is the ability to decide which semantic layer is active, which representation fits the domain, which abstraction mechanism is justified, which boundary must be explicit, and which implementation assumptions are being made.

A competent Common Lisp programmer can write functions. A professional Common Lisp programmer can answer these questions while writing them:

| Judgment area  | Core question                                                                                    |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Evaluation     | Is this a function call, special form, macro form, or generated expansion?                       |
| Phase          | Does this happen at read time, macro expansion time, compile time, load time, or runtime?        |
| Representation | Should this be a list, vector, hash table, structure, class, condition, closure, or symbol?      |
| Equality       | Is the program asking about identity, numeric equality, string equality, or structural equality? |
| Mutation       | Who owns this object, and is destructive update safe?                                            |
| API boundary   | Is this symbol public, internal, implementation-specific, or accidental?                         |
| Failure        | Should this return `nil`, return multiple values, signal a condition, or offer restarts?         |
| Resource       | What guarantees cleanup on normal and nonlocal exits?                                            |
| Performance    | Is cost coming from consing, generic dispatch, arithmetic, allocation, copying, or I/O?          |
| Portability    | Is this ANSI Common Lisp, SBCL-specific behavior, ASDF/UIOP workflow, or library convention?     |

**Central conclusion:** Common Lisp gives unusually high expressive control. The price is that the programmer must supply more semantic discipline than languages that force one module system, one object model, one error model, one static type discipline, or one deployment workflow.

### The complete mental model — `reader`, `evaluator`, `compiler`, `image`, `system`

A professional mental model of Common Lisp connects the whole stack:

```text
source text
  -> reader
  -> Lisp objects
  -> macro expansion
  -> compilation / evaluation
  -> loading into image
  -> runtime execution
  -> debugger / restarts / redefinition
  -> source correction and ASDF reload
```

Each layer has its own failure modes.

| Layer           | Main objects                 | Main mistakes                         | Diagnostic question                         |
| --------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Source text     | characters, tokens           | case/package confusion                | How is this text read?                      |
| Reader          | symbols, lists, literals     | wrong package, unsafe `read`          | Which objects does the reader produce?      |
| Macro expansion | forms, generated code        | double evaluation, capture            | What does this expand into?                 |
| Compilation     | declarations, compiler notes | false type assumptions                | What does the compiler know or warn about?  |
| Loading         | top-level forms, definitions | load-order and side effects           | Does ASDF encode this dependency?           |
| Image           | live functions/classes/state | stale definitions                     | Does this work from a fresh image?          |
| Runtime         | objects, calls, conditions   | wrong equality/mutation/failure model | What actually happens with these values?    |
| Debugger        | conditions, restarts, frames | manual repair not saved               | What source change corresponds to this fix? |

**Professional habit:** when a bug appears, do not immediately patch the visible symptom. First classify the layer where the bug lives.

### From beginner syntax to expert judgment — `same code, deeper reading`

A beginner sees this:

```lisp
(when-let (user (find-user id))
  (notify user))
```

A professional reads several layers at once:

| Layer             | Expert reading                                                                                                               |
| ----------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Surface syntax    | This is not standard ANSI syntax unless `when-let` is defined or imported.                                                   |
| Package layer     | Which package provides `when-let`, `find-user`, and `notify`?                                                                |
| Macro layer       | `when-let` probably evaluates `(find-user id)` once, binds `user`, and evaluates the body if non-`nil`. Verify if necessary. |
| Failure layer     | If `find-user` returns `nil`, no notification occurs. Is `nil` an unambiguous absence marker?                                |
| API layer         | Does `notify` signal conditions, return status, mutate state, or perform I/O?                                                |
| Test layer        | There should be tests for found and not-found cases.                                                                         |
| Performance layer | Likely irrelevant unless this is in a hot path.                                                                              |
| Dependency layer  | If `when-let` comes from Alexandria or another utility library, the ASDF dependency must be declared.                        |

The code is small, but Common Lisp encourages layered reading. This is why professional fluency is less about parentheses and more about semantic classification.

### Diagnostic workflow — `bug classification`, `macroexpand`, `trace`, `inspect`, `fresh image`

A useful diagnostic workflow:

```text
1. Identify the failure category.
2. Reproduce the failure from source, not only from REPL state.
3. Check packages and symbol identity.
4. Macroexpand if macros are involved.
5. Trace or inspect runtime values.
6. Check multiple values and equality predicates.
7. Check mutation/aliasing.
8. Check condition types and handlers.
9. Check ASDF dependencies and load order.
10. Verify in a fresh image.
```

| Symptom                                  | Likely cause                                         | First diagnostic move                         |
| ---------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Function undefined in fresh image        | Missing ASDF dependency or source definition         | Load system from clean image                  |
| Symbol exists but wrong behavior         | Package/symbol mismatch                              | Inspect symbol package                        |
| Macro works strangely                    | Expansion issue                                      | `macroexpand-1`                               |
| Value is unexpectedly `nil`              | Ambiguous absence/failure                            | Check multiple values or condition path       |
| String comparison fails                  | Wrong equality predicate                             | Use `string=` / `equal` as appropriate        |
| List mutation appears elsewhere          | Shared cons structure                                | Check aliases and destructive operations      |
| Hash lookup misses                       | Wrong hash-table test                                | Inspect `:test`                               |
| Code works in REPL but not after restart | Image/source drift                                   | Fresh-image load                              |
| Numeric code slow                        | Generic arithmetic or allocation                     | Read compiler notes/profile                   |
| Resource leak                            | Missing cleanup on nonlocal exit                     | Look for `with-*` / `unwind-protect`          |
| Handler does not recover                 | Handler did not invoke restart or wrong handler form | Distinguish `handler-case` and `handler-bind` |

**Professional rule:** Common Lisp bugs often look local but live in a different layer: package, phase, image state, expansion, equality, or boundary design.

### Reading unknown Common Lisp code — `top-down`, `package-first`, `macro-aware`

When reading a Common Lisp codebase, begin with structure before details.

Recommended order:

```text
1. Read the ASDF system definition.
2. Read package definitions.
3. Identify exported symbols.
4. Identify major data representations.
5. Identify public generic functions and macros.
6. Identify condition types.
7. Inspect tests.
8. Read implementation files in ASDF order.
9. Macroexpand unfamiliar macros.
10. Load in a fresh image and explore interactively.
```

| File or construct         | What to extract                                    |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| `.asd`                    | System name, dependencies, components, test system |
| `defpackage`              | Public API, imports, internal packages             |
| `defstruct` / `defclass`  | Core data model                                    |
| `defgeneric`              | Protocols and extension points                     |
| `defmethod`               | Runtime dispatch behavior                          |
| `defmacro`                | Source-level language extensions                   |
| `define-condition`        | Failure model                                      |
| `defparameter` / `defvar` | Dynamic/global context                             |
| Tests                     | Intended behavior and edge cases                   |
| README/manual             | Human-level contract                               |

**Common Pitfall**

A common mistake is reading implementation files first and ignoring package/system definitions. In Common Lisp, names and loading are too important to skip.

### Writing new Common Lisp code — `start simple`, `strengthen boundaries`, `abstract last`

A disciplined writing process:

```text
1. Start with clear functions and simple data.
2. Add constructors and predicates when invariants appear.
3. Choose structures/classes when representation stabilizes.
4. Add package exports only for stable API.
5. Use conditions when failures need structure.
6. Use macros only when syntax/evaluation/binding requires them.
7. Use generic functions when open extension is needed.
8. Add declarations after correctness and representation are clear.
9. Add ASDF dependencies immediately when libraries are used.
10. Run tests from a fresh image.
```

| Design pressure                  | First response                     | Escalate to                                    |
| -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Repeated value-level computation | function                           | higher-order function                          |
| Repeated local helper            | `flet` / `labels`                  | package-level internal function                |
| Stable record                    | `defstruct`                        | `defclass` if protocol extension needed        |
| Open polymorphic operation       | ordinary function initially        | `defgeneric` + `defmethod`                     |
| Repeated syntax pattern          | function if possible               | macro if binding/evaluation changes            |
| Expected absence                 | `nil` or multiple values           | condition if diagnostic failure                |
| External input                   | parser + validation                | structured condition                           |
| Resource lifetime                | `with-*` macro                     | custom `unwind-protect` abstraction            |
| Performance issue                | profile and inspect representation | declarations/specialized arrays/compiler notes |

**Professional rule:** do not begin with the most powerful construct. Begin with the clearest construct that preserves the semantic contract.

### The five final language-specific indexes

The final language-specific indexes should be high-value diagnostic artifacts rather than encyclopedic lists. They are designed for reviewing, debugging, and designing Common Lisp code.

## Index 1 — Macro Expansion and Evaluation Phase Index

| Question                                          | Check                                                   | Corrective action                |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Is this an ordinary function call?                | Operator names a function, not a macro/special operator | Evaluate arguments normally      |
| Is this a special operator?                       | `if`, `let`, `quote`, `block`, etc.                     | Apply special evaluation rule    |
| Is this a macro?                                  | `macro-function`, documentation, `macroexpand-1`        | Inspect expansion                |
| Does the macro evaluate a subform more than once? | Macro expansion duplicates subform                      | Bind to gensym temporary         |
| Does the macro introduce user-visible bindings?   | Syntax contains names like `(var expr)`                 | Document scope and evaluation    |
| Does the macro create internal bindings?          | Expansion uses temporary variables                      | Use `gensym`                     |
| Does generated code depend on caller package?     | Expansion contains unqualified symbols                  | Qualify or control symbol origin |
| Does macro logic use runtime values?              | Macro body branches on source variables                 | Move branch into generated code  |
| Did a macro change recently?                      | Old compiled code may contain old expansion             | Recompile dependent files        |
| Is the macro actually needed?                     | Arguments all evaluated normally                        | Use function instead             |

**Diagnostic commands:**

```lisp
(macroexpand-1 form)
(macroexpand form)
```

**Review rule:** a macro must justify its phase complexity by controlling syntax, binding, evaluation, resource scope, or definition generation.

## Index 2 — Symbol, Package, and Reader Index

| Question                           | Check                                          | Corrective action                                  |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Which package is current?          | `*package*`                                    | Use `in-package` deliberately                      |
| Which symbol is being read?        | `symbol-package`, `symbol-name`                | Confirm package identity                           |
| Is the symbol exported?            | `find-symbol`, package exports                 | Use single colon only for external symbols         |
| Is code using `pkg::x`?            | Double colon in source                         | Replace with public API or isolate dependency      |
| Is a keyword appropriate?          | `:name` self-evaluates                         | Use for options/tags, not arbitrary text           |
| Is user input being interned?      | `intern` on external string                    | Keep as string unless symbol identity needed       |
| Does case matter?                  | Reader uppercases unescaped symbols by default | Use strings or escaped symbols only when justified |
| Is `read` used on untrusted input? | `read`, `read-from-string`                     | Use parser or restricted validated reader          |
| Is `*readtable*` changed globally? | `setf *readtable*`                             | Use dynamic binding                                |
| Does loading import names?         | ASDF/Quicklisp load only                       | Use package-qualified names/imports                |

**Diagnostic commands:**

```lisp
(symbol-package 'foo)
(symbol-name 'foo)
(find-symbol "FOO" "PACKAGE-NAME")
```

**Review rule:** packages control symbols; ASDF controls systems; neither replaces the other.

## Index 3 — Equality, Identity, and Mutation Index

| Need                                                | Prefer                                   | Avoid                   |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Symbol identity                                     | `eq`                                     | String comparison       |
| Default object/numeric/character equality           | `eql`                                    | `eq` for numbers/chars  |
| List/tree/string structural equality                | `equal`                                  | `eq`                    |
| Permissive structural/case-insensitive-ish equality | `equalp`                                 | When strictness matters |
| Numeric equality                                    | `=`                                      | `equal` as numeric API  |
| String exact equality                               | `string=`                                | `eq`                    |
| Character exact equality                            | `char=`                                  | String functions        |
| Hash table with symbol keys                         | `:test 'eq` or `eql` depending semantics | `equalp` by habit       |
| Hash table with string keys                         | `:test 'equal` or suitable test          | `eq`                    |
| Detect presence with possible `nil` value           | second value from `gethash`              | single-value lookup     |

Mutation review:

| Operation                   | Safe when                                   |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `setf` on accessor          | Mutation is public or internal-owned        |
| `push` on local accumulator | Local binding owns list                     |
| `nreverse`                  | List is freshly built or owned              |
| `nconc`                     | Input lists are owned                       |
| `delete` / `sort`           | Caller permits destructive modification     |
| `copy-list`                 | Only top-level list structure needs copying |
| `copy-tree`                 | Cons tree needs copying                     |
| Structure copier            | Shallow copy is enough                      |

**Review rule:** before destructive update, identify ownership, aliasing, and whether the object may be literal/shared.

## Index 4 — CLOS and Generic Dispatch Index

| Question                                        | Check                    | Corrective action                                                     |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Is polymorphism open or closed?                 | New types expected?      | Use generic function for open behavior, `ecase`/`typecase` for closed |
| Is the generic function contract documented?    | `defgeneric` docstring   | Define return values, side effects, conditions                        |
| Which arguments dispatch?                       | Method specializers      | Avoid accidental receiver-only thinking                               |
| Are methods too scattered?                      | Search `defmethod` forms | Clarify protocol/package organization                                 |
| Are `:before`/`:after` methods hiding behavior? | Method combination       | Document lifecycle effects                                            |
| Does `:around` call `call-next-method`?         | Inspect method           | Make suppression intentional                                          |
| Is class used only as a record?                 | No generic protocol      | Consider `defstruct`                                                  |
| Is `typecase` repeated across code?             | Same branches recur      | Consider generic function                                             |
| Is dispatch in a hot loop?                      | Profiling/compiler notes | Consider ordinary function or specialization                          |
| Are slot accessors public?                      | Export list              | Export only stable accessors                                          |

**Review rule:** CLOS is for protocols and extensibility, not merely for making code look object-oriented.

## Index 5 — Condition, Restart, and Resource Boundary Index

| Situation                         | Mechanism                  | Review question                          |
| --------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Simple absence                    | `nil`                      | Can `nil` be a valid value?              |
| Absence with possible `nil` value | multiple values            | Is caller using secondary value?         |
| Public invalid input              | condition class            | Are slots inspectable?                   |
| Internal invariant failure        | `assert` or `error`        | Is this really programmer error?         |
| Nonfatal abnormal situation       | `warn` / warning condition | Can caller muffle or handle it?          |
| Context-dependent recovery        | restart                    | Are recovery choices meaningful?         |
| Simple exit-style handling        | `handler-case`             | Is condition type specific enough?       |
| Dynamic recovery participation    | `handler-bind`             | Does handler invoke restart when needed? |
| Resource cleanup                  | `unwind-protect`           | Does cleanup run on nonlocal exit?       |
| File scope                        | `with-open-file`           | Is stream used only inside body?         |
| Lock/transaction scope            | `with-*` macro             | Is release/rollback guaranteed?          |
| Foreign resource                  | wrapper + cleanup protocol | Who owns release responsibility?         |

**Review rule:** failures and resources are boundary problems. They should not be hidden as bare `nil`, printed strings, or unprotected open/close pairs.

### Separate artifact — Consing and Performance Checklist

This is useful enough to keep separate from the five formal indexes.

| Performance question                                  | Check                         | Typical fix                                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Is a list built by repeated `append`?                 | Accumulation loop             | Use `push` + `nreverse` or `loop collect`                        |
| Is `mapcar` used only for effects?                    | Result ignored                | Use `dolist` or `mapc`                                           |
| Is `format nil` in a hot loop?                        | Allocating strings repeatedly | Write to stream or reuse buffer strategy                         |
| Is exact rational arithmetic accidental?              | Ratios appear in numeric code | Use floats deliberately                                          |
| Is generic arithmetic in hot code?                    | SBCL notes, profiling         | Add accurate type declarations                                   |
| Is a list used for random access?                     | `nth` in loop                 | Use vector/array                                                 |
| Is `equalp` used for hot hash keys?                   | Hash table test               | Use stricter equality if correct                                 |
| Is `sort` mutating caller data?                       | `sort` on shared sequence     | Copy first                                                       |
| Are closures capturing large objects?                 | Retained memory               | Use explicit state object or reduce capture                      |
| Is generic dispatch in an inner loop?                 | Profiler                      | Use ordinary function/local specialization if semantically valid |
| Are conditions used for frequent normal control flow? | Hot signaling path            | Use return values for expected cases                             |
| Are declarations added before validation?             | External data path            | Validate first, declare internal invariants                      |
| Is safety lowered globally?                           | `(declaim (optimize ...))`    | Keep aggressive settings local                                   |
| Is FFI called many times for tiny operations?         | Boundary overhead             | Batch calls or move work across boundary                         |

**Performance rule:** do not optimize syntax. Optimize representation, allocation, dispatch, arithmetic, and boundary crossings.

### Final synthesis by task pattern

| Task                      | Best first thought                           | Escalation path                           |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Need a value computation  | Write a function                             | Higher-order function or local helper     |
| Need private state        | Closure or structure                         | Class if protocol/inspection needed       |
| Need stable record        | `defstruct`                                  | `defclass` if CLOS extension needed       |
| Need open polymorphism    | `defgeneric` + methods                       | Method combination if lifecycle needed    |
| Need custom syntax        | Function first; macro only if necessary      | DSL only with grammar and diagnostics     |
| Need options/config       | `&key`, plist, config structure              | Parser + condition boundary               |
| Need lookup               | Hash table/alist/plist by size and semantics | Multiple values if `nil` valid            |
| Need text processing      | Standard string functions                    | Regex or parser when justified            |
| Need file I/O             | Streams + `with-open-file`                   | UIOP/path utilities                       |
| Need error reporting      | Condition class                              | Restarts if recovery is context-dependent |
| Need cleanup              | `with-*` or `unwind-protect`                 | Custom resource macro                     |
| Need performance          | Profile and inspect compiler notes           | Representation and declarations           |
| Need project organization | ASDF + packages                              | Internal packages and test systems        |
| Need deployment           | Explicit startup function                    | Image/executable/container workflow       |

### What to study after this guide

A serious learner should not proceed by reading random macro tricks. The next stage should be organized by mechanism.

| Next study area                               | Why it matters                         |
| --------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Common Lisp HyperSpec navigation              | Precise standard vocabulary            |
| SBCL manual and compiler notes                | Practical implementation behavior      |
| ASDF manual                                   | System definition and build discipline |
| UIOP utilities                                | Practical portability layer            |
| CLOS method combination and MOP overview      | Advanced object/protocol design        |
| Condition/restart examples                    | Distinctive recovery model             |
| Macro-writing case studies                    | Expansion discipline                   |
| Sequence and hash-table cost profiling        | Everyday performance                   |
| Testing framework practice                    | Reproducible correctness               |
| Deployment workflow for chosen implementation | Real application delivery              |

**Study rule:** learn one mechanism deeply enough to know when not to use it.

### Final expert standard

A Common Lisp programmer reaches expert judgment when they can consistently do the following:

| Expert capability                  | Meaning                                                           |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Explain a form’s evaluation rule   | Function, macro, special operator, or generated form              |
| Trace symbol identity              | Name, package, export status, keyword/uninterned status           |
| Choose representation deliberately | List vs vector vs hash table vs structure vs class                |
| Select equality correctly          | `eq`, `eql`, `equal`, `equalp`, numeric/string/char predicates    |
| Design public packages             | Export stable API and hide internals                              |
| Write macros conservatively        | Avoid double evaluation, capture, and phase confusion             |
| Design CLOS protocols              | Generic functions as documented operations                        |
| Model failure structurally         | Conditions, multiple values, restarts where appropriate           |
| Manage resources reliably          | `with-*`, `unwind-protect`, ownership rules                       |
| Control image state                | REPL for exploration, ASDF/fresh image for truth                  |
| Read compiler feedback             | Use implementation notes without confusing them with the standard |
| Maintain portability boundaries    | Separate ANSI CL, SBCL, ASDF, UIOP, and library assumptions       |
| Test dynamic behavior              | Multiple values, conditions, macros, mutation, package boundaries |
| Optimize responsibly               | Measure before local declarations and representation changes      |

**Final conclusion:** Common Lisp is best learned as a system of semantic powers: symbolic representation, explicit evaluation, syntactic abstraction, dynamic but disciplined typing, open generic dispatch, interactive recovery, live image development, and implementation-aware compilation. Its danger is the same as its strength: it permits the programmer to shape too much without forcing enough structure. Professional Common Lisp is therefore not maximal cleverness. It is controlled expressiveness.

## Appendix A — Common Lisp Syntax and Form Recognition Index

### A.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **form-recognition reference**. Its purpose is to help a reader look at a piece of Common Lisp source and answer the first necessary question:

**What kind of form is this, and what evaluation rule applies?**

Common Lisp syntax looks visually uniform because many forms are written as parenthesized lists. But semantic behavior is not uniform. The first element of a compound form may name a special operator, a macro, an ordinary function, a local function, a generic function, or a defining form. Each case has different evaluation behavior.

A beginner often reads this:

```lisp
(operator arg1 arg2 arg3)
```

as “call `operator` with three arguments.”

That is only sometimes correct.

| Surface shape    | Possible semantic kind                                              | Example                                |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| `object`         | Self-evaluating object                                              | `42`, `"hello"`, `#\A`                 |
| `symbol`         | Variable reference, constant symbol, special variable, symbol macro | `x`, `*print-case*`, `nil`, `t`        |
| `(operator ...)` | Special operator form                                               | `(if test a b)`                        |
| `(operator ...)` | Macro form                                                          | `(when test body...)`                  |
| `(operator ...)` | Ordinary function call                                              | `(+ 1 2)`                              |
| `(operator ...)` | Generic function call                                               | `(render object stream)`               |
| `(operator ...)` | Local function call                                                 | `(helper x)` inside `flet` or `labels` |
| `(operator ...)` | Definition form                                                     | `(defun f (x) ...)`                    |
| `(operator ...)` | Declaration-bearing form                                            | `(let (...) (declare ...) ...)`        |
| `(operator ...)` | DSL form                                                            | `(loop for x in xs collect x)`         |

**Core rule:** before reasoning about values, first classify the form.



### A.2 The complete first-pass recognition table

| Form pattern                     | Category                                                    | Evaluation rule                                                | Example                                        | Main pitfall                                                     |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `42`, `2/3`, `3.0d0`             | Self-evaluating number                                      | Evaluates to itself                                            | `42`                                           | Forgetting exact ratios such as `2/3` are exact rational numbers |
| `"abc"`                          | Self-evaluating string                                      | Evaluates to a string object                                   | `"abc"`                                        | Mutating literal strings                                         |
| `#\A`                            | Self-evaluating character                                   | Evaluates to a character                                       | `#\A`                                          | Confusing character with one-character string                    |
| `#(1 2 3)`                       | Vector literal                                              | Evaluates to literal vector object                             | `#(1 2 3)`                                     | Mutating literal compound data                                   |
| `:name`                          | Keyword symbol                                              | Evaluates to itself                                            | `:name`                                        | Treating keyword as string                                       |
| `nil` / `()`                     | False and empty list                                        | Evaluates to itself                                            | `nil`, `()`                                    | Forgetting `nil` is also empty list                              |
| `t`                              | Canonical true symbol                                       | Evaluates to itself                                            | `t`                                            | Thinking all predicates must return exactly `t`                  |
| `x`                              | Symbol form                                                 | Looks up variable binding                                      | `x`                                            | Package/scope confusion                                          |
| `*x*`                            | Usually special variable                                    | Dynamic variable lookup if declared special                    | `*standard-output*`                            | Hidden dynamic dependency                                        |
| `'x`                             | Quote abbreviation                                          | Reads as `(quote x)`                                           | `'name`                                        | Overquoting                                                      |
| `#'f`                            | Function quote abbreviation                                 | Reads as `(function f)`                                        | `#'car`                                        | Confusing function and value namespaces                          |
| `(quote x)`                      | Special operator                                            | Returns object without evaluating it                           | `(quote x)`                                    | Treating quoted compound data as fresh mutable data              |
| `(function f)`                   | Special operator                                            | Returns function binding/object                                | `(function car)`                               | Using when a variable contains a function object                 |
| `(if test then else)`            | Special operator                                            | Evaluates test, then one branch                                | `(if x a b)`                                   | Assuming both branches evaluate                                  |
| `(progn a b c)`                  | Special operator                                            | Evaluates forms in order, returns last values                  | `(progn (print x) x)`                          | Overusing where body already allows multiple forms               |
| `(let (...) ...)`                | Special operator                                            | Creates local bindings                                         | `(let ((x 1)) x)`                              | Confusing `let` and `let*`                                       |
| `(setq x v)`                     | Special operator                                            | Assigns variable binding                                       | `(setq x 10)`                                  | Using for generalized places where `setf` is needed              |
| `(setf place value)`             | Macro                                                       | Generalized assignment                                         | `(setf (car xs) 1)`                            | Thinking it is only variable assignment                          |
| `(when test body...)`            | Macro                                                       | Expands to conditional body execution                          | `(when x (print x))`                           | Treating as ordinary function                                    |
| `(unless test body...)`          | Macro                                                       | Expands to negative conditional body execution                 | `(unless x (error ...))`                       | Complex negative logic                                           |
| `(cond ...)`                     | Macro                                                       | Tests clauses in order                                         | `(cond ((x) a) (t b))`                         | Missing default clause                                           |
| `(case key ...)`                 | Macro                                                       | Compares key to literal alternatives                           | `(case status (:ok ...))`                      | Expecting case keys to be evaluated                              |
| `(loop ...)`                     | Macro DSL                                                   | Expands into iteration code                                    | `(loop for x in xs collect x)`                 | Treating `loop` syntax as ordinary Lisp syntax                   |
| `(+ a b)`                        | Function call                                               | Evaluates arguments left to right, calls function              | `(+ 1 2)`                                      | Forgetting arguments evaluate before call                        |
| `(render object stream)`         | Generic function call, if `render` names a generic function | Ordinary call followed by CLOS dispatch                        | `(render user s)`                              | Ignoring method dispatch                                         |
| `(helper x)` inside `flet`       | Local function call                                         | Calls local function binding                                   | `(helper x)`                                   | Confusing local and global function bindings                     |
| `(defun f (...) ...)`            | Defining macro                                              | Defines function                                               | `(defun square (x) (* x x))`                   | Treating as runtime object construction only                     |
| `(defmacro m (...) ...)`         | Defining macro                                              | Defines macro transformer                                      | `(defmacro m (...) ...)`                       | Forgetting later users must be macroexpanded/recompiled          |
| `(defclass c (...) ...)`         | Defining macro                                              | Defines class                                                  | `(defclass user () (...))`                     | Treating slots as automatically good public API                  |
| `(defgeneric g (...) ...)`       | Defining macro                                              | Defines generic function protocol                              | `(defgeneric render (x s))`                    | Leaving protocol undocumented                                    |
| `(defmethod g (...) ...)`        | Defining macro                                              | Defines method                                                 | `(defmethod render ((x user) s) ...)`          | Forgetting dispatch happens at runtime                           |
| `(define-condition c (...) ...)` | Defining macro                                              | Defines condition class                                        | `(define-condition invalid-input (error) ...)` | Using string errors for public API failures                      |
| `(defpackage ...)`               | Defining macro                                              | Defines package namespace policy                               | `(defpackage #:x ...)`                         | Thinking it loads implementation files                           |
| `(in-package ...)`               | Macro/top-level form                                        | Changes current package for following forms                    | `(in-package #:x)`                             | Thinking it imports a module                                     |
| `(declare ...)`                  | Declaration form, not evaluated as normal form              | Gives compiler/environment information in declaration contexts | `(declare (type fixnum x))`                    | Putting it where declarations are not recognized                 |
| `(the type form)`                | Special operator                                            | Declares/asserts result type of form                           | `(the fixnum x)`                               | Treating as runtime conversion                                   |
| `(eval-when (...) ...)`          | Special operator                                            | Controls compile/load/execute phase                            | `(eval-when (:compile-toplevel ...) ...)`      | Using without understanding phase                                |



### A.3 Self-evaluating forms

A **self-evaluating form** evaluates to itself. This category includes many literal objects.

```lisp
42
2/3
3.14
1.0d0
#\A
"hello"
#(1 2 3)
:keyword
```

| Literal          | Category              | Evaluates to          | Notes                                       |
| ---------------- | --------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `42`             | Integer               | Integer object        | Exact integer                               |
| `2/3`            | Ratio                 | Exact rational number | Not a float                                 |
| `3.14`           | Float                 | Float object          | Implementation/default float details matter |
| `1.0d0`          | Double float notation | Double-float value    | Common in numeric code                      |
| `#C(1 2)`        | Complex number        | Complex object        | Real and imaginary parts                    |
| `#\A`            | Character             | Character object      | Not the same as `"A"`                       |
| `"hello"`        | String                | String object         | Literal compound object; mutation caution   |
| `#(1 2 3)`       | Vector                | Vector object         | Literal compound object; mutation caution   |
| `#*1010`         | Bit vector            | Bit-vector object     | Reader syntax                               |
| `#P"/tmp/a.txt"` | Pathname              | Pathname object       | Pathname portability caveats                |
| `:name`          | Keyword symbol        | Same keyword symbol   | Self-evaluating symbol                      |

**Important distinction:** self-evaluating does not always mean “safe to mutate.”

Numbers and characters are effectively value-like. But strings, vectors, and other compound literals may be treated as constants in source code. Destructive modification of literal compound data is a serious mistake.

Bad:

```lisp
(defparameter *xs* '(1 2 3))

(setf (car *xs*) 99)
```

Better:

```lisp
(defparameter *xs* (list 1 2 3))

(setf (car *xs*) 99)
```

**Recognition rule:** if the form is a literal object and not a symbol or list form requiring evaluation, it probably evaluates to itself. If it is compound literal data, treat it as constant unless freshly constructed.



### A.4 Symbol forms

A symbol form is usually a variable reference.

```lisp
x
user
current-status
*standard-output*
```

When evaluated, the symbol is looked up in the current lexical and dynamic environment.

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  x)
;; => 10
```

But some symbols are self-evaluating by special rule or convention:

```lisp
nil
t
:keyword
```

| Symbol form | Category                                                      | Evaluation behavior                                                             |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `x`         | Ordinary variable reference                                   | Looks up lexical or dynamic variable binding                                    |
| `*x*`       | Special variable by convention if declared/proclaimed special | Dynamic lookup                                                                  |
| `nil`       | Constant symbol / empty list / false                          | Evaluates to itself                                                             |
| `t`         | Constant true symbol                                          | Evaluates to itself                                                             |
| `:name`     | Keyword symbol                                                | Evaluates to itself                                                             |
| `cl:car`    | Package-qualified symbol                                      | Depends on context; as form, variable lookup unless quoted or function position |
| `#:temp`    | Uninterned symbol syntax                                      | As data when quoted/read; as form, variable lookup if evaluated                 |

Example:

```lisp
(let ((name "Ada"))
  name)
;; => "Ada"
```

Quoted symbol:

```lisp
(let ((name "Ada"))
  'name)
;; => NAME, the symbol, not "Ada"
```

Keyword:

```lisp
:name
;; => :NAME
```

**Failure-first explanation:** the surface text `name` does not mean the string `"name"`. It reads as a symbol, and if evaluated as a form, it is a variable reference.

**Recognition rule:** if a symbol appears alone as a form, ask whether it is `nil`, `t`, a keyword, a symbol macro, a lexical variable, or a special variable.



### A.5 Compound forms: the operator-position question

A compound form is a list used as a form:

```lisp
(operator subform-1 subform-2 ...)
```

The first element is in **operator position**. Recognition depends on what the operator position denotes.

| Operator names...          | Form kind                      | Example                                                     |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Special operator           | Special form                   | `(if test then else)`                                       |
| Macro                      | Macro form                     | `(when test body...)`                                       |
| Function                   | Function call                  | `(+ 1 2)`                                                   |
| Generic function           | Generic function call          | `(render object stream)`                                    |
| Local function             | Local function call            | `(helper x)`                                                |
| Lambda expression          | Direct lambda call             | `((lambda (x) (+ x 1)) 10)`                                 |
| Symbol macro?              | Not operator-position function | Usually relevant in value position, not direct operator use |
| Invalid/non-function value | Error                          | `(42 x)`                                                    |

Ordinary function call:

```lisp
(+ (* 2 3) 4)
```

Evaluation:

1. Evaluate `(* 2 3)`.
2. Evaluate `4`.
3. Call function `+` with the resulting values.

Special operator:

```lisp
(if (> x 0)
    (print "positive")
    (print "not positive"))
```

Evaluation:

1. Evaluate test.
2. Evaluate only selected branch.

Macro:

```lisp
(when (> x 0)
  (print "positive"))
```

Evaluation:

1. Expand macro.
2. Evaluate expansion.

**Recognition rule:** do not assume parentheses mean a function call. First identify the operator.



### A.6 Ordinary function calls

An ordinary function call has this semantic pattern:

```lisp
(function-name argument-form-1 argument-form-2 ...)
```

The argument forms are evaluated left to right. The function is then called with their primary values.

```lisp
(defun square (x)
  (* x x))

(square (+ 1 2))
;; => 9
```

Evaluation:

1. Evaluate `(+ 1 2)` to `3`.
2. Call `square` with `3`.

Function object stored in variable:

```lisp
(let ((f #'square))
  (funcall f 5))
;; => 25
```

Directly writing this does **not** call the function stored in variable `f`:

```lisp
(f 5)
```

That attempts to call the function binding named by symbol `f`, not the value of variable `f`.

Use:

```lisp
(funcall f 5)
```

Apply argument list:

```lisp
(apply #'+ '(1 2 3 4))
;; => 10
```

| Pattern          | Meaning                                           |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `(f x)`          | Call function binding named `f`                   |
| `(funcall f x)`  | Call function object stored in variable `f`       |
| `(apply f args)` | Call function object `f` with arguments from list |
| `#'f`            | Refer to function binding named `f`               |
| `'f`             | Refer to symbol `f` as data                       |

**Common pitfall:** Common Lisp is a Lisp-2. Function bindings and value bindings are separate.

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  (+ x 1))

(let ((f 100))
  (list f
        (funcall #'f 10)))
;; => (100 11)
```

The variable `f` and function `f` are different bindings.



### A.7 Special operators

Special operators have built-in evaluation rules. They are not functions and cannot be implemented as ordinary functions because they control evaluation, binding, assignment, declaration, or nonlocal control.

Important special operators include:

| Special operator       | Main role                                  |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `quote`                | Prevent evaluation                         |
| `if`                   | Conditional evaluation                     |
| `progn`                | Sequential evaluation                      |
| `let`                  | Local parallel binding                     |
| `let*`                 | Local sequential binding                   |
| `setq`                 | Variable assignment                        |
| `function`             | Refer to function binding/object           |
| `block`                | Establish named exit                       |
| `return-from`          | Exit named block                           |
| `tagbody`              | Low-level tagged control                   |
| `go`                   | Jump to tag                                |
| `catch`                | Establish dynamic catch tag                |
| `throw`                | Transfer to catch tag                      |
| `unwind-protect`       | Ensure cleanup                             |
| `flet`                 | Local function bindings                    |
| `labels`               | Recursive local function bindings          |
| `macrolet`             | Local macro bindings                       |
| `symbol-macrolet`      | Local symbol macro bindings                |
| `the`                  | Type assertion/declaration for form result |
| `locally`              | Local declarations                         |
| `multiple-value-call`  | Multiple-value argument plumbing           |
| `multiple-value-prog1` | Preserve first form’s multiple values      |
| `progv`                | Dynamic binding of symbols                 |
| `eval-when`            | Phase control                              |
| `load-time-value`      | Load-time object computation               |

#### `quote`

```lisp
(quote x)
'x
```

Returns `x` as data, without evaluating it.

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  'x)
;; => X
```

#### `if`

```lisp
(if test
    then-form
    else-form)
```

Only the selected branch evaluates.

```lisp
(if (> x 0)
    "positive"
    "not positive")
```

#### `let` and `let*`

`let` evaluates initializers in the outer environment.

```lisp
(let ((x 1))
  (let ((x 2)
        (y x))
    (list x y)))
;; => (2 1)
```

`let*` evaluates and binds sequentially.

```lisp
(let* ((x 1)
       (y x))
  (list x y))
;; => (1 1)
```

#### `block` and `return-from`

```lisp
(block search
  (dolist (x xs)
    (when (target-p x)
      (return-from search x)))
  nil)
```

#### `unwind-protect`

```lisp
(let ((resource (open-resource)))
  (unwind-protect
       (use-resource resource)
    (close-resource resource)))
```

Ensures cleanup even on nonlocal exit.

**Recognition rule:** if the operator is a special operator, ordinary function-call evaluation does not apply.



### A.8 Macro forms

A macro form looks like a function call but is expanded before runtime evaluation.

```lisp
(when test
  body...)
```

`when` is not an ordinary function. It is a macro because it must avoid evaluating the body when the test is false.

Approximate expansion:

```lisp
(if test
    (progn body...)
    nil)
```

Examples of common macro forms:

| Macro                   | Role                                |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `when`                  | Conditional body if true            |
| `unless`                | Conditional body if false           |
| `cond`                  | Multi-branch condition              |
| `case`                  | Literal finite dispatch             |
| `ecase`                 | Exhaustive literal finite dispatch  |
| `typecase`              | Type dispatch                       |
| `etypecase`             | Exhaustive type dispatch            |
| `and`                   | Short-circuit conjunction           |
| `or`                    | Short-circuit disjunction           |
| `setf`                  | Generalized assignment              |
| `incf`, `decf`          | Increment/decrement place           |
| `push`, `pop`           | List-place update                   |
| `rotatef`, `shiftf`     | Place value movement                |
| `loop`                  | Iteration DSL                       |
| `dolist`                | List iteration                      |
| `dotimes`               | Count iteration                     |
| `do`, `do*`             | General iteration                   |
| `with-open-file`        | File stream resource scope          |
| `with-output-to-string` | String output stream scope          |
| `destructuring-bind`    | Pattern binding over list structure |
| `defun`                 | Function definition                 |
| `defmacro`              | Macro definition                    |
| `defclass`              | Class definition                    |
| `defgeneric`            | Generic function definition         |
| `defmethod`             | Method definition                   |
| `define-condition`      | Condition class definition          |
| `defpackage`            | Package definition                  |

Macro expansion can be inspected:

```lisp
(macroexpand-1
 '(when (> x 0)
    (print x)))
```

**Macro recognition questions:**

| Question                                  | Why it matters                                          |
| ----------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Does this form control evaluation?        | It likely must be a macro or special operator           |
| Does it introduce bindings?               | Functions cannot create caller-visible lexical bindings |
| Does it manage resource lifetime?         | Often a `with-*` macro                                  |
| Does it look like a definition?           | Usually a macro defining global objects                 |
| Does it use DSL-like syntax?              | Likely a macro such as `loop`                           |
| Does expansion duplicate subforms?        | Possible double evaluation                              |
| Does expansion introduce temporary names? | Capture risk                                            |

**Common pitfall:** macro arguments are source forms, not runtime values.

Bad:

```lisp
(defmacro bad-square (x)
  (* x x))
```

Good macro shape, though still double-evaluating `x`:

```lisp
(defmacro square-form (x)
  `(* ,x ,x))
```

Safer:

```lisp
(defmacro square-once (x)
  (let ((temp (gensym "X-")))
    `(let ((,temp ,x))
       (* ,temp ,temp))))
```

**Recognition rule:** if the operator names a macro, ask what it expands into before reasoning about runtime behavior.

### A.9 Definition forms

Definition forms usually look like ordinary compound forms, but they are normally macros that establish global or top-level definitions.

```lisp
(defun square (x)
  (* x x))
```

This does not call a function named `defun` at runtime in the ordinary sense. It defines a function binding.

| Definition form                    | Defines                                           | Typical result of evaluating form | Main recognition issue                                               |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `defun`                            | Function                                          | Function name                     | Function body is not called immediately                              |
| `defmacro`                         | Macro transformer                                 | Macro name                        | Later uses expand through this transformer                           |
| `defvar`                           | Special variable, if unbound                      | Variable name                     | Does not reset value if already bound                                |
| `defparameter`                     | Special variable, assigned value                  | Variable name                     | Resets on evaluation                                                 |
| `defconstant`                      | Constant                                          | Constant name                     | Redefinition can be implementation-sensitive                         |
| `defstruct`                        | Structure type, constructor, accessors, predicate | Structure name                    | Creates several related functions/types                              |
| `defclass`                         | CLOS class                                        | Class name                        | Defines slots and class metadata, not Java-style class-owned methods |
| `defgeneric`                       | Generic function                                  | Generic function name             | Defines protocol                                                     |
| `defmethod`                        | Method on generic function                        | Method object/name information    | Runtime dispatch depends on specializers                             |
| `define-condition`                 | Condition class                                   | Condition name                    | Public error type if exported                                        |
| `deftype`                          | Type specifier abbreviation                       | Type name                         | Does not create new runtime object representation                    |
| `defpackage`                       | Package                                           | Package object/name               | Namespace only, not loading                                          |
| `defsetf` / `define-setf-expander` | `setf` update protocol                            | Name                              | Advanced generalized-place protocol                                  |
| `define-modify-macro`              | Place-modifying macro                             | Macro name                        | Advanced place abstraction                                           |
| `define-compiler-macro`            | Compiler macro                                    | Function name                     | Optimization transformation, not ordinary macro syntax               |

#### `defun`

```lisp
(defun greet (name)
  "Return a greeting for NAME."
  (format nil "Hello, ~A." name))
```

Recognition:

| Part      | Meaning                                                               |
| --------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `defun`   | Definition macro                                                      |
| `greet`   | Function name                                                         |
| `(name)`  | Lambda list                                                           |
| docstring | Function documentation                                                |
| body      | Code evaluated when function is called, not when `defun` is evaluated |

#### `defmacro`

```lisp
(defmacro when-found ((var expr) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     (when ,var
       ,@body)))
```

Recognition:

| Part                      | Meaning                                   |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| `defmacro`                | Defines macro transformer                 |
| `when-found`              | Macro name                                |
| `((var expr) &body body)` | Macro lambda list receiving source syntax |
| backquoted body           | Constructs expansion                      |
| later call                | Expanded before runtime                   |

#### `defclass`, `defgeneric`, `defmethod`

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
   (email :initarg :email :reader user-email)))

(defgeneric render (object stream)
  (:documentation "Render OBJECT to STREAM."))

(defmethod render ((object user) stream)
  (format stream "~A <~A>"
          (user-name object)
          (user-email object)))
```

Recognition:

| Form         | Meaning                                      |
| ------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| `defclass`   | Defines class and slot metadata              |
| `defgeneric` | Defines operation/protocol                   |
| `defmethod`  | Defines method specialized on argument types |

**Failure-first explanation:** a definition form may execute at load time, but its main purpose is to establish a definition in the image. Do not read `(defun f ...)` as “run `f` now.”

**Recognition rule:** if a form starts with `def...` or `define...`, first assume it is a defining macro. Ask what global binding, type, package, protocol, or compiler information it establishes.



### A.10 Declaration forms

Declarations are not ordinary executable forms. They appear only in declaration contexts.

Common declaration contexts include:

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  (declare (type integer x))
  (+ x 1))
```

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  (declare (fixnum x))
  (* x x))
```

```lisp
(locally
  (declare (optimize (speed 3)))
  (compute))
```

| Declaration construct          | Category                    | Meaning                                    |
| ------------------------------ | --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `declare`                      | Declaration form            | Local compiler/environment information     |
| `declaim`                      | Top-level declaration macro | Global proclamation at compile/load time   |
| `proclaim`                     | Function                    | Runtime proclamation                       |
| `the`                          | Special operator            | Declare/assert type of a form result       |
| `locally`                      | Special operator            | Introduce declaration scope                |
| `declare special`              | Declaration                 | Makes variable dynamically scoped in scope |
| `declare ignore`               | Declaration                 | Suppress unused variable warnings          |
| `declare ignorable`            | Declaration                 | Variable may or may not be used            |
| `declare dynamic-extent`       | Declaration                 | Object does not escape dynamic extent      |
| `declare optimize`             | Declaration                 | Compiler policy                            |
| `declare inline` / `notinline` | Declaration                 | Inlining policy                            |

Correct:

```lisp
(defun add (x y)
  (declare (type fixnum x y))
  (+ x y))
```

Incorrect use as an ordinary form:

```lisp
(progn
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

A `declare` form only has meaning where declarations are syntactically recognized. It is not an ordinary runtime check.

`the` example:

```lisp
(the fixnum (+ x y))
```

This does not convert the result to a `fixnum`. It asserts or declares that the result is of that type, depending on context and implementation policy.

**Failure-first explanation:** declarations are not validators.

Bad boundary design:

```lisp
(defun parse-count (x)
  (declare (type integer x))
  x)
```

If `x` is external input, this does not parse or validate it.

Better:

```lisp
(defun parse-count (x)
  (unless (typep x '(integer 0 *))
    (error "Invalid count: ~S" x))
  x)
```

**Recognition rule:** if a form starts with `declare`, it is meaningful only in a declaration position. If a form starts with `the`, it is a type assertion/declaration around a value-producing form, not a conversion function.



### A.11 Body forms and implicit `progn`

Many Common Lisp constructs have a **body**: a sequence of forms evaluated in order, with the values of the last form returned.

Examples:

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  (print x)
  (+ x 1))
```

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  (print x)
  (* x x))
```

```lisp
(when condition
  (log-message "condition true")
  (do-work)
  :done)
```

| Construct              | Has body? | Return value                                  |
| ---------------------- | --------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| `defun`                | Yes       | Last body form when function is called        |
| `lambda`               | Yes       | Last body form when function is called        |
| `let`, `let*`          | Yes       | Last body form                                |
| `flet`, `labels`       | Yes       | Last body form                                |
| `macrolet`             | Yes       | Last body form after expansion rules          |
| `when`, `unless`       | Yes       | Last body form if executed, otherwise `nil`   |
| `dolist`, `dotimes`    | Yes       | Usually optional result form, otherwise `nil` |
| `with-open-file`       | Yes       | Body values                                   |
| `handler-case` clauses | Yes       | Clause body values                            |
| `restart-case` clauses | Yes       | Restart body values                           |
| `progn`                | Yes       | Last form values                              |

Implicit `progn` means this:

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  a
  b
  c)
```

acts like:

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  (progn
    a
    b
    c))
```

in the function body sense.

`prog1` is different:

```lisp
(prog1
    (compute-result)
  (cleanup-or-log))
```

It returns the values of the first form, not the last.

| Sequencing construct   | Returns                      |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| `progn`                | Last form’s values           |
| `prog1`                | First form’s values          |
| `prog2`                | Second form’s values         |
| `multiple-value-prog1` | First form’s multiple values |

**Recognition rule:** if a construct has a body, do not look for an explicit `return` keyword. In ordinary code, the last body form is the return value.



### A.12 Binding forms

Binding forms introduce names with a scope.

| Binding form                       | Binds                          | Scope kind                              | Use                                 |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `let`                              | Variables                      | Lexical by default, dynamic if special  | Parallel local bindings             |
| `let*`                             | Variables                      | Lexical by default, dynamic if special  | Sequential local bindings           |
| Function parameters                | Variables                      | Lexical by default, special if declared | Function call input                 |
| `lambda`                           | Variables                      | Lexical by default                      | Anonymous function                  |
| `flet`                             | Local functions                | Lexical function namespace              | Non-recursive local helper          |
| `labels`                           | Local functions                | Lexical function namespace              | Recursive/mutually recursive helper |
| `macrolet`                         | Local macros                   | Lexical macro environment               | Local syntactic abstraction         |
| `symbol-macrolet`                  | Symbol macros                  | Lexical symbol expansion                | Advanced place/syntax abstraction   |
| `destructuring-bind`               | Variables from list pattern    | Lexical by default                      | Destructure list-shaped data        |
| `multiple-value-bind`              | Variables from multiple values | Lexical by default                      | Receive multiple values             |
| `handler-case` clause variable     | Condition variable             | Lexical                                 | Handle condition                    |
| `restart-case` restart lambda list | Restart parameters             | Lexical                                 | Recovery arguments                  |
| `with-open-file`                   | Stream variable                | Lexical binding around resource         | File resource scope                 |

#### `let`

```lisp
(let ((x 1)
      (y 2))
  (+ x y))
```

The initializer forms are evaluated before the new bindings are active.

#### `let*`

```lisp
(let* ((x 1)
       (y (+ x 1)))
  (+ x y))
```

Later bindings see earlier ones.

#### `flet`

```lisp
(flet ((double (x)
         (* 2 x)))
  (double 10))
```

#### `labels`

```lisp
(labels ((fact (n)
           (if (zerop n)
               1
               (* n (fact (1- n))))))
  (fact 5))
```

#### `multiple-value-bind`

```lisp
(multiple-value-bind (quotient remainder)
    (floor 7 3)
  (list quotient remainder))
```

#### `destructuring-bind`

```lisp
(destructuring-bind (name age)
    '("Ada" 36)
  (format nil "~A is ~A." name age))
```

**Failure-first explanation:** not all names live in the same namespace.

```lisp
(defun f (x)
  (+ x 1))

(let ((f 100))
  (funcall #'f 10))
;; => 11
```

The value binding `f` and function binding `f` are distinct.

**Recognition rule:** when reading a form, identify newly introduced names and ask which namespace they enter: value, function, macro, block, tag, type/class, or package.



### A.13 Assignment and place forms

Common Lisp has variable assignment and generalized place assignment.

| Assignment form | Category         | Updates                               |
| --------------- | ---------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `setq`          | Special operator | Variable binding                      |
| `setf`          | Macro            | Generalized place                     |
| `psetq`         | Macro            | Parallel variable assignment          |
| `psetf`         | Macro            | Parallel generalized place assignment |
| `incf`          | Macro            | Numeric place increment               |
| `decf`          | Macro            | Numeric place decrement               |
| `push`          | Macro            | Cons onto list stored in place        |
| `pop`           | Macro            | Remove first element from list place  |
| `rotatef`       | Macro            | Rotate place values                   |
| `shiftf`        | Macro            | Shift place values                    |

Variable assignment:

```lisp
(let ((x 1))
  (setq x 2)
  x)
;; => 2
```

Generalized place assignment:

```lisp
(let ((xs (list 1 2 3)))
  (setf (car xs) 99)
  xs)
;; => (99 2 3)
```

Array place:

```lisp
(let ((v (vector 1 2 3)))
  (setf (aref v 1) 99)
  v)
;; => #(1 99 3)
```

Hash table place:

```lisp
(let ((table (make-hash-table)))
  (setf (gethash :x table) 10)
  (gethash :x table))
;; => 10
```

CLOS accessor place:

```lisp
(setf (user-name user) "Ada")
```

**Failure-first explanation:** `setf` is not just Lisp spelling for assignment. It is a generalized update protocol.

```lisp
(setf (car xs) 1)
(setf (aref vector i) value)
(setf (gethash key table) value)
(setf (slot-accessor object) value)
```

These all update different kinds of places.

**Recognition rule:** when seeing `setf`, identify the place, not merely the value. Ask what object or binding is being updated.



### A.14 Control forms

Control forms decide which forms are evaluated, how often, and in what order.

| Control task              | Common form                          | Category          |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ----------------- |
