| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `` `form ``            | Template form                              |
| `,x`                   | Insert value of `x` into template          |
| `,@xs`                 | Splice list `xs` into surrounding list     |
| `',x`                  | Quote the inserted value in generated code |
| `,x` outside backquote | Invalid                                    |

Example:

```lisp
(let ((name 'x)
      (value 10))
  `(let ((,name ,value))
     ,name))
```

Produces:

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  x)
```

Body splicing:

```lisp
(defmacro my-progn (&body body)
  `(progn ,@body))
```

Call:

```lisp
(my-progn
  (print 1)
  (print 2))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(progn
  (print 1)
  (print 2))
```

Without splicing:

```lisp
(defmacro bad-progn (&body body)
  `(progn ,body))
```

Expansion would contain the list of body forms as one form, which is wrong.

**Professional rule:** use `,` to insert one object; use `,@` to splice a list of forms into a surrounding list.



### E.6 Evaluation count

The most common macro bug is evaluating a subform more times than the caller expects.

Bad:

```lisp
(defmacro square-form (x)
  `(* ,x ,x))
```

Call:

```lisp
(square-form (next-value))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(* (next-value) (next-value))
```

`next-value` runs twice.

Safer:

```lisp
(defmacro square-once (x)
  (let ((temp (gensym "X-")))
    `(let ((,temp ,x))
       (* ,temp ,temp))))
```

Expansion shape:

```lisp
(let ((#:x-123 (next-value)))
  (* #:x-123 #:x-123))
```

| Macro pattern                         | Evaluation count risk         |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| `,x` appears once                     | Usually once                  |
| `,x` appears twice                    | May evaluate twice            |
| `,@body` appears twice                | Body executes twice           |
| Subform appears under conditional     | May evaluate zero or one time |
| Subform appears inside generated loop | May evaluate many times       |
| Subform bound to `gensym` temp        | Usually controlled once       |

Sometimes evaluating body twice is intentional:

```lisp
(defmacro do-twice (&body body)
  `(progn
     ,@body
     ,@body))
```

But it must be documented.

**Professional rule:** every macro should have an evaluation-count contract.



### E.7 Evaluation order

Macros must also preserve or document evaluation order.

Bad example:

```lisp
(defmacro bad-with-values ((a b) expr1 expr2 &body body)
  `(let ((,b ,expr2)
         (,a ,expr1))
     ,@body))
```

Callers may expect `expr1` before `expr2`, but the macro evaluates `expr2` first.

Better:

```lisp
(defmacro with-values ((a b) expr1 expr2 &body body)
  `(let ((,a ,expr1))
     (let ((,b ,expr2))
       ,@body)))
```

or:

```lisp
(defmacro with-values ((a b) expr1 expr2 &body body)
  `(let* ((,a ,expr1)
          (,b ,expr2))
     ,@body))
```

| Situation               | Evaluation-order concern            |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Generated `let`         | Initializer order matters           |
| Generated `let*`        | Sequential evaluation is explicit   |
| Generated function call | Arguments evaluate left to right    |
| Generated `and` / `or`  | Short-circuiting changes evaluation |
| Generated loop          | Repeated evaluation                 |
| Generated condition     | Branch-specific evaluation          |

**Professional rule:** macro expansions should evaluate user subforms in a predictable order, usually the order in which they appear in the macro call.



### E.8 Variable capture

Common Lisp macros are not hygienic by default. Macro-generated symbols can accidentally capture or be captured by caller bindings.

Bad:

```lisp
(defmacro with-temp (expr &body body)
  `(let ((temp ,expr))
     ,@body))
```

Call:

```lisp
(let ((temp :outer))
  (with-temp 10
    temp))
```

The `temp` inside the body may refer to the macro’s binding, not the caller’s intended variable.

If `temp` is internal, use `gensym`:

```lisp
(defmacro with-temp-internal (expr &body body)
  (let ((temp (gensym "TEMP-")))
    `(let ((,temp ,expr))
       ,@body)))
```

But this macro is useless if the body needs the value. If the macro should expose the binding, require the user to name it:

```lisp
(defmacro with-value ((var expr) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     ,@body))
```

Call:

```lisp
(with-value (temp 10)
  temp)
```

| Binding kind                       | Correct strategy                             |
| ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Internal temporary                 | `gensym`                                     |
| User-visible variable              | User supplies the name                       |
| Symbolic tag intentionally visible | Document it                                  |
| Generated function name            | Usually supplied or constructed deliberately |
| Package-qualified operator         | Use intended package symbol                  |

**Professional rule:** generated internal names should be `gensym`s; user-visible names should be explicit in the macro syntax.



### E.9 Package capture and symbol origin

Macros generate symbols. Those symbols belong to packages. If not handled carefully, the generated code may refer to the wrong symbol.

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro bad-when (test &body body)
  `(if ,test
       (progn ,@body)))
```

Here `if` and `progn` are symbols in the macro definition package. If that package uses `COMMON-LISP`, these may resolve correctly when the macro is read and compiled. Usually this works because backquote includes the actual symbols.

But problems arise when generating unqualified symbols intended to be looked up in the caller package.

Bad:

```lisp
(defmacro call-helper (x)
  `(helper ,x))
```

Which `helper` is this? It is the symbol `HELPER` as read in the macro definition package, not automatically the caller’s `helper`.

If the helper is internal to the macro’s package, qualify it deliberately:

```lisp
(defmacro call-helper (x)
  `(my-package::helper ,x))
```

If the helper should be supplied by the caller, make it syntax:

```lisp
(defmacro call-with-helper (helper x)
  `(,helper ,x))
```

| Generated symbol kind | Strategy                                              |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| CL operator           | Use normal `cl` symbols through package setup         |
| Internal helper       | Use package-qualified/internal symbol deliberately    |
| Public helper         | Use exported package-qualified symbol                 |
| Caller variable       | User provides variable name                           |
| Caller function       | User provides function name or function object syntax |
| Temporary             | `gensym`                                              |

**Professional rule:** macro expansions should not accidentally depend on the caller’s package.



### E.10 User-visible binding macros

A binding macro introduces variables for the caller.

Examples:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream path :direction :input)
  (read-line stream nil nil))
```

```lisp
(when-let (user (find-user id))
  (notify user))
```

The syntax usually contains a binding specification:

```lisp
(var expr)
```

Example implementation:

```lisp
(defmacro when-let ((var expr) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     (when ,var
       ,@body)))
```

Key design questions:

| Question                                 | Why it matters                 |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| What names are bound?                    | Caller must know scope         |
| Is expression evaluated once?            | Avoid duplicate side effects   |
| What if value is `nil`?                  | Body execution rule            |
| What values are returned?                | API contract                   |
| Can declarations appear?                 | Body/declaration handling      |
| Are multiple values preserved?           | Often not unless designed      |
| Is binding lexical or dynamic?           | Usually lexical unless special |
| Can the variable name be `_` or ignored? | Style/tooling issue            |

Example with explicit declaration support is usually automatic because declarations can appear at the start of body where the expansion places them.

```lisp
(when-let (user (find-user id))
  (declare (type user user))
  (notify user))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(let ((user (find-user id)))
  (when user
    (declare (type user user))
    (notify user)))
```

This particular expansion may be problematic because declarations inside `when` may or may not be in the intended declaration context depending on expansion. A more careful macro may need to handle declarations/body parsing if declaration support is important.

**Professional rule:** binding macros must document scope, evaluation count, and return values.



### E.11 Body macros and return values

Many macros accept `&body`.

```lisp
(defmacro my-when (test &body body)
  `(if ,test
       (progn ,@body)
       nil))
```

A body macro should specify what it returns.

Examples:

| Macro                   | Return behavior                                         |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| `when`                  | Values of last body form if test true; otherwise `nil`  |
| `unless`                | Values of last body form if test false; otherwise `nil` |
| `with-open-file`        | Values of body                                          |
| `with-output-to-string` | The generated string                                    |
| `prog1`-style macro     | Values of first form                                    |
| Resource wrapper        | Usually body values unless documented otherwise         |
| Definition macro        | Usually name or implementation-specific useful value    |

Resource macro preserving body values:

```lisp
(defmacro with-resource ((var acquire release) &body body)
  (let ((result (gensym "RESULT-")))
    `(let ((,var ,acquire))
       (unwind-protect
            (multiple-value-prog1
                (progn ,@body)
              ;; optional before-cleanup action
              )
         ,release))))
```

A cleaner concrete version:

```lisp
(defmacro with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

This usually preserves multiple values from `progn`, because `unwind-protect` returns the protected form’s values.

Bad macro that returns cleanup result instead of body result:

```lisp
(defmacro bad-with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (prog1
         (progn ,@body)
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

This one actually preserves body value because of `prog1`; a truly bad version is:

```lisp
(defmacro worse-with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (progn
       ,@body
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

Here the macro returns the value of `(close-widget var)`, not the body.

**Professional rule:** body macros should normally return the body’s meaningful values unless the macro’s purpose says otherwise.



### E.12 Resource macros

Resource macros are among the strongest legitimate macro use cases.

Standard example:

```lisp
(with-open-file (stream path :direction :input)
  (read-line stream nil nil))
```

A custom resource macro:

```lisp
(defmacro with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

Key responsibilities:

| Responsibility           | Why                              |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Acquire resource once    | Avoid duplicate side effects     |
| Bind resource variable   | Caller needs access              |
| Run body                 | Main work                        |
| Cleanup on normal exit   | Prevent leaks                    |
| Cleanup on nonlocal exit | Handle errors/returns/throws     |
| Preserve body values     | Avoid surprising return contract |
| Avoid variable capture   | Internal names need `gensym`     |
| Document lifetime        | Resource valid only inside body  |

Potential issue with `release` forms that refer to `var`:

```lisp
(defmacro with-resource ((var acquire release) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,acquire))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       ,release)))
```

Call:

```lisp
(with-resource (r (open-r) (close-r r))
  (use-r r))
```

This can be acceptable, but the macro’s syntax should clearly document that `release` is code evaluated in a scope where `var` is bound.

A more controlled macro is often better:

```lisp
(defmacro with-open-resource ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-resource ,spec)))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       (close-resource ,var))))
```

**Professional rule:** resource macros should be boring, predictable, and based on `unwind-protect`.



### E.13 Definition-generating macros

Macros can generate definitions.

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro define-simple-predicate (name slot expected)
  `(defun ,name (object)
     (eql (,slot object) ,expected)))
```

Call:

```lisp
(define-simple-predicate active-user-p user-status :active)
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(defun active-user-p (object)
  (eql (user-status object) :active))
```

Definition macros are useful when a repeated definition pattern is stable.

Common definition-generating macros include:

| Macro                         | Defines                         |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| `defun`                       | Function                        |
| `defmacro`                    | Macro                           |
| `defclass`                    | Class                           |
| `defgeneric`                  | Generic function                |
| `defmethod`                   | Method                          |
| `defstruct`                   | Structure and related functions |
| `define-condition`            | Condition class                 |
| `deftype`                     | Type specifier abbreviation     |
| Project-specific `define-...` | Repeated framework concepts     |

Design questions:

| Question                                   | Reason                             |
| ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
| What names are generated?                  | Public API implications            |
| Which package receives generated symbols?  | Symbol correctness                 |
| Are definitions available at compile time? | Macro users may need phase control |
| Can generated code be inspected?           | Debugging                          |
| Does the macro create too much hidden API? | Maintainability                    |
| Are docstrings generated?                  | Documentation                      |
| Are conditions/errors readable?            | Diagnostics                        |

**Failure-first explanation:** definition macros can hide too much.

A macro that defines five functions, two classes, and several methods from one compact form can be powerful, but it can also make the codebase hard to navigate.

**Professional rule:** definition macros should generate predictable names and provide clear expansion/debugging paths.



### E.14 Macro expansion tools

Macro debugging begins with expansion inspection.

```lisp
(macroexpand-1 '(when x y))
```

expands one macro layer.

```lisp
(macroexpand '(when x y))
```

expands repeatedly until the top-level form is no longer a macro form.

| Tool                           | Use                                |
| ------------------------------ | ---------------------------------- |
| `macroexpand-1`                | Inspect one expansion step         |
| `macroexpand`                  | Inspect full top-level expansion   |
| editor macroexpand command     | Interactive macro debugging        |
| `*macroexpand-hook*`           | Advanced expansion instrumentation |
| compiler warnings              | Catch expansion-generated issues   |
| `trace` macro helper functions | Debug expansion-time helper logic  |
| `pprint`                       | Pretty-print expansion             |
| tests                          | Check macro behavior               |

Example:

```lisp
(pprint
 (macroexpand-1
  '(when-let (user (find-user id))
     (notify user))))
```

If a macro expands into another macro, `macroexpand-1` is often easier to understand than full expansion.

**Professional rule:** always inspect nontrivial macro expansions.



### E.15 Macro testing

Macros need two kinds of tests:

1. **Behavior tests**: does the macro call behave correctly?
2. **Expansion tests**: does the macro expand with the intended evaluation/binding structure?

Behavior test:

```lisp
(test when-let-behavior
  (let ((called nil))
    (when-let (x 1)
      (setf called x))
    (is (= called 1))))
```

Evaluation-count test:

```lisp
(test when-let-evaluates-once
  (let ((count 0))
    (when-let (x (incf count))
      x)
    (is (= count 1))))
```

Capture test:

```lisp
(test macro-does-not-capture-temp
  (let ((temp :outer))
    (with-some-macro (...)
      ...)
    (is (eq temp :outer))))
```

Expansion shape test:

```lisp
(is (equal
     (macroexpand-1 '(my-when test body))
     '(if test (progn body) nil)))
```

Expansion tests can be brittle if they check exact generated symbols or implementation-dependent expansions. Prefer semantic expansion tests unless exact expansion is part of the contract.

| Test type             | Use                                  |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Behavior test         | Public macro behavior                |
| Evaluation count test | Prevent double evaluation            |
| Capture test          | Prevent accidental binding capture   |
| Expansion smoke test  | Check broad expansion shape          |
| Error test            | Invalid syntax reports useful errors |
| Compile/load test     | Macro available at needed phase      |
| Recompile test        | Macro changes propagate to users     |

**Professional rule:** macros should be tested for evaluation count and binding behavior, not just final happy-path result.



### E.16 Macro error reporting

A macro should reject invalid syntax early and clearly.

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro when-let ((var expr) &body body)
  (unless (symbolp var)
    (error "WHEN-LET binding variable must be a symbol, got ~S." var))
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     (when ,var
       ,@body)))
```

Invalid call:

```lisp
(when-let ((car x) (find-user id))
  ...)
```

Better error: “binding variable must be a symbol.”

Without validation, the macro may expand into invalid code and produce a confusing compiler error later.

| Macro syntax problem               | Good diagnostic                 |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Wrong number of binding elements   | Explain expected syntax         |
| Variable not a symbol              | Say which part must be a symbol |
| Unknown option                     | List accepted options           |
| Mutually exclusive options         | Explain conflict                |
| Invalid declaration/body placement | Explain grammar                 |
| Unsupported clause                 | Show allowed clauses            |
| Missing required clause            | Say which clause is required    |

Use `destructuring-bind` carefully. It can produce errors, but not always the most user-friendly ones.

More explicit validation may be better for public macros.

**Professional rule:** public macros should fail at expansion time with messages about the macro’s syntax, not later with obscure generated-code errors.



### E.17 Macro phase issues and `eval-when`

Macros run during macro expansion. If a macro calls helper functions during expansion, those helpers must be available at expansion time.

Example:

```lisp
(defun normalize-option-name (name)
  (intern (string-upcase name) :keyword))

(defmacro define-option (name)
  `(defparameter ,(normalize-option-name name) nil))
```

When compiling a file, `define-option` may need `normalize-option-name` available during compilation.

A phase-aware version:

```lisp
(eval-when (:compile-toplevel :load-toplevel :execute)
  (defun normalize-option-name (name)
    (intern (string-upcase name) :keyword)))

(defmacro define-option (name)
  `(defparameter ,(normalize-option-name name) nil))
```

Use `eval-when` sparingly and deliberately.

| Situation                                     | Phase concern                                    |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Macro used later in same file                 | Macro definition must be compile-time available  |
| Macro helper function called during expansion | Helper must be compile-time available            |
| Generated code calls helper at runtime        | Helper must be runtime available                 |
| Macro reads global registry                   | Registry must exist at expansion time            |
| Macro computes class/slot names               | Naming helper may need compile-time availability |

**Failure-first explanation:** runtime availability is not compile-time availability.

A function defined in source may exist when loaded, but the compiler may need it earlier while expanding macros.

**Professional rule:** keep macro expansion helpers small, explicit, and phase-aware.



### E.18 Macros and multiple values

Macros can accidentally discard or preserve multiple values depending on expansion.

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro with-logging (&body body)
  `(progn
     (format *trace-output* "Starting~%")
     ,@body
     (format *trace-output* "Done~%")))
```

This returns the value of the final `format`, not the body.

Better:

```lisp
(defmacro with-logging (&body body)
  `(progn
     (format *trace-output* "Starting~%")
     (multiple-value-prog1
         (progn ,@body)
       (format *trace-output* "Done~%"))))
```

`multiple-value-prog1` preserves all values of the first form.

Use this when body may return multiple values:

```lisp
(with-logging
  (floor 7 3))
```

Should return:

```lisp
2, 1
```

not just one value or the logging result.

| Macro pattern                                   | Multiple-value behavior                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `(progn ,@body)`                                | Returns multiple values of last body form                    |
| `(prog1 (progn ,@body) cleanup)`                | Usually preserves primary value only                         |
| `(multiple-value-prog1 (progn ,@body) cleanup)` | Preserves all body values                                    |
| `let` binding body result                       | Usually only primary value unless using multiple-value forms |
| `unwind-protect` protected form                 | Preserves protected form values                              |

**Professional rule:** if a macro wraps a body, decide whether multiple values must be preserved.



### E.19 Macros and declarations

Bodies may begin with declarations.

Example:

```lisp
(my-binding (x expr)
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

A macro that inserts body forms into a declaration-valid context may support declarations naturally.

Good:

```lisp
(defmacro with-value ((var expr) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     ,@body))
```

Call:

```lisp
(with-value (x 10)
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

The declaration is in a valid place.

But if the macro wraps body inside something where declarations are not at the beginning of the binding body, it can break.

Problematic:

```lisp
(defmacro with-value-logged ((var expr) &body body)
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     (format *trace-output* "Bound value.~%")
     ,@body))
```

Call:

```lisp
(with-value-logged (x 10)
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(let ((x 10))
  (format *trace-output* "Bound value.~%")
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  (+ x 1))
```

The declaration is no longer in a valid declaration position.

Solutions include:

1. Document that declarations are not supported.
2. Parse declarations from body.
3. Place logging after declarations.
4. Use helper utilities to split declarations and forms.

A simple manual approach is nontrivial and must be done carefully.

**Professional rule:** macros with bodies should consider whether body declarations are supported. If supported, preserve declaration position.



### E.20 Macros and DSL grammar

A macro DSL should have a clear grammar.

Bad:

```lisp
(query user
  active
  age > 18
  sort name)
```

Without grammar documentation, nobody knows what is valid.

Better grammar description:

```text
(with-query (var source)
  clause*)

clause =
  (where predicate-form)
  (select expression)
  (order-by expression &key descending)
  (limit integer)
```

Example:

```lisp
(with-query (user users)
  (where (active-user-p user))
  (where (> (user-age user) 18))
  (select (user-name user))
  (limit 10))
```

DSL macro design questions:

| Question                                    | Reason              |
| ------------------------------------------- | ------------------- |
| What forms are valid?                       | Grammar             |
| Which names are bound?                      | Scope               |
| Which forms are evaluated, and when?        | Semantics           |
| What does the DSL return?                   | API                 |
| How are errors reported?                    | Diagnostics         |
| Can clauses appear multiple times?          | Grammar             |
| Does clause order matter?                   | Semantics           |
| Are unknown clauses rejected?               | Safety              |
| Is expansion inspectable?                   | Debugging           |
| Could this be data instead of macro syntax? | Simpler alternative |

**Professional rule:** a DSL is a language. Even a small language needs grammar and error messages.



### E.21 Data DSL vs macro DSL

Not every DSL should be a macro. Sometimes data is better.

Data DSL:

```lisp
'(:and (:status :active)
       (:age-greater-than 18))
```

Interpreter:

```lisp
(defun evaluate-query (query object)
  ...)
```

Macro DSL:

```lisp
(with-query (user users)
  (where (eq (user-status user) :active))
  (where (> (user-age user) 18)))
```

| Factor                     | Data DSL                  | Macro DSL                        |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Runtime construction       | Easy                      | Harder                           |
| External input             | Safer if parsed/validated | Dangerous if treated as code     |
| Tooling                    | Data inspection           | Macro expansion                  |
| Binding syntax             | Weak                      | Strong                           |
| Compile-time optimization  | Less direct               | Strong                           |
| User-visible custom syntax | Limited                   | Strong                           |
| Security                   | Easier to control         | Code execution risk if untrusted |
| Debugging                  | Interpreter traces        | Macro expansion/debugger         |

Use a data DSL when the language represents external or runtime data.

Use a macro DSL when the abstraction needs lexical bindings, compile-time expansion, or integration with Lisp code syntax.

**Professional rule:** do not use macros to process untrusted external data. Use parsers and data interpreters.



### E.22 Macro checklist

Before accepting a macro, check:

| Check            | Question                                              |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Necessity        | Can this be a function?                               |
| Syntax           | Is the accepted grammar clear?                        |
| Expansion        | Is `macroexpand-1` understandable?                    |
| Evaluation count | How many times is each user form evaluated?           |
| Evaluation order | Is order predictable?                                 |
| Binding          | Which names are introduced?                           |
| Capture          | Are internal names `gensym`ed?                        |
| Package          | Which package do generated symbols belong to?         |
| Return values    | What values does the macro return?                    |
| Multiple values  | Are body multiple values preserved if needed?         |
| Declarations     | Are body declarations supported or rejected?          |
| Error reporting  | Are invalid macro calls diagnosed clearly?            |
| Phase            | Are expansion-time helpers available at compile time? |
| Recompilation    | Are dependent files recompiled after macro change?    |
| Tests            | Are evaluation count and capture tested?              |
| Documentation    | Does docstring describe syntax and semantics?         |

**Professional rule:** a macro should be reviewed more strictly than an ordinary function.



### E.23 Macro anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern                                  | Problem                      | Better alternative                             |
| --------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Macro for simple computation                  | Unnecessary phase complexity | Function                                       |
| Macro for ordinary callback                   | No syntax control needed     | Higher-order function                          |
| Macro duplicates subform accidentally         | Double evaluation            | `gensym` + `let`                               |
| Macro introduces fixed variable name          | Capture                      | User-supplied name or `gensym`                 |
| Macro hides large generated program           | Debugging hard               | Smaller functions + simpler macro              |
| Macro builds DSL without grammar              | Unclear valid syntax         | Document grammar                               |
| Macro uses `eval`                             | Phase/security confusion     | Generate code or use function/data interpreter |
| Macro expands into caller-package assumptions | Package bugs                 | Explicit symbols                               |
| Macro swallows errors                         | Bad diagnostics              | Expansion-time validation                      |
| Macro changes public syntax casually          | Source compatibility break   | Deprecation/migration                          |
| Macro handles external data as code           | Security risk                | Parser/data DSL                                |
| Macro has no tests                            | Hidden semantic bugs         | Behavior + expansion tests                     |



### E.24 Diagnostic table: macro bugs

| Symptom                                      | Likely cause                                    | Diagnostic                       |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Body runs twice                              | `,@body` appears twice                          | `macroexpand-1`                  |
| Argument side effect happens twice           | Subform inserted multiple times                 | `macroexpand-1`                  |
| Variable unexpectedly shadowed               | Capture                                         | Look for fixed generated names   |
| User variable not visible                    | Macro used `gensym` where user binding intended | Inspect binding syntax           |
| Wrong helper called                          | Package-symbol issue                            | Inspect expansion symbols        |
| Macro works in REPL but fails compiling file | Phase issue                                     | Check `eval-when` and ASDF order |
| Declaration ignored/error                    | Body declarations not preserved                 | Inspect expansion around body    |
| Return value is cleanup/log result           | Macro returns wrong final form                  | Inspect expansion                |
| Multiple values lost                         | Used `prog1`/`let` incorrectly                  | Test with `(floor 7 3)`          |
| Unknown syntax gives obscure compiler error  | No macro validation                             | Add expansion-time checks        |
| Macro change has no effect                   | Dependent code not recompiled                   | Recompile users                  |
| Expansion enormous/unreadable                | Macro doing too much                            | Move runtime work to functions   |



### E.25 Final macro decision tree

```text
SHOULD THIS BE A MACRO?
├── Do arguments evaluate normally before abstraction runs?
│   └── Yes -> use a function.
│
├── Does abstraction need to decide whether body runs?
│   └── Yes -> macro may be justified.
│
├── Does abstraction introduce caller-visible lexical bindings?
│   └── Yes -> macro may be justified.
│
├── Does abstraction wrap body with resource cleanup?
│   └── Yes -> macro is usually justified.
│
├── Does abstraction generate definitions?
│   └── Yes -> macro may be justified.
│
├── Does abstraction create source-level DSL?
│   └── Yes -> macro may be justified, but document grammar.
│
├── Is the input external/untrusted data?
│   └── Yes -> do not use macro/eval; use parser/data DSL.
│
└── If macro is still justified:
    ├── inspect expansion
    ├── control evaluation count
    ├── prevent capture
    ├── preserve return values
    ├── handle declarations if needed
    ├── test behavior
    └── document syntax
```

### E.26 Final compact macro template

A safe binding macro pattern:

```lisp
(defmacro when-let ((var expr) &body body)
  "Evaluate EXPR once. If non-NIL, bind VAR to it and evaluate BODY."
  (unless (symbolp var)
    (error "WHEN-LET requires a symbol variable, got ~S." var))
  `(let ((,var ,expr))
     (when ,var
       ,@body)))
```

A safe internal-temporary pattern:

```lisp
(defmacro square-once (expr)
  "Evaluate EXPR once and return its square."
  (let ((value (gensym "VALUE-")))
    `(let ((,value ,expr))
       (* ,value ,value))))
```

A safe resource macro pattern:

```lisp
(defmacro with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  "Bind VAR to an opened widget for SPEC during BODY, then close it."
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

A body-wrapper preserving multiple values:

```lisp
(defmacro with-log-message ((message) &body body)
  "Log MESSAGE before and after BODY, preserving BODY's values."
  `(progn
     (format *trace-output* "~&Start: ~A~%" ,message)
     (multiple-value-prog1
         (progn ,@body)
       (format *trace-output* "~&End: ~A~%" ,message))))
```

### E.27 Final rule

**Final conclusion:** macros are not “advanced functions.” They are source-to-source transformations with binding, phase, and evaluation consequences. Professional macro writing is conservative: use macros only when necessary, make expansion boring, preserve caller expectations, and test the semantic contract.

## Appendix F — CLOS Protocol Design and Method Combination Reference

### F.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **CLOS design reference**. Its purpose is not merely to list `defclass`, `defgeneric`, and `defmethod` syntax. It is meant to help decide when CLOS is the right abstraction, how to design a generic function as a protocol, how to reason about runtime dispatch, and how to avoid object-system overuse.

CLOS, the Common Lisp Object System, is not Java-style OOP transplanted into Lisp. Its center is the **generic function**: an operation to which methods are attached. Classes matter, but behavior is not owned by one receiver object in the same way as in many class-based languages.

**Core rule:** in CLOS, design the generic function protocol first. Methods implement that protocol for particular argument specializers.



### F.2 The basic CLOS vocabulary

| Term               | Meaning                                               | Example                                    |
| ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Class              | Runtime object type with slots and inheritance        | `user`, `document`                         |
| Instance           | Object created from a class                           | `(make-instance 'user ...)`                |
| Slot               | Field/storage associated with an instance             | `name`, `email`                            |
| Initarg            | Keyword accepted by `make-instance` or initialization | `:name`                                    |
| Reader             | Function that reads a slot                            | `user-name`                                |
| Writer             | Function that writes a slot                           | `(setf user-name)`                         |
| Accessor           | Reader plus writer                                    | `user-status`                              |
| Generic function   | Operation with methods                                | `render`, `serialize`                      |
| Method             | Implementation specialized for arguments              | `(defmethod render ((x user) stream) ...)` |
| Specializer        | Type/class/value constraint for method applicability  | `user`, `(eql :json)`                      |
| Multiple dispatch  | Dispatch on more than one argument                    | `(collide asteroid spaceship)`             |
| Method combination | How applicable methods are combined                   | primary, `:before`, `:after`, `:around`    |
| `call-next-method` | Continue to next less-specific method                 | layered behavior                           |
| Protocol           | Documented operation contract                         | return values, effects, conditions         |



### F.3 Minimal class, generic function, and method

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
   (email :initarg :email :reader user-email)))
```

```lisp
(defgeneric render (object stream)
  (:documentation "Render OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT."))
```

```lisp
(defmethod render ((object user) stream)
  (format stream "~A <~A>"
          (user-name object)
          (user-email object))
  object)
```

Use:

```lisp
(let ((u (make-instance 'user
                        :name "Ada"
                        :email "ada@example.com")))
  (render u *standard-output*))
```

| Form                  | Role                                                  |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `defclass`            | Defines data representation and class identity        |
| `defgeneric`          | Defines operation/protocol                            |
| `defmethod`           | Defines behavior for particular argument specializers |
| `make-instance`       | Constructs an instance                                |
| slot reader           | Observes slot value                                   |
| generic function call | Performs runtime dispatch                             |

**Important:** `(render u stream)` looks like an ordinary function call. The difference is that `render` is a generic function, so CLOS selects applicable methods at runtime.



### F.4 CLOS is operation-centered, not receiver-centered

In Java-style OOP, one often thinks:

```text
object.method(argument)
```

In CLOS, the natural model is:

```lisp
(operation object argument)
```

Example:

```lisp
(render object stream)
```

The operation is central. Methods specialize the operation.

This matters because CLOS supports multiple dispatch:

```lisp
(defgeneric collide (a b))

(defmethod collide ((a asteroid) (b spaceship))
  :asteroid-hits-spaceship)

(defmethod collide ((a spaceship) (b asteroid))
  :spaceship-hits-asteroid)
```

Both arguments participate in dispatch.

| Design model          | Question                                                        |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Receiver-centered OOP | “Which object owns this method?”                                |
| CLOS protocol design  | “Which operation is being specialized, and on which arguments?” |

**Professional rule:** name generic functions as operations, not as vague message buckets.

Bad:

```lisp
(defgeneric handle (x))
```

Better:

```lisp
(defgeneric render-to-stream (object stream)
  (:documentation "Write a textual representation of OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT."))
```



### F.5 When to use CLOS

Use CLOS when behavior should be extended by type, by class, or by independently added methods.

| Need                                   |  CLOS fit? | Reason                                             |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------: | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Stable record with fields only         |   Maybe no | `defstruct` may be simpler                         |
| Open polymorphic operation             |        Yes | New classes can add methods                        |
| Multiple dispatch                      |        Yes | CLOS handles more than one specialized argument    |
| Method layering                        |        Yes | `:before`, `:after`, `:around`, `call-next-method` |
| Public extension point                 |        Yes | Generic function protocol                          |
| Framework lifecycle hook               |        Yes | Methods implement phases                           |
| Simple finite symbolic state           | Usually no | `ecase` may be clearer                             |
| One-off local type branch              | Usually no | `typecase` may be enough                           |
| Private data with no behavior protocol | Usually no | structure, plist, or closure may suffice           |
| Numeric hot loop                       | Usually no | dispatch overhead may be unnecessary               |

**Decision rule:**

| If the alternatives are...                    | Prefer                                          |
| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Closed and local                              | `case`, `ecase`, `typecase`, ordinary functions |
| Open and extensible                           | generic functions and methods                   |
| Merely data fields                            | `defstruct` or class depending on needs         |
| Behavior depends on two or more runtime types | CLOS multiple dispatch                          |
| Behavior needs setup/post/wrapper hooks       | CLOS method combination                         |

**Failure-first explanation:** CLOS is not automatically more professional than functions.

Bad overdesign:

```lisp
(defclass adder ()
  ())

(defgeneric add-two (object x))

(defmethod add-two ((object adder) x)
  (+ x 2))
```

Better:

```lisp
(defun add-two (x)
  (+ x 2))
```

**Professional rule:** use CLOS when its runtime dispatch and protocol extensibility are actually needed.



### F.6 Class vs structure

Common Lisp has both `defstruct` and `defclass`.

| Need                                      |                                 Prefer `defstruct` | Prefer `defclass` |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------: | ----------------: |
| Lightweight record                        |                                                Yes |             Maybe |
| Simple fixed fields                       |                                                Yes |             Maybe |
| Constructor/accessor speed and simplicity |                                          Often yes |             Maybe |
| CLOS generic protocol                     |                                              Maybe |               Yes |
| Inheritance with CLOS classes             |                                                 No |               Yes |
| Redefinition flexibility                  |                                               Less |              More |
| Method dispatch on instances              | Works for structure classes too, but less flexible |           Natural |
| Slot options and initialization protocol  |                                            Limited |              Rich |
| Framework extension                       |                                                 No |               Yes |
| Metaobject/customization needs            |                                                 No |               Yes |

`defstruct`:

```lisp
(defstruct user
  name
  email)
```

`defclass`:

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
   (email :initarg :email :reader user-email)))
```

Use `defstruct` when the object is primarily a simple record.

Use `defclass` when object identity, extensibility, initialization protocol, inheritance, or CLOS methods are central.

**Common Pitfall**

A common mistake is creating a `defclass` for every record because of habits from Java or Python. In Common Lisp, `defstruct` is often the right tool for plain data.



### F.7 Slot options

A class slot can have several options.

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name
    :initarg :name
    :reader user-name
    :type string
    :documentation "The user's display name.")
   (email
    :initarg :email
    :accessor user-email
    :initform nil
    :documentation "The user's email address or NIL.")))
```

| Slot option             | Meaning                                |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| `:initarg`              | Keyword accepted during initialization |
| `:initform`             | Default value if not provided          |
| `:reader`               | Defines reader generic function        |
| `:writer`               | Defines writer function                |
| `:accessor`             | Defines reader and writer              |
| `:allocation :instance` | Each instance has its own slot value   |
| `:allocation :class`    | Slot value shared at class level       |
| `:type`                 | Declared slot type                     |
| `:documentation`        | Slot documentation                     |

Reader only:

```lisp
(name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
```

Accessor:

```lisp
(status :initarg :status :accessor user-status)
```

Then:

```lisp
(user-status user)
(setf (user-status user) :active)
```

**Design rule:** use `:reader` when mutation should not be public. Use `:accessor` only when mutation is part of the API.

Bad:

```lisp
(defclass account ()
  ((balance :initarg :balance :accessor account-balance)))
```

If arbitrary external mutation of `balance` violates invariants, do not export a public writer.

Better:

```lisp
(defclass account ()
  ((balance :initarg :balance :reader account-balance)))

(defun deposit (account amount)
  (check-type amount (real 0 *))
  (incf (slot-value account 'balance) amount)
  account)
```

Or use an internal accessor not exported.



### F.8 Initialization protocol

Basic construction:

```lisp
(make-instance 'user :name "Ada" :email "ada@example.com")
```

CLOS initialization can be customized with generic functions such as `initialize-instance`.

Example:

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
   (email :initarg :email :reader user-email)))

(defmethod initialize-instance :after ((object user) &key)
  (unless (plusp (length (user-name object)))
    (error "User name must not be empty.")))
```

Better public constructor wrapper:

```lisp
(defun make-user (&key name email)
  (unless (and (stringp name) (plusp (length name)))
    (error "Invalid user name: ~S" name))
  (unless (stringp email)
    (error "Invalid user email: ~S" email))
  (make-instance 'user :name name :email email))
```

| Initialization technique     | Use                                     |
| ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| `make-instance` directly     | Simple/internal construction            |
| Constructor wrapper          | Public validation and stable API        |
| `:initform`                  | Default slot values                     |
| `initialize-instance` method | Class-specific initialization hooks     |
| `shared-initialize`          | Advanced shared initialization protocol |
| Supplied-p keyword handling  | Required/optional distinction           |

**Professional rule:** public construction should enforce invariants. Do not expose raw initialization if it lets callers create invalid objects.



### F.9 Generic functions as protocols

A generic function should be treated as a protocol.

Poor protocol:

```lisp
(defgeneric process (x))
```

Better:

```lisp
(defgeneric encode-to-stream (object stream &key pretty)
  (:documentation
   "Encode OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT.
Methods should write an external representation of OBJECT to STREAM.
If PRETTY is true, methods may include whitespace for readability.
Methods may signal ENCODING-ERROR."))
```

A good generic function protocol documents:

| Protocol part                   | Question                                      |
| ------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Operation meaning               | What does this operation do?                  |
| Arguments                       | What does each argument represent?            |
| Dispatch arguments              | Which arguments may be specialized?           |
| Return values                   | What does the call return?                    |
| Side effects                    | Does it write, mutate, allocate, log, signal? |
| Conditions                      | What failures may be signaled?                |
| Method responsibilities         | What must each method guarantee?              |
| Extension policy                | May users define methods?                     |
| Method combination expectations | Should methods call `call-next-method`?       |
| Performance expectations        | Is this a hot path?                           |

Example:

```lisp
(defgeneric validate (object)
  (:documentation
   "Return true if OBJECT satisfies its domain invariants.
Methods should not mutate OBJECT.
Methods may signal a condition only for malformed objects that cannot be inspected safely."))
```

**Professional rule:** a generic function without a clear contract becomes dynamic dispatch without design.



### F.10 Method specializers

Methods can specialize parameters.

Class specializer:

```lisp
(defmethod render ((object user) stream)
  ...)
```

Built-in type/class specializer:

```lisp
(defmethod render ((object string) stream)
  (write-string object stream))
```

`eql` specializer:

```lisp
(defgeneric status-label (status))

(defmethod status-label ((status (eql :new)))
  "New")

(defmethod status-label ((status (eql :done)))
  "Done")
```

Multiple dispatch:

```lisp
(defgeneric convert (object format))

(defmethod convert ((object user) (format (eql :json)))
  ...)

(defmethod convert ((object user) (format (eql :text)))
  ...)
```

| Specializer kind                | Use                                          |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Class specializer               | Behavior for class instances                 |
| Built-in class/type specializer | Behavior for strings, numbers, streams, etc. |
| `eql` specializer               | Behavior for exact symbolic/sentinel values  |
| Unspecialized parameter         | Equivalent to `t` specializer                |
| Multiple specialized parameters | Multiple dispatch                            |

**Common Pitfall**

Using many `eql` methods for a simple closed finite state may be overkill:

```lisp
(defmethod status-label ((status (eql :new))) ...)
(defmethod status-label ((status (eql :done))) ...)
```

If the state set is closed and local, `ecase` is clearer:

```lisp
(defun status-label (status)
  (ecase status
    (:new "New")
    (:done "Done")))
```

**Professional rule:** use `eql` specializers when extension through method addition is valuable; use `ecase` for closed local symbolic dispatch.



### F.11 Multiple dispatch

Multiple dispatch is one of CLOS’s major strengths.

```lisp
(defgeneric merge-objects (left right))

(defmethod merge-objects ((left user) (right user))
  ...)

(defmethod merge-objects ((left user) (right group))
  ...)

(defmethod merge-objects ((left group) (right user))
  ...)
```

This avoids awkward receiver-centered code.

Without multiple dispatch, one might write:

```lisp
(defun merge-objects (left right)
  (typecase left
    (user
     (typecase right
       (user ...)
       (group ...)))
    (group
     (typecase right
       (user ...)
       (group ...)))))
```

That is closed and hard to extend.

| Situation                                 | Multiple dispatch fit      |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Binary operations                         | Strong                     |
| Type interaction matrix                   | Strong                     |
| Conversion by object and target format    | Strong                     |
| Serialization by object and stream/format | Strong                     |
| Collision/interaction logic               | Strong                     |
| Simple method on one object               | Single dispatch sufficient |
| Closed local finite matrix                | Maybe `ecase`/`typecase`   |

**Professional rule:** if behavior depends naturally on more than one argument’s runtime type, CLOS multiple dispatch may model the domain better than nested `typecase`.



### F.12 Method applicability and specificity

When a generic function is called, CLOS determines applicable methods and orders them by specificity.

Example:

```lisp
(defclass document () ())
(defclass article (document) ())

(defgeneric summarize (object))

(defmethod summarize ((object document))
  :document)

(defmethod summarize ((object article))
  :article)
```

Call:

```lisp
(summarize (make-instance 'article))
;; => :ARTICLE
```

The `article` method is more specific than the `document` method.

If no applicable method exists:

```lisp
(summarize 42)
```

there may be a no-applicable-method error.

You can define a default method:

```lisp
(defmethod summarize (object)
  :unknown)
```

This specializes on `t` implicitly.

| Method                | Specificity                  |
| --------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| `((object article))`  | More specific                |
| `((object document))` | Less specific                |
| `(object)`            | Default / specialized on `t` |

**Professional rule:** define default methods deliberately. A default method can make APIs friendlier, but it can also hide unsupported input.



### F.13 Method combination overview

CLOS method combination determines how applicable methods are combined.

Standard method kinds:

| Method kind        | Role                             |
| ------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Primary method     | Main behavior                    |
| `:before` method   | Runs before primary methods      |
| `:after` method    | Runs after primary methods       |
| `:around` method   | Wraps the whole call             |
| `call-next-method` | Continue to next method          |
| `next-method-p`    | Check whether next method exists |

Example:

```lisp
(defgeneric save (object))

(defmethod save :before ((object document))
  (validate object))

(defmethod save ((object document))
  (write-document object))

(defmethod save :after ((object document))
  (log-save object))
```

Call:

```lisp
(save document)
```

General standard order:

1. Most specific `:around` method runs first.
2. If it calls `call-next-method`, other around/primary combination proceeds.
3. `:before` methods run, most specific first.
4. Most specific primary method runs.
5. Primary methods may call `call-next-method`.
6. `:after` methods run, least specific first.
7. Return values usually come from the primary/around chain.

**Important:** an `:around` method can choose not to call `call-next-method`.

```lisp
(defmethod save :around ((object cached-document))
  (if (already-saved-p object)
      object
      (call-next-method)))
```

This may be correct for caching, but it must be intentional.

**Professional rule:** method combination is protocol-level control flow. Do not hide surprising business behavior in `:before`, `:after`, or `:around` methods.



### F.14 Primary methods and `call-next-method`

Primary methods can call less-specific primary methods.

```lisp
(defclass document () ())
(defclass report (document) ())

(defgeneric render (object stream))

(defmethod render ((object document) stream)
  (format stream "Document"))

(defmethod render ((object report) stream)
  (call-next-method)
  (format stream ": Report"))
```

Call with a `report`:

```lisp
(render report stream)
```

Output:

```text
Document: Report
```

`call-next-method` means: continue to the next applicable method according to method ordering.

You can test for a next method:

```lisp
(defmethod render ((object report) stream)
  (when (next-method-p)
    (call-next-method))
  ...)
```

| Use of `call-next-method`          | Meaning              |
| ---------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| Extend superclass/general behavior | Common               |
| Layer validation/rendering         | Common               |
| Reuse less-specific method         | Useful               |
| Required by protocol               | Should be documented |
| Skipped deliberately               | Must be intentional  |

**Common Pitfall**

A method that should layer behavior but forgets `call-next-method` silently replaces the general behavior.

**Professional rule:** if generic function methods are expected to cooperate through `call-next-method`, document that in the generic function protocol.



### F.15 `:before` methods

`:before` methods run before the primary method chain.

```lisp
(defmethod save :before ((object user))
  (validate-user object))
```

Use `:before` for:

| Good use                            | Reason                             |
| ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Validation                          | Precondition before main operation |
| Setup                               | Prepare object/context             |
| Logging/tracing                     | Non-return-affecting observation   |
| Authorization check                 | If part of protocol                |
| Cache invalidation before operation | If clearly documented              |

Avoid `:before` for hidden essential transformations that readers of the primary method cannot anticipate.

Example risk:

```lisp
(defmethod save :before ((object user))
  (normalize-user object))
```

This mutates the object before saving. That may be valid, but it should be part of the protocol.

**Return values:** `:before` method return values are ignored.

**Professional rule:** `:before` methods should enforce or prepare protocol behavior, not hide surprising core logic.



### F.16 `:after` methods

`:after` methods run after the primary method chain.

```lisp
(defmethod save :after ((object user))
  (log-save object))
```

Use `:after` for:

| Good use                       | Reason                          |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------- |
| Logging                        | Post-event observation          |
| Recording metrics              | Does not change main result     |
| Postcondition checks           | Verify operation result         |
| Notification                   | If protocol allows side effects |
| Cleanup-like secondary effects | When not resource-critical      |

Return values of `:after` methods are ignored.

Example:

```lisp
(defmethod render :after ((object report) stream)
  (declare (ignore stream))
  (record-render object))
```

**Caution:** resource cleanup should usually be handled with `unwind-protect`, not only `:after`, because errors and nonlocal exits need careful handling.

**Professional rule:** `:after` methods are good for post-effects, but not for determining the generic function’s main return value.



### F.17 `:around` methods

`:around` methods wrap the whole call.

```lisp
(defmethod save :around ((object persistent-object))
  (with-transaction ()
    (call-next-method)))
```

Use `:around` for:

| Good use            | Reason                                          |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Transaction wrapper | Body must be wrapped                            |
| Authorization gate  | May prevent operation                           |
| Caching             | May return cached result without primary method |
| Timing/profiling    | Wrap duration                                   |
| Dynamic binding     | Establish context around operation              |
| Error translation   | Convert lower-level conditions                  |
| Retry wrapper       | Control operation retry                         |

Example cache:

```lisp
(defmethod render :around ((object cached-renderable) stream)
  (let ((cached (cached-rendering object)))
    (if cached
        (write-string cached stream)
        (call-next-method))))
```

`around` methods can completely suppress primary methods. That makes them powerful and dangerous.

**Professional rule:** every `:around` method should make it obvious whether and when it calls `call-next-method`.



### F.18 Method combination design table

| Need                                 | Method kind                                      |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Main behavior                        | primary                                          |
| Validate before main behavior        | `:before`                                        |
| Log after success                    | `:after`                                         |
| Wrap with transaction                | `:around`                                        |
| Cache and maybe skip main behavior   | `:around`                                        |
| Extend less-specific behavior        | primary + `call-next-method`                     |
| Add non-return-affecting side effect | `:before` or `:after`                            |
| Change return value                  | primary or `:around`                             |
| Ensure cleanup on nonlocal exit      | usually `unwind-protect`, often inside `:around` |
| Enforce protocol precondition        | `:before` or primary                             |
| Compose superclass behavior          | `call-next-method`                               |



### F.19 Designing CLOS protocols

A CLOS protocol should be designed explicitly.

Protocol template:

```lisp
(defgeneric operation-name (arg1 arg2 &key option)
  (:documentation
   "One-sentence operation meaning.

Arguments:
- ARG1 ...
- ARG2 ...

Return values:
- ...

Side effects:
- ...

Conditions:
- ...

Extension:
- Users may define methods specializing ARG1.
- Methods should/should not call CALL-NEXT-METHOD.
- Methods should preserve ..."))
```

Example:

```lisp
(defgeneric encode-to-stream (object stream &key pretty)
  (:documentation
   "Encode OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT.

Methods should write a representation of OBJECT to STREAM.
If PRETTY is true, methods may include whitespace for readability.
Methods may signal ENCODING-ERROR.
User-defined methods may specialize OBJECT but should accept STREAM and PRETTY."))
```

**Protocol checklist:**

| Question                                                       |           Must be answered? |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------: |
| What does the operation mean?                                  |                         Yes |
| Which argument is the main extension point?                    |                         Yes |
| Are multiple arguments intended for dispatch?                  |                 If relevant |
| What does the call return?                                     |                         Yes |
| Does it mutate arguments?                                      |                         Yes |
| Does it write to streams or external resources?                |                         Yes |
| What conditions may be signaled?                               |                         Yes |
| May users define methods?                                      |    Yes for public protocols |
| Should methods call `call-next-method`?                        | If method layering expected |
| Are `:before`/`:after`/`:around` methods part of the protocol? |                     If used |
| Are keyword options stable?                                    |                         Yes |
| What happens for unsupported types?                            |                         Yes |

**Professional rule:** a public `defgeneric` without a docstring is usually underdesigned.



### F.20 Accessor design

Slot accessors are generic functions too.

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader user-name)
   (status :initarg :status :accessor user-status)))
```

This defines:

| Slot option                | Function                                                       |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `:reader user-name`        | `(user-name object)`                                           |
| `:writer (setf user-name)` | writer only if specified                                       |
| `:accessor user-status`    | `(user-status object)` and `(setf (user-status object) value)` |

Design questions:

| Question                                | Reader                                 | Writer/accessor |
| --------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | --------------- |
| Should callers observe this field?      | Export reader                          |                 |
| Should callers mutate this field?       | Export writer/accessor only if yes     |                 |
| Is the slot representation stable?      | If no, avoid direct accessor export    |                 |
| Are invariants needed on mutation?      | Use function/method, not raw writer    |                 |
| Is field computed rather than stored?   | Generic reader can hide implementation |                 |
| Should subclasses override observation? | Generic accessor may help              |                 |

Bad:

```lisp
(defclass account ()
  ((balance :initarg :balance :accessor account-balance)))
```

This lets external callers do:

```lisp
(setf (account-balance account) -1000)
```

Better:

```lisp
(defclass account ()
  ((balance :initarg :balance :reader account-balance)))

(defgeneric deposit (account amount))
(defgeneric withdraw (account amount))
```

**Professional rule:** exported writers are mutation APIs. Treat them as seriously as functions.



### F.21 Slot access and `slot-value`

`slot-value` accesses a slot by name.

```lisp
(slot-value object 'name)
```

Example:

```lisp
(defmethod normalize-user ((object user))
  (setf (slot-value object 'name)
        (string-downcase (slot-value object 'name)))
  object)
```

`slot-value` is sometimes useful internally, but public code should usually use accessors.

| Access style     | Use                                             |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Reader/accessor  | Public or normal internal API                   |
| `slot-value`     | Internal implementation, low-level manipulation |
| `with-slots`     | Convenient internal slot access                 |
| `with-accessors` | Convenient accessor-based access                |

`with-slots`:

```lisp
(defmethod normalize-user ((object user))
  (with-slots (name) object
    (setf name (string-downcase name)))
  object)
```

`with-accessors`:

```lisp
(defmethod normalize-user ((object user))
  (with-accessors ((name user-name)) object
    ...))
```

**Common Pitfall**

Using `slot-value` outside the defining package or protocol couples external code to representation.

**Professional rule:** use accessors as the abstraction boundary; use `slot-value` for internal representation work.



### F.22 Inheritance

Classes can inherit from superclasses.

```lisp
(defclass person ()
  ((name :initarg :name :reader person-name)))

(defclass employee (person)
  ((employee-id :initarg :employee-id :reader employee-id)))
```

An `employee` is also a `person`.

```lisp
(typep employee 'person)
;; => true
```

Methods on `person` apply to `employee` unless more specific methods exist.

```lisp
(defgeneric display-name (object))

(defmethod display-name ((object person))
  (person-name object))

(defmethod display-name ((object employee))
  (format nil "~A [~A]"
          (person-name object)
          (employee-id object)))
```

| Inheritance use                       |   Good fit? |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------: |
| Shared protocol/behavior              |         Yes |
| Shared slots with clear is-a relation |         Yes |
| Framework base class                  |         Yes |
| Merely reusing fields                 |    Maybe no |
| Deep taxonomy                         |       Risky |
| Avoiding composition                  | Usually bad |

**Professional rule:** use inheritance for semantic subtype relationships and protocol sharing, not merely for code reuse.



### F.23 Class precedence and specificity

When inheritance is involved, CLOS computes a class precedence list. More specific classes are considered before less specific ones.

```lisp
(class-precedence-list (class-of object))
```

Availability and exact output format are implementation/MOP-dependent, but conceptually every class has an ordering of itself and its superclasses.

Example:

```lisp
(defclass a () ())
(defclass b (a) ())
(defclass c (b) ())
```

A method specialized on `c` is more specific than one specialized on `b`, which is more specific than one specialized on `a`.

**Multiple inheritance** can make method ordering more complex.

```lisp
(defclass timestamped () ())
(defclass archived () ())
(defclass archived-document (document timestamped archived) ())
```

CLOS has rules for computing precedence. In complex cases, method specificity should be tested and documented.

**Professional rule:** avoid inheritance structures so complex that method selection becomes surprising.



### F.24 `no-applicable-method` and unsupported operations

If no method applies, CLOS invokes the generic function `no-applicable-method`.

Usually this leads to an error.

You can provide default behavior:

```lisp
(defmethod render (object stream)
  (format stream "~S" object)
  object)
```

Or signal a domain-specific condition:

```lisp
(define-condition unsupported-render-object (error)
  ((object :initarg :object :reader unsupported-render-object))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Cannot render object: ~S"
                     (unsupported-render-object condition)))))

(defmethod render (object stream)
  (declare (ignore stream))
  (error 'unsupported-render-object :object object))
```

| Unsupported behavior                           | Use when                                |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Let standard no-applicable-method error happen | Internal/simple code                    |
| Define default method                          | Meaningful fallback exists              |
| Signal domain condition                        | Public API needs structured failure     |
| Return `nil`                                   | Only if unsupported is expected absence |
| Provide restart                                | Caller may supply fallback              |

**Professional rule:** for public protocols, decide what unsupported types mean.



### F.25 `eql` specializer vs `case`

`eql` specializers allow dispatch on exact values.

```lisp
(defgeneric format-value (value format))

(defmethod format-value (value (format (eql :json)))
  ...)

(defmethod format-value (value (format (eql :text)))
  ...)
```

Alternative:

```lisp
(defun format-value (value format)
  (ecase format
    (:json ...)
    (:text ...)))
```

| Use `eql` specializer when          | Use `ecase` when                    |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| New formats may be added externally | Format set is closed                |
| Methods live near extensions        | Logic is local                      |
| Multiple dispatch matters           | One finite variable controls branch |
| Protocol is public/open             | Internal finite state               |
| Method combination useful           | Simple branch                       |

**Professional rule:** `eql` specializers are open dispatch. `ecase` is closed branch. Choose based on extension model.



### F.26 CLOS and conditions

Condition classes are part of CLOS-like class machinery.

```lisp
(define-condition validation-error (error)
  ((object :initarg :object :reader validation-error-object)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader validation-error-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Validation error: ~A"
                     (validation-error-reason condition)))))
```

Generic validation can signal conditions:

```lisp
(defgeneric validate (object)
  (:documentation "Return OBJECT if valid, otherwise signal VALIDATION-ERROR."))

(defmethod validate ((object user))
  (unless (stringp (user-name object))
    (error 'validation-error
           :object object
           :reason "User name must be a string."))
  object)
```

| CLOS + condition use               | Benefit                  |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| Condition classes                  | Structured errors        |
| Generic validators                 | Type-specific validation |
| Slot readers on conditions         | Programmatic handling    |
| Method dispatch on conditions      | Specialized handling     |
| Restarts around generic operations | Recovery protocols       |

**Professional rule:** public CLOS protocols should specify their condition behavior.



### F.27 CLOS and packages

CLOS protocols are part of package APIs.

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.render
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:render-to-stream
           #:rendering-error))
```

Public generic function:

```lisp
(defgeneric render-to-stream (object stream)
  (:documentation "Render OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT."))
```

External users may define methods if the protocol allows:

```lisp
(defmethod example.render:render-to-stream ((object my-object) stream)
  ...)
```

| Symbol                                  |    Export? | Reason                       |
| --------------------------------------- | ---------: | ---------------------------- |
| Generic function intended for extension |        Yes | Users need to define methods |
| Condition type users should handle      |        Yes | Structured failure           |
| Class intended for subclassing          |  Maybe yes | Extension point              |
| Slot accessors stable for callers       |  Maybe yes | Public observation           |
| Internal helper method                  |         No | Implementation detail        |
| Raw slot names                          | Usually no | Representation detail        |

**Common Pitfall**

If a generic function is not exported, external users may need `pkg::generic` to define methods. That indicates the extension protocol is not public.

**Professional rule:** export generic functions that are public extension points.



### F.28 CLOS and performance

Generic function dispatch has runtime cost. Implementations optimize it, but it is not free.

| Cost source              | Meaning                                     |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Generic dispatch         | Determine applicable method                 |
| Method combination       | Combine before/after/around/primary methods |
| Slot access              | Accessor call or slot lookup                |
| Allocation               | Instance creation                           |
| Dynamic type flexibility | Less static optimization                    |
| Class redefinition       | Runtime flexibility cost                    |
| Around methods           | Extra call layers                           |

Most application code should prioritize clarity. But in hot paths, consider:

| Hot-path issue                        | Possible response                              |
| ------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Generic call inside numeric loop      | Use ordinary function/local specialized helper |
| Many tiny objects allocated           | Use structures or arrays                       |
| Slot access in tight loop             | Declarations/implementation-aware optimization |
| Method combination too layered        | Simplify protocol                              |
| Type known statically inside function | Use specialized helper                         |
| Dispatch on simple closed state       | Use `ecase`                                    |

**Professional rule:** do not avoid CLOS because it has cost; avoid unnecessary CLOS in hot paths where dispatch is not semantically needed.



### F.29 CLOS design anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern                                          | Problem                        | Better pattern                            |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
| Class for every record                                | Overengineering                | `defstruct` or simple data                |
| Generic function named `process`                      | Vague protocol                 | Specific operation name                   |
| Methods with unrelated meanings under one generic     | Protocol collapse              | Separate generic functions                |
| Repeated `typecase` over same family                  | Closed extension bottleneck    | Generic function                          |
| `:around` method silently suppresses primary behavior | Hidden control flow            | Document or redesign                      |
