| Public accessor for invariant-sensitive slot          | External invalid mutation      | Reader + operation functions              |
| Deep inheritance tree                                 | Method specificity confusion   | Composition or flatter protocols          |
| Slot-value use in external code                       | Representation coupling        | Public accessors/protocol                 |
| No default unsupported behavior                       | Confusing no-applicable-method | Structured condition or default method    |
| Method lambda list not congruent with generic         | Protocol error                 | Fix `defgeneric`                          |
| Class without protocol                                | Data-only class                | `defstruct` or define meaningful generics |
| Generic dispatch for closed symbolic state            | Overkill                       | `ecase`                                   |



### F.30 CLOS diagnostic table

| Symptom                            | Likely cause                                     | Diagnostic                           |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| No applicable method               | Wrong class/type, missing method                 | Check `class-of`, method definitions |
| Method not called                  | More specific method absent or wrong specializer | Inspect `defmethod` specializers     |
| Unexpected method called           | Class precedence/specificity issue               | Inspect class hierarchy              |
| Around behavior skips main method  | `:around` did not call `call-next-method`        | Inspect around methods               |
| Validation happens invisibly       | Hidden `:before` method                          | Search method combination            |
| Return value unexpected            | `:after` ignored or `:around` changed return     | Inspect method kinds                 |
| Slot unbound                       | Missing initarg/initform or initialization bug   | Check slot options                   |
| Cannot initialize with keyword     | Missing `:initarg`                               | Check class slot definitions         |
| External mutation breaks invariant | Public `:accessor` exported                      | Restrict writer/export               |
| Method definition error            | Lambda list not congruent                        | Compare with `defgeneric`            |
| Generic function too broad         | Vague protocol                                   | Split operations                     |
| Typecase duplicated everywhere     | Missing open protocol                            | Introduce generic function           |

Useful runtime checks:

```lisp
(type-of object)
```

```lisp
(class-of object)
```

```lisp
(typep object 'some-class)
```

Implementation/editor tools can also inspect generic functions and methods.



### F.31 CLOS review checklist

Before accepting CLOS design, check:

| Check                 | Question                                                    |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Need                  | Is runtime polymorphism/extensibility actually needed?      |
| Representation        | Should this be `defstruct` instead?                         |
| Generic function name | Is the operation specific and meaningful?                   |
| Protocol docstring    | Are return values, side effects, and conditions documented? |
| Dispatch arguments    | Which arguments are meant to specialize?                    |
| Method congruence     | Do methods match generic function lambda list?              |
| Method combination    | Are `:before`, `:after`, `:around` justified?               |
| `call-next-method`    | Is method layering documented?                              |
| Accessors             | Are public readers/writers intentional?                     |
| Invariants            | Can invalid objects be created or mutated?                  |
| Unsupported cases     | Is no-method behavior acceptable?                           |
| Package exports       | Are public generics/classes/conditions exported?            |
| Extension policy      | May external users define methods/subclasses?               |
| Tests                 | Are dispatch cases and method combination tested?           |
| Performance           | Is dispatch acceptable in this path?                        |



### F.32 Final CLOS decision tree

```text
SHOULD THIS USE CLOS?
├── Is it just a simple computation?
│   └── Use a function.
│
├── Is it just a stable record?
│   └── Use DEFSTRUCT or a simple class if CLOS integration matters.
│
├── Is behavior closed and local?
│   └── Use CASE/ECASE/TYPECASE or ordinary functions.
│
├── Should new types add behavior without editing original code?
│   └── Use DEFGENERIC + DEFMETHOD.
│
├── Does behavior depend on multiple runtime argument types?
│   └── Use multiple dispatch.
│
├── Does operation need lifecycle layering?
│   └── Consider method combination.
│
├── Are callers allowed to extend this operation?
│   └── Export and document the generic function.
│
├── Are invariants important?
│   └── Use constructor wrappers, readers, controlled mutation, validation.
│
└── Is it in a hot path?
    └── Profile; simplify dispatch only if cost matters.
```

### F.33 Final compact CLOS template

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.render
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:render-to-stream
           #:rendering-error))
```

```lisp
(in-package #:example.render)

(define-condition rendering-error (error)
  ((object :initarg :object :reader rendering-error-object)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader rendering-error-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Cannot render ~S: ~A"
                     (rendering-error-object condition)
                     (rendering-error-reason condition)))))

(defgeneric render-to-stream (object stream &key pretty)
  (:documentation
   "Render OBJECT to STREAM and return OBJECT.

Methods should write a textual representation to STREAM.
If PRETTY is true, methods may include additional whitespace.
Methods may signal RENDERING-ERROR.
External users may define methods specializing OBJECT."))

(defmethod render-to-stream ((object string) stream &key pretty)
  (declare (ignore pretty))
  (write-string object stream)
  object)

(defmethod render-to-stream (object stream &key pretty)
  (declare (ignore pretty))
  (error 'rendering-error
         :object object
         :reason "No rendering method is defined."))
```

This template shows:

| Feature                   | Purpose                         |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| exported generic function | public protocol                 |
| condition type            | structured failure              |
| method for known type     | specialized behavior            |
| default method            | controlled unsupported behavior |
| docstring                 | extension contract              |

### F.34 Final rule

**Final conclusion:** CLOS is most powerful when treated as protocol design. A class is not automatically an architecture, and a method is not just a function with different syntax. The professional center of CLOS is the documented generic function: a stable operation, specialized by methods, extended under clear rules, and tested as a runtime dispatch protocol.

## Appendix G — Condition and Restart Pattern Catalog

### G.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **failure-handling and recovery-pattern catalog** for Common Lisp.

Common Lisp does not force every abnormal situation into one mechanism. A professional API chooses among several tools:

| Situation                                          | Usual mechanism                   |
| -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Expected absence                                   | `nil`                             |
| Absence where `nil` is valid data                  | multiple values                   |
| Invalid external input                             | condition                         |
| Internal invariant failure                         | `error` / `assert`                |
| Nonfatal abnormal situation                        | `warn`                            |
| Recoverable situation with caller-dependent policy | restart                           |
| Cleanup requirement                                | `unwind-protect` / `with-*` macro |
| Simple exception-like recovery                     | `handler-case`                    |
| Dynamic recovery or restart selection              | `handler-bind`                    |

**Core rule:** failure handling is API design. Do not choose `nil`, `error`, or restart casually.



### G.2 The condition-system vocabulary

| Term             | Meaning                                                   | Example                           |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Condition        | Object representing a situation                           | `invalid-config`, `missing-user`  |
| Error            | Serious condition normally entering debugger if unhandled | subclass of `error`               |
| Warning          | Nonfatal condition                                        | subclass of `warning`             |
| Signal           | Announce condition in dynamic context                     | `signal`, `error`, `warn`         |
| Handler          | Dynamically active function that responds to condition    | `handler-case`, `handler-bind`    |
| Restart          | Dynamically available recovery option                     | `use-value`, `retry`, `skip`      |
| `handler-case`   | Exit-style condition handling                             | catch and return fallback         |
| `handler-bind`   | Dynamic handler binding                                   | inspect condition, invoke restart |
| `restart-case`   | Establish named restarts around failing code              | offer recovery choices            |
| `invoke-restart` | Choose a restart programmatically                         | `(invoke-restart 'use-value 10)`  |
| Debugger         | Interactive condition/restart interface                   | inspect, choose restart           |

Condition object:

```lisp
(define-condition invalid-port (error)
  ((value :initarg :value :reader invalid-port-value))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid port: ~S"
                     (invalid-port-value condition)))))
```

Signal:

```lisp
(error 'invalid-port :value value)
```

Handle:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (parse-port input)
  (invalid-port (condition)
    (format *error-output* "~A~%" condition)
    nil))
```

**Professional rule:** condition reports are for humans; condition slots are for programs.



### G.3 Decision table: `nil`, multiple values, condition, restart

| Situation                                     | Use                                      | Reason                                      |
| --------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Lookup fails and `nil` cannot be valid result | return `nil`                             | Simple expected absence                     |
| Lookup fails and `nil` can be valid result    | return value + present-p                 | Avoid ambiguity                             |
| Hash table lookup                             | `gethash` multiple values                | Standard pattern                            |
| Parser fails with useful diagnostic           | signal condition                         | Caller needs reason                         |
| Parser failure is expected and frequent       | return values or condition depending API | Avoid conditions for hot normal control     |
| External input invalid                        | condition                                | Boundary needs explanation                  |
| Internal invariant impossible                 | `assert` or `error`                      | Programmer/system bug                       |
| Missing config may be supplied by caller      | condition + restart                      | Policy belongs higher up                    |
| Network operation may be retried              | condition + `retry` restart              | Recovery may be contextual                  |
| Batch item invalid but process can continue   | condition + `skip` restart               | Caller can choose policy                    |
| Deprecated option used                        | warning condition                        | Nonfatal but visible                        |
| Need cleanup no matter how exit occurs        | `unwind-protect`                         | Not an error mechanism, but boundary safety |
| Simple fallback on specific condition         | `handler-case`                           | Exit-style recovery                         |
| Handler should choose restart at signal point | `handler-bind`                           | Dynamic recovery                            |

**Core distinction:**

| Mechanism       | Means                              |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `nil`           | no value / false / empty / absent  |
| multiple values | primary result plus status/details |
| condition       | structured abnormal situation      |
| restart         | possible recovery action           |
| handler         | policy for responding              |



### G.4 Pattern 1 — Simple expected absence with `nil`

Use `nil` when absence is expected and unambiguous.

```lisp
(defun find-user (id users)
  (find id users :key #'user-id :test #'equal))
```

If no user is found, `find` returns `nil`.

Good when:

| Condition                   | Example                      |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Result type cannot be `nil` | users are objects, not `nil` |
| Absence is ordinary         | search operation             |
| Caller can branch simply    | `(if user ...)`              |
| No diagnostic needed        | missing is expected          |

Use:

```lisp
(let ((user (find-user id users)))
  (if user
      (notify user)
      (format t "No such user.~%")))
```

**Do not use bare `nil` if different failure reasons matter.**

Bad:

```lisp
(defun parse-user (data)
  (when (valid-user-data-p data)
    (make-user data)))
```

This returns `nil` for all invalid input reasons.

**Professional rule:** return `nil` only when “not present” is the only information the caller needs.



### G.5 Pattern 2 — Presence-sensitive lookup with multiple values

Use multiple values when `nil` can be a valid result.

Standard example:

```lisp
(gethash key table)
;; => value, present-p
```

Example:

```lisp
(let ((table (make-hash-table)))
  (setf (gethash :debug table) nil)
  (multiple-value-bind (value present-p)
      (gethash :debug table)
    (list value present-p)))
;; => (NIL T)
```

Custom lookup:

```lisp
(defun alist-lookup (key alist &key (test #'eql))
  (let ((pair (assoc key alist :test test)))
    (if pair
        (values (cdr pair) t)
        (values nil nil))))
```

Use:

```lisp
(multiple-value-bind (value present-p)
    (alist-lookup :debug settings)
  (if present-p
      value
      :default))
```

Good when:

| Condition                             | Example                         |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| `nil` is valid value                  | `:debug nil`                    |
| Caller often only needs primary value | multiple values are lightweight |
| Status is secondary                   | value + found-p                 |
| No rich error object needed           | lookup, cache, parse maybe      |

**Professional rule:** use multiple values for value + status; use conditions for abnormal diagnostic failure.



### G.6 Pattern 3 — Sentinel value for internal absence

A sentinel is a unique marker that cannot be confused with domain values.

```lisp
(defparameter *missing* (gensym "MISSING-"))
```

Example:

```lisp
(defun get-setting-or-missing (key table)
  (multiple-value-bind (value present-p)
      (gethash key table)
    (if present-p
        value
        *missing*)))
```

Use:

```lisp
(let ((value (get-setting-or-missing :timeout table)))
  (if (eq value *missing*)
      :not-found
      value))
```

Good when:

| Situation                                 | Why sentinel works          |
| ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Internal algorithm needs one return value | Simpler local control       |
| Sentinel cannot occur as valid data       | Unique `gensym` object      |
| Public API still wraps result properly    | Avoid exposing weird marker |

Bad public API:

```lisp
(defun find-user-or-missing (id)
  *missing*)
```

External callers now must know the sentinel.

Better public API:

```lisp
(defun find-user (id)
  (values user found-p))
```

**Professional rule:** sentinels are good internal tools; public APIs usually prefer multiple values or conditions.



### G.7 Pattern 4 — Structured public error with `define-condition`

Use condition classes when callers need to handle failures programmatically.

```lisp
(define-condition invalid-config (error)
  ((path :initarg :path :reader invalid-config-path)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader invalid-config-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid config file ~S: ~A"
                     (invalid-config-path condition)
                     (invalid-config-reason condition)))))
```

Signal:

```lisp
(error 'invalid-config
       :path path
       :reason "Missing :host.")
```

Handle:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (load-config path)
  (invalid-config (condition)
    (format *error-output* "~A~%" condition)
    nil))
```

Good when:

| Situation                           | Why condition                        |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Public API failure                  | Callers can handle by condition type |
| Failure has structured data         | Slots expose path/reason/value       |
| Diagnostic matters                  | Report explains human message        |
| Several failure types exist         | Condition hierarchy                  |
| Tests should check failure contract | Condition type and slots             |

**Avoid string-only public errors:**

```lisp
(error "Invalid config.")
```

This is acceptable for quick internal code, but weak as an API.

**Professional rule:** for handleable public failures, define condition classes with slot readers.



### G.8 Pattern 5 — Condition hierarchy

Condition inheritance lets callers handle broad or narrow classes of failures.

```lisp
(define-condition application-error (error)
  ()
  (:documentation "Base condition for application-level errors."))

(define-condition config-error (application-error)
  ()
  (:documentation "Base condition for configuration errors."))

(define-condition missing-config-key (config-error)
  ((key :initarg :key :reader missing-config-key))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Missing config key: ~S"
                     (missing-config-key condition)))))

(define-condition invalid-config-value (config-error)
  ((key :initarg :key :reader invalid-config-value-key)
   (value :initarg :value :reader invalid-config-value))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid config value for ~S: ~S"
                     (invalid-config-value-key condition)
                     (invalid-config-value condition)))))
```

Broad handler:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (load-config path)
  (config-error (condition)
    (format *error-output* "Configuration failed: ~A~%" condition)))
```

Narrow handler:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (load-config path)
  (missing-config-key (condition)
    (use-default-for (missing-config-key condition))))
```

| Hierarchy level        | Use                    |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------- |
| `application-error`    | All app-level errors   |
| `config-error`         | All config failures    |
| `missing-config-key`   | Specific missing key   |
| `invalid-config-value` | Specific invalid value |

**Professional rule:** create hierarchies when callers need both broad and specific handling. Do not create deep taxonomies without handling value.



### G.9 Pattern 6 — Warnings for nonfatal abnormal situations

Use `warn` when something is abnormal but execution can continue.

```lisp
(warn "Deprecated option: ~S" option)
```

Structured warning:

```lisp
(define-condition deprecated-option (warning)
  ((option :initarg :option :reader deprecated-option-name))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Deprecated option: ~S"
                     (deprecated-option-name condition)))))
```

Signal:

```lisp
(warn 'deprecated-option :option :old-timeout)
```

Muffle warning:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((deprecated-option
       (lambda (condition)
         (declare (ignore condition))
         (muffle-warning))))
  (load-config config))
```

Good uses:

| Use                          | Example                                      |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Deprecated option            | old config key still accepted                |
| Suspicious fallback          | using default because optional field missing |
| Nonfatal compatibility issue | old syntax accepted                          |
| Performance warning          | unusual but allowed path                     |
| Data quality warning         | recoverable irregularity                     |

Bad uses:

| Bad use                        | Better                     |
| ------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| Fatal invalid input            | `error` condition          |
| Frequent loop logging          | rate-limit/log differently |
| Silent warning text to stream  | `warn` condition           |
| Warning as hidden control flow | explicit status/condition  |

**Professional rule:** warning conditions are structured nonfatal signals. Prefer them to ad hoc printing for nonfatal abnormal situations.



### G.10 Pattern 7 — `handler-case` for exit-style recovery

`handler-case` handles a condition by exiting the protected form.

```lisp
(handler-case
    (parse-port input)
  (invalid-port (condition)
    (format *error-output* "~A~%" condition)
    nil))
```

Pattern:

```lisp
(handler-case
    protected-form
  (condition-type (condition-variable)
    recovery-body))
```

Example:

```lisp
(defun safe-parse-port (input)
  (handler-case
      (parse-port input)
    (invalid-port (condition)
      (declare (ignore condition))
      nil)))
```

Good when:

| Situation                         | Why `handler-case`             |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Need simple fallback              | return `nil` or default        |
| Protected computation should stop | exit-style handling            |
| Specific condition type           | narrow recovery                |
| Local boundary                    | parse one value, load one file |

Bad broad handling:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (run-entire-application)
  (error (condition)
    nil))
```

This catches too much and hides bugs.

**Professional rule:** use `handler-case` for narrow, exit-style recovery from specific condition types.



### G.11 Pattern 8 — `handler-bind` for dynamic handling and restart selection

`handler-bind` installs handlers dynamically. It does not automatically exit the protected form. A handler may inspect the condition and invoke a restart.

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((missing-config-key
       (lambda (condition)
         (declare (ignore condition))
         (invoke-restart 'use-default))))
  (load-config path))
```

Pattern:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((condition-type handler-function))
  body...)
```

Good when:

| Situation                                    | Why `handler-bind`            |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Need to invoke restarts                      | Handler acts at signal point  |
| Need dynamic policy                          | Higher layer chooses recovery |
| Need observe warnings                        | Log/muffle/transform          |
| Need handle without unwinding immediately    | Restart or continue           |
| Need debugger-like recovery programmatically | Dynamic protocol              |

Bad:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((error (lambda (c)
              (format t "Error: ~A~%" c))))
  (dangerous-operation))
```

The handler prints and returns, but does not necessarily recover. The condition may continue to debugger.

Correct recovery requires a restart or nonlocal transfer.

**Professional rule:** use `handler-bind` when the handler participates in dynamic recovery, especially by invoking a restart.



### G.12 Pattern 9 — Restart with `use-value`

A `use-value` restart lets a caller supply a replacement value.

```lisp
(define-condition missing-setting (error)
  ((key :initarg :key :reader missing-setting-key))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Missing setting: ~S"
                     (missing-setting-key condition)))))
```

Function offering restart:

```lisp
(defun required-setting (settings key)
  (multiple-value-bind (value present-p)
      (gethash key settings)
    (if present-p
        value
        (restart-case
            (error 'missing-setting :key key)
          (use-value (value)
            :report "Use a supplied value."
            value)))))
```

Caller policy:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((missing-setting
       (lambda (condition)
         (when (eq (missing-setting-key condition) :timeout)
           (invoke-restart 'use-value 30)))))
  (required-setting settings :timeout))
```

Good when:

| Situation              | Example                    |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Missing config value   | supply default dynamically |
| Missing object         | provide substitute         |
| Invalid parsed value   | use corrected value        |
| Interactive correction | debugger asks user         |

**Professional rule:** `use-value` is appropriate when the computation can continue with a substitute value.



### G.13 Pattern 10 — Restart with `retry`

A `retry` restart lets the operation be attempted again.

```lisp
(define-condition transient-service-error (error)
  ((service :initarg :service :reader transient-service-error-service))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Transient service failure: ~A"
                     (transient-service-error-service condition)))))
```

Function:

```lisp
(defun fetch-with-restart (service request)
  (restart-case
      (fetch service request)
    (retry ()
      :report "Retry the fetch operation."
      (fetch-with-restart service request))))
```

Caller policy:

```lisp
(let ((attempts 0))
  (handler-bind
      ((transient-service-error
         (lambda (condition)
           (declare (ignore condition))
           (when (< attempts 3)
             (incf attempts)
             (invoke-restart 'retry)))))
    (fetch-with-restart service request)))
```

Good when:

| Situation                      | Example            |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------ |
| Transient network failure      | retry request      |
| Temporary file lock            | retry open         |
| External service unavailable   | retry with backoff |
| Race condition around resource | retry operation    |

**Caution:** retry can loop forever if policy is careless.

**Professional rule:** `retry` restarts should be paired with a clear retry policy.



### G.14 Pattern 11 — Restart with `skip`

A `skip` restart is useful in batch processing.

```lisp
(define-condition invalid-record (error)
  ((record :initarg :record :reader invalid-record-record)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader invalid-record-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid record ~S: ~A"
                     (invalid-record-record condition)
                     (invalid-record-reason condition)))))
```

Processor:

```lisp
(defun process-record (record)
  (restart-case
      (progn
        (unless (valid-record-p record)
          (error 'invalid-record
                 :record record
                 :reason "Record failed validation."))
        (store-record record))
    (skip ()
      :report "Skip this record."
      nil)))
```

Batch:

```lisp
(defun process-records (records)
  (dolist (record records)
    (handler-bind
        ((invalid-record
           (lambda (condition)
             (format *error-output* "Skipping: ~A~%" condition)
             (invoke-restart 'skip))))
      (process-record record))))
```

Good when:

| Situation       | Example               |
| --------------- | --------------------- |
| Batch import    | skip invalid row      |
| File processing | skip unreadable file  |
| Test discovery  | skip unsupported test |
| Optional plugin | skip failing plugin   |

**Professional rule:** `skip` is good when one unit can fail without invalidating the whole operation.



### G.15 Pattern 12 — Restart with `store-value`

`store-value` both supplies and records a corrected value.

```lisp
(defun setting-value (settings key)
  (multiple-value-bind (value present-p)
      (gethash key settings)
    (if present-p
        value
        (restart-case
            (error 'missing-setting :key key)
          (store-value (value)
            :report "Store a supplied value and continue."
            (setf (gethash key settings) value)
            value)
          (use-value (value)
            :report "Use a supplied value once."
            value)))))
```

Caller:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((missing-setting
       (lambda (condition)
         (when (eq (missing-setting-key condition) :timeout)
           (invoke-restart 'store-value 30)))))
  (setting-value settings :timeout))
```

Difference:

| Restart       | Effect                      |
| ------------- | --------------------------- |
| `use-value`   | Use substitute now          |
| `store-value` | Store substitute and use it |
| `use-default` | Use known default           |
| `retry`       | Try operation again         |

**Professional rule:** use `store-value` when correction should mutate the source of future lookups.



### G.16 Pattern 13 — `with-simple-restart`

`with-simple-restart` establishes a restart with a simple report.

```lisp
(with-simple-restart (skip "Skip this operation.")
  (dangerous-operation))
```

If an error occurs inside, the debugger may show a `skip` restart.

Example:

```lisp
(defun process-file (path)
  (with-simple-restart (skip-file "Skip file ~S." path)
    (read-and-process-file path)))
```

This does not handle errors by itself. It offers a restart.

Handler may invoke it:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((file-error
       (lambda (condition)
         (declare (ignore condition))
         (invoke-restart 'skip-file))))
  (process-file path))
```

Good when:

| Situation                             | Why                     |
| ------------------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Need simple restart with no arguments | Compact                 |
| Debugger recovery option              | Human can choose        |
| Batch skip/continue                   | Handler can invoke      |
| Wrapper-level recovery option         | No complex restart body |

**Professional rule:** `with-simple-restart` offers recovery; it does not itself catch or fix the condition.



### G.17 Pattern 14 — `ignore-errors`

`ignore-errors` catches conditions of type `error` and returns two values:

```lisp
(ignore-errors
  (parse-integer input))
```

Return values:

| Case    | Values                |
| ------- | --------------------- |
| Success | result values of body |
| Error   | `nil`, condition      |

Example:

```lisp
(multiple-value-bind (value condition)
    (ignore-errors (parse-integer "abc"))
  (list value condition))
```

Good use:

```lisp
(defun maybe-parse-integer (string)
  (ignore-errors
    (parse-integer string :junk-allowed nil)))
```

But be careful:

```lisp
(ignore-errors
  (run-large-operation))
```

This may hide programming errors.

| Use `ignore-errors` when            | Avoid when                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Small optional operation            | Large critical region          |
| Failure is expected and unimportant | Diagnostics matter             |
| You inspect second value if needed  | You silently discard condition |
| Boundary wrapper                    | Internal bug debugging         |

**Professional rule:** `ignore-errors` is convenient but blunt. Prefer `handler-case` with specific condition types for public or serious code.



### G.18 Pattern 15 — `assert` for invariants

Use `assert` for internal assumptions that should hold if the program is correct.

```lisp
(defun percent (part whole)
  (assert (plusp whole) (whole)
          "WHOLE must be positive, got ~S." whole)
  (* 100 (/ part whole)))
```

`assert` can be interactive and may offer correction.

Good for:

| Situation                | Example                       |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
| Internal invariant       | list should already be sorted |
| Impossible branch        | state machine invariant       |
| Debug-time assumption    | algorithm precondition        |
| Development sanity check | data structure integrity      |

Not ideal for:

| Situation                                        | Better                    |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------- |
| External input validation                        | condition with clear type |
| Public API error                                 | structured condition      |
| Normal business rule failure                     | predicate/condition       |
| Performance-critical inner loop after validation | declarations maybe        |

**Professional rule:** use `assert` for “this should never be false if the program is correct,” not for ordinary user input validation.



### G.19 Pattern 16 — `check-type` for type correction

`check-type` checks that a place has a value of a given type and may offer interactive correction.

```lisp
(defun parse-port (value)
  (check-type value (integer 1 65535))
  value)
```

Good for simple local type/domain checks.

But for public APIs, a custom condition may be better if callers need structured data.

Custom condition version:

```lisp
(define-condition invalid-port (error)
  ((value :initarg :value :reader invalid-port-value))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid port: ~S"
                     (invalid-port-value condition)))))

(defun parse-port (value)
  (unless (typep value '(integer 1 65535))
    (error 'invalid-port :value value))
  value)
```

| Use `check-type` when               | Use custom condition when     |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Simple programmer-facing validation | Public handleable error       |
| Interactive correction useful       | Need condition slots          |
| Type specifier captures rule        | Domain error richer than type |
| Quick internal check                | Stable API contract           |

**Professional rule:** `check-type` is useful, but public domain validation often deserves explicit condition classes.



### G.20 Pattern 17 — Translating lower-level conditions

Boundary code may catch lower-level conditions and signal application-level conditions.

```lisp
(define-condition config-load-error (error)
  ((path :initarg :path :reader config-load-error-path)
   (cause :initarg :cause :reader config-load-error-cause))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Could not load config ~S: ~A"
                     (config-load-error-path condition)
                     (config-load-error-cause condition)))))
```

Wrapper:

```lisp
(defun load-config-file (path)
  (handler-case
      (with-open-file (stream path :direction :input)
        (read-config stream))
    (file-error (condition)
      (error 'config-load-error
             :path path
             :cause condition))))
```

Good when:

| Situation                                | Why translate            |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| External library condition too low-level | App API should be stable |
| Need add context                         | path/request/user ID     |
| Need hide implementation detail          | Library may change       |
| Public API needs own condition hierarchy | Clean handling           |

**Professional rule:** translate low-level conditions at subsystem boundaries, preserving the cause when useful.



### G.21 Pattern 18 — Cleanup with `unwind-protect`

Cleanup is related to failure handling but not the same thing.

```lisp
(let ((resource (open-resource)))
  (unwind-protect
       (use-resource resource)
    (close-resource resource)))
```

Cleanup runs on normal and nonlocal exits.

Good for:

| Resource               | Cleanup         |
| ---------------------- | --------------- |
| File stream            | close           |
| Lock                   | release         |
| Transaction            | commit/rollback |
| Foreign pointer        | free            |
| Temporary file         | delete          |
| Dynamic external state | restore         |

Resource macro:

```lisp
(defmacro with-open-widget ((var spec) &body body)
  `(let ((,var (open-widget ,spec)))
     (unwind-protect
          (progn ,@body)
       (close-widget ,var))))
```

**Professional rule:** do not rely on handlers for cleanup. Use `unwind-protect` or `with-*` resource macros.



### G.22 Pattern 19 — Conditions plus cleanup

Condition handling and cleanup often appear together.

```lisp
(defun process-file (path)
  (with-open-file (stream path :direction :input)
    (handler-case
        (process-stream stream)
      (invalid-record (condition)
        (format *error-output* "Invalid record: ~A~%" condition)
        nil))))
```

Here:

| Mechanism        | Role                        |
| ---------------- | --------------------------- |
| `with-open-file` | cleanup resource            |
| `handler-case`   | recover from invalid record |
| condition object | structured error            |

Do not replace resource cleanup with condition handling:

Bad:

```lisp
(let ((stream (open path)))
  (handler-case
      (process-stream stream)
    (error ()
      (close stream))))
```

This may fail to close on normal success.

Better:

```lisp
(let ((stream (open path)))
  (unwind-protect
       (process-stream stream)
    (close stream)))
```

**Professional rule:** cleanup must be guaranteed independently of which condition path is taken.



### G.23 Pattern 20 — Restarts as public recovery protocol

Restarts can be part of a documented API.

Example protocol:

```lisp
(defgeneric resolve-user (id)
  (:documentation
   "Return the user for ID.

If no user is found, signals MISSING-USER.
While signaling MISSING-USER, the following restarts may be available:
- USE-VALUE: provide a user object.
- CREATE-USER: create and return a new user.
- SKIP: return NIL."))
```

Implementation:

```lisp
(define-condition missing-user (error)
  ((id :initarg :id :reader missing-user-id))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Missing user: ~S"
                     (missing-user-id condition)))))

(defun resolve-user (id)
  (or (find-user id)
      (restart-case
          (error 'missing-user :id id)
        (use-value (user)
          :report "Use a supplied user."
          user)
        (create-user ()
          :report "Create a new user."
          (make-user :id id))
        (skip ()
          :report "Return NIL."
          nil))))
```

**Documentation matters.** Programmatic callers need to know restart names and arguments.

**Professional rule:** if restarts are part of public behavior, document their names, arguments, and effects.



### G.24 Condition testing

Tests should check condition type and slots, not just message strings.

Bad:

```lisp
(is (search "Invalid port"
            (princ-to-string condition)))
```

Better:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (parse-port 99999)
  (invalid-port (condition)
    (is (= 99999 (invalid-port-value condition)))))
```

Testing warnings:

```lisp
(let ((warned nil))
  (handler-bind
      ((deprecated-option
         (lambda (condition)
           (setf warned (deprecated-option-name condition))
           (muffle-warning))))
    (load-config config))
  (is (eq warned :old-timeout)))
```

Testing restarts:

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((missing-setting
       (lambda (condition)
         (declare (ignore condition))
         (invoke-restart 'use-value 30))))
  (is (= 30 (required-setting settings :timeout))))
```

| Test target    | Check                                 |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Condition type | handler catches correct type          |
| Slot data      | readers return expected data          |
| Report         | optional human-facing smoke test      |
| Warning        | handler sees warning condition        |
| Restart        | handler can invoke restart            |
| Fallback       | `handler-case` returns expected value |
| Cleanup        | resource released after error         |

**Professional rule:** condition and restart behavior is API behavior and should be tested.



### G.25 Anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern                                | Problem                              | Better                       |
| ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------- |
| Return `nil` for every failure              | No diagnostics                       | Multiple values or condition |
| Catch broad `error` around huge block       | Hides bugs                           | Narrow condition and scope   |
| Print error and continue                    | Caller cannot react                  | Signal/handle condition      |
| Parse report string                         | Fragile human text                   | Read condition slots         |
| Use conditions for common fast path absence | Overhead and semantic noise          | `nil` or multiple values     |
| Use restarts where simple default is enough | Overengineering                      | Local default                |
| Handler prints but does not recover         | Condition still unresolved           | Invoke restart or exit       |
| Cleanup only in error handler               | Normal path leaks                    | `unwind-protect`             |
| String-only public errors                   | Not type-handleable                  | `define-condition`           |
| Undocumented restart names                  | Programmatic callers cannot use them | Protocol docs                |
| `ignore-errors` over large operation        | Swallows real bugs                   | Specific `handler-case`      |
| Warning spam                                | Users ignore warnings                | Rate-limit or aggregate      |



### G.26 Complete condition/restart design checklist

| Check                | Question                                                                        |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Situation category   | Absence, invalid input, invariant failure, external failure, warning, recovery? |
| `nil` ambiguity      | Can `nil` be valid data?                                                        |
| Multiple values      | Is status secondary to primary result?                                          |
| Condition class      | Do callers need structured handling?                                            |
| Condition hierarchy  | Are broad and narrow handlers useful?                                           |
| Slots                | Does condition carry machine-readable data?                                     |
| Report               | Is human message clear?                                                         |
| Warning              | Is the situation nonfatal but abnormal?                                         |
| Handler style        | Should handling exit or invoke restart?                                         |
| Restart              | Is there a meaningful way to continue?                                          |
| Restart name         | Is the recovery action concrete?                                                |
| Restart arguments    | Are they documented?                                                            |
| Cleanup              | Is `unwind-protect` needed?                                                     |
| Boundary translation | Should low-level condition be wrapped?                                          |
| Tests                | Are condition slots and restarts tested?                                        |
| Scope                | Is the handler region narrow enough?                                            |



### G.27 Final decision tree

```text
FAILURE OR ABSENCE?
├── Is this ordinary expected absence?
│   ├── Can NIL be a valid value?
│   │   ├── Yes -> return value + present-p
│   │   └── No -> return NIL
│
├── Is this invalid external input?
│   └── Signal structured condition.
│
├── Is this an internal invariant violation?
│   └── Use ASSERT or ERROR.
│
├── Is this nonfatal but abnormal?
│   └── Signal warning condition with WARN.
│
├── Can computation continue with caller-supplied policy?
│   └── Signal condition inside RESTART-CASE.
│       ├── substitute value -> USE-VALUE
│       ├── try again -> RETRY
│       ├── skip current unit -> SKIP
│       ├── store corrected value -> STORE-VALUE
│       └── use known default -> USE-DEFAULT
│
├── Does caller simply need fallback after failure?
│   └── HANDLER-CASE with specific condition.
│
├── Does caller need to choose a restart dynamically?
│   └── HANDLER-BIND + INVOKE-RESTART.
│
└── Does any resource need cleanup?
    └── Use UNWIND-PROTECT or WITH-* macro regardless of error handling.
```

### G.28 Final compact templates

#### Structured error template

```lisp
(define-condition invalid-value (error)
  ((value :initarg :value :reader invalid-value-value)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader invalid-value-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid value ~S: ~A"
                     (invalid-value-value condition)
                     (invalid-value-reason condition)))))
```

#### Signal template

```lisp
(error 'invalid-value
       :value value
       :reason "Expected a positive integer.")
```

#### Simple handler template

```lisp
(handler-case
    (operation)
  (invalid-value (condition)
    (format *error-output* "~A~%" condition)
    nil))
```

#### Restart template

```lisp
(restart-case
    (error 'missing-value :key key)
  (use-value (value)
    :report "Use a supplied value."
    value)
  (use-default ()
    :report "Use the default value."
    default))
```

#### Handler invoking restart template

```lisp
(handler-bind
    ((missing-value
       (lambda (condition)
         (declare (ignore condition))
         (invoke-restart 'use-default))))
  (operation))
```

#### Cleanup template

```lisp
(let ((resource (acquire-resource)))
  (unwind-protect
       (use-resource resource)
    (release-resource resource)))
```

### G.29 Final rule

**Final conclusion:** Common Lisp failure handling is not a single exception mechanism. It is a layered toolkit: `nil` for simple absence, multiple values for presence-sensitive results, conditions for structured abnormal situations, handlers for policy, restarts for recovery, and `unwind-protect` for cleanup. Professional code chooses the weakest mechanism that preserves the semantic distinction the caller needs.

## Appendix H — ASDF, Package, and Project Template

### H.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **copyable project-structure reference** for Common Lisp.

The goal is to make a project loadable, testable, readable, and maintainable from a fresh image. It separates five things that beginners often confuse:

| Concept     | Controls                                | Example                    |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| ASDF system | Files, dependencies, compile/load order | `example.asd`              |
| Package     | Symbol namespace and public API         | `defpackage`, `in-package` |
| Source file | Text containing Lisp forms              | `src/users.lisp`           |
| Test system | Separate system for tests               | `example/test`             |
| Entry point | Explicit function that starts the app   | `main`                     |

**Core rule:** packages are namespaces; ASDF systems are build/load units; files are source containers. They are related, but not identical.



### H.2 Minimal recommended project layout

A small but professional project can start like this:

```text
example/
  example.asd
  README.md
  packages.lisp
  src/
    users.lisp
    config.lisp
    main.lisp
  test/
    packages.lisp
    users-test.lisp
    config-test.lisp
```

| Path                    | Purpose                                     |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `example.asd`           | ASDF system and test-system definitions     |
| `README.md`             | Loading, testing, usage, API overview       |
| `packages.lisp`         | Main package definitions                    |
| `src/users.lisp`        | Domain model or user-related implementation |
| `src/config.lisp`       | Configuration parsing/validation            |
| `src/main.lisp`         | Application/library entry points            |
| `test/packages.lisp`    | Test package definitions                    |
| `test/users-test.lisp`  | Tests for user model                        |
| `test/config-test.lisp` | Tests for config behavior                   |

This layout is intentionally conservative. It avoids too many packages too early, but still separates source and tests.

**Professional rule:** a project should have a predictable path from `example.asd` to package definitions to implementation files to tests.



### H.3 Minimal `.asd` file

File: `example.asd`

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:example
  :description "Example Common Lisp project."
  :author "Your Name"
  :license "MIT"
  :version "0.1.0"
  :depends-on (#:alexandria)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:file "packages")
   (:module "src"
    :components
    ((:file "users")
     (:file "config")
     (:file "main")))))

(asdf:defsystem #:example/test
  :description "Tests for the EXAMPLE system."
  :depends-on (#:example #:fiveam)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:module "test"
    :components
    ((:file "packages")
     (:file "users-test")
     (:file "config-test"))))
  :perform (asdf:test-op (op c)
             (declare (ignore op c))
             (symbol-call '#:fiveam '#:run! '#:example-tests)))
```

| Field                         | Meaning                                   |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| `#:example`                   | System name                               |
| `:depends-on`                 | Other ASDF systems required               |
| `:serial t`                   | Load components in listed order           |
| `:components`                 | Source files and modules                  |
| `(:file "packages")`          | Loads `packages.lisp` first               |
| `(:module "src" ...)`         | Loads files under `src/`                  |
| `#:example/test`              | Separate test system                      |
| `:perform (asdf:test-op ...)` | Defines how `asdf:test-system` runs tests |

Load:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
```

Test:

```lisp
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

or:

```lisp
(asdf:test-system :example/test)
```

depending on implementation and ASDF setup.

**Common Pitfall**

A common mistake is adding a library to the REPL with `ql:quickload` but not declaring it in `:depends-on`. The current image works; a fresh image fails.

**Professional rule:** if source code uses a library, the ASDF system must declare it.



### H.4 Package file template

File: `packages.lisp`

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:alexandria
                #:when-let)
  (:export
   ;; User model
   #:user
   #:make-user
   #:user-p
   #:user-name
   #:user-email

   ;; Config
   #:config
   #:make-config
   #:config-host
   #:config-port
   #:invalid-config

   ;; Entry point
   #:main))

(defpackage #:example.internal
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:invalid-config))
```

This defines two packages:

| Package            | Role                            |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------- |
| `example`          | Public package                  |
| `example.internal` | Internal implementation helpers |

For small projects, one public package may be enough. An internal package is useful when there are many helper functions or implementation-specific utilities.

**Export policy:**

| Export            | Reason                                |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Constructors      | Public object creation                |
| Predicates        | Public recognition                    |
| Readers           | Public observation                    |
| Conditions        | Public error handling                 |
| Generic functions | Public protocols                      |
| Entry points      | Application start or library main API |

Do not export:

| Do not export                    | Reason                              |
| -------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `%make-user`                     | Raw constructor bypasses validation |
| `%parse-config-token`            | Implementation detail               |
| temporary helpers                | Refactoring freedom                 |
| implementation-specific wrappers | Unless part of public API           |
| raw slot names                   | Representation detail               |

**Professional rule:** `packages.lisp` is the project’s public vocabulary map.



### H.5 Source file template: domain model

File: `src/users.lisp`

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(defstruct (%user
            (:constructor %make-user)
            (:predicate user-p))
  name
  email)

(defun make-user (&key name email)
  "Create and return a validated user object.

NAME and EMAIL must be strings."
  (unless (stringp name)
    (error "User name must be a string, got ~S." name))
  (unless (stringp email)
    (error "User email must be a string, got ~S." email))
  (%make-user :name name :email email))

(defun user-name (user)
  "Return USER's name."
  (%user-name user))

(defun user-email (user)
  "Return USER's email."
  (%user-email user))
```

This pattern hides the raw structure constructor and raw accessors.

| Symbol       | Public? | Reason                      |
| ------------ | ------: | --------------------------- |
| `make-user`  |     Yes | Validating constructor      |
| `user-p`     |     Yes | Predicate                   |
| `user-name`  |     Yes | Stable reader               |
| `user-email` |     Yes | Stable reader               |
| `%make-user` |      No | Bypasses validation         |
| `%user-name` |      No | Raw representation accessor |

**Design note:** `defstruct` automatically generates accessors, but exporting them without thought can freeze representation. Wrapping accessors may be slightly verbose, but it gives stronger API control.

Simpler alternative for small internal projects:

```lisp
(defstruct user
  name
  email)
```

Then export generated names if the representation is intended to be public.

**Professional rule:** public constructors should enforce invariants; raw constructors should usually remain internal.



### H.6 Source file template: structured conditions and config

File: `src/config.lisp`

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(define-condition invalid-config (error)
  ((source :initarg :source :reader invalid-config-source)
   (reason :initarg :reason :reader invalid-config-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid config from ~S: ~A"
                     (invalid-config-source condition)
                     (invalid-config-reason condition)))))

(defstruct (%config
            (:constructor %make-config))
  host
  port)

(defun make-config (&key host port (source :unknown))
  "Create and return a validated config object."
  (unless (stringp host)
    (error 'invalid-config
           :source source
           :reason "HOST must be a string."))
  (unless (typep port '(integer 1 65535))
    (error 'invalid-config
           :source source
           :reason "PORT must be an integer in the range 1..65535."))
  (%make-config :host host :port port))

(defun config-host (config)
  "Return CONFIG's host."
  (%config-host config))

(defun config-port (config)
  "Return CONFIG's port."
  (%config-port config))
```

This gives the project a structured public error.

| Design choice             | Reason                               |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `invalid-config` exported | Callers can handle config failures   |
| condition slots           | Programmatic diagnostics             |
| report function           | Human-readable debugger/error output |
| `make-config` wrapper     | Validates external data              |
| raw `%make-config` hidden | Prevents invalid object creation     |

Example handler:

```lisp
(handler-case
    (make-config :host "localhost" :port 8080)
  (invalid-config (condition)
    (format *error-output* "~A~%" condition)
    nil))
```

**Professional rule:** configuration is external input. Validate it at the boundary.



### H.7 Source file template: explicit entry point

File: `src/main.lisp`

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(defun main (&key
               (host "localhost")
               (port 8080))
  "Run the example application with HOST and PORT."
  (let ((config (make-config :host host
                             :port port
                             :source :main)))
    (format t "Starting example on ~A:~D~%"
            (config-host config)
            (config-port config))
    config))
```

Do not start the application at top level:

```lisp
;; Bad in a library or reusable system:
(main)
```

This causes load-time side effects.

Better:

```lisp
(defun main (...)
  ...)
```

Then the caller decides when to run:

```lisp
(example:main :host "localhost" :port 8080)
```

| Top-level form                     |      Good? | Reason                              |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------: | ----------------------------------- |
| `defun`                            |        Yes | Defines entry point                 |
| `defclass`                         |        Yes | Defines type                        |
| `defgeneric`                       |        Yes | Defines protocol                    |
| `defparameter`                     |      Maybe | If global/dynamic state is intended |
| `(main)`                           | Usually no | Loading should not start execution  |
| `(start-server)`                   | Usually no | Load-time side effect               |
| opening files/sockets at load time | Usually no | Hard to test/reload                 |

**Professional rule:** loading defines the system; an explicit entry point runs it.



### H.8 Test package template

File: `test/packages.lisp`

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.test
  (:use #:cl #:fiveam)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:make-user
                #:user-p
                #:user-name
                #:user-email
                #:make-config
                #:config-host
                #:config-port
                #:invalid-config))
```

This test package imports the public API.

**Testing through the public API** is usually the default. If tests need internals, decide deliberately.

| Test package strategy               | Use when                                      |
| ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Import public API only              | Black-box library tests                       |
| Use `example::internal` symbols     | White-box tests, brittle but sometimes useful |
| Separate internal test package      | Large systems with internal contracts         |
| Test package `:use`s public package | Simple projects, but can hide origins         |
| Explicit `:import-from`             | Clear dependencies                            |

**Professional rule:** most tests should exercise the public API. Internal tests should be labeled as such.



### H.9 Test file template: user tests

File: `test/users-test.lisp`

```lisp
(in-package #:example.test)

(def-suite example-tests)

(in-suite example-tests)

(test make-user-test
  (let ((user (make-user :name "Ada"
                         :email "ada@example.com")))
    (is (user-p user))
    (is (string= "Ada" (user-name user)))
    (is (string= "ada@example.com" (user-email user)))))

(test make-user-invalid-name-test
  (signals error
    (make-user :name 42
               :email "ada@example.com")))
```

This uses a FiveAM-style test structure.

| Form        | Role                  |
| ----------- | --------------------- |
| `def-suite` | Defines test suite    |
| `in-suite`  | Selects current suite |
| `test`      | Defines test case     |
| `is`        | Assertion             |
| `signals`   | Condition assertion   |

For public APIs with custom conditions, prefer testing condition type and slots where available.

The `make-user` example currently signals a simple `error`, so `signals error` is enough. If a custom `invalid-user` condition exists, test that condition type.

**Professional rule:** tests should verify both valid construction and invalid boundary behavior.



### H.10 Test file template: config tests

File: `test/config-test.lisp`

```lisp
(in-package #:example.test)

(test make-config-test
  (let ((config (make-config :host "localhost"
                             :port 8080)))
    (is (string= "localhost" (config-host config)))
    (is (= 8080 (config-port config)))))

(test make-config-invalid-port-test
  (handler-case
      (progn
        (make-config :host "localhost"
                     :port 99999
                     :source :test)
        (fail "Expected INVALID-CONFIG."))
    (invalid-config (condition)
      (is (eq :test
              (example::invalid-config-source condition)))
      (is (search "PORT"
                  (example::invalid-config-reason condition))))))
```

This test uses internal condition readers with `example::...` because the package template exported only `invalid-config`, not its readers. That reveals an API decision.

Better package export if callers should inspect details:

```lisp
(:export
 #:invalid-config
 #:invalid-config-source
 #:invalid-config-reason)
```

Then tests can import:

```lisp
#:invalid-config-source
#:invalid-config-reason
```

and write:

```lisp
(invalid-config-source condition)
```

**Design lesson:** if a condition is public and callers should handle it programmatically, export its slot readers.

Recommended revised export list:

```lisp
#:invalid-config
#:invalid-config-source
#:invalid-config-reason
```

**Professional rule:** tests often reveal whether the public API is complete enough.



### H.11 Revised package file with condition readers

A stronger `packages.lisp`:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:alexandria
                #:when-let)
  (:export
   ;; User model
   #:user
   #:make-user
   #:user-p
   #:user-name
   #:user-email

   ;; Config
   #:config
   #:make-config
   #:config-host
   #:config-port
   #:invalid-config
   #:invalid-config-source
   #:invalid-config-reason

   ;; Entry point
   #:main))
```

Then `test/packages.lisp` can be:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.test
  (:use #:cl #:fiveam)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:make-user
                #:user-p
                #:user-name
                #:user-email
                #:make-config
                #:config-host
                #:config-port
                #:invalid-config
                #:invalid-config-source
                #:invalid-config-reason))
```

Config test:

```lisp
(test make-config-invalid-port-test
  (handler-case
      (progn
        (make-config :host "localhost"
                     :port 99999
                     :source :test)
        (fail "Expected INVALID-CONFIG."))
    (invalid-config (condition)
      (is (eq :test
              (invalid-config-source condition)))
      (is (search "PORT"
                  (invalid-config-reason condition))))))
```

**Professional rule:** export condition readers when callers are expected to handle conditions programmatically.



### H.12 Loading and testing workflow

From a fresh Lisp image:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :example)
```

or:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
```

Run tests:

```lisp
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

If ASDF does not map `:example` to the test operation as expected in a particular setup, load and run the test system directly:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example/test)
```

Then:

```lisp
(fiveam:run! 'example.test::example-tests)
```

Exact testing command may vary by framework and package setup. The important invariant is:

```text
fresh image -> load system -> load/run tests
```

**Fresh-image check catches:**

| Problem                 | Example                                           |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Missing dependency      | Used Alexandria but did not list in `:depends-on` |
| Package order error     | `in-package` before `defpackage`                  |
| Hidden REPL state       | Function existed only interactively               |
| Macro phase issue       | Macro helper unavailable at compile time          |
| Test dependency leakage | FiveAM used in source system accidentally         |
| Load-time side effects  | System starts server when loaded                  |

**Professional rule:** a Common Lisp project is not reproducible until it loads and tests from a fresh image.



### H.13 Dependency declaration pattern

If a source file uses `alexandria:when-let`, declare Alexandria in ASDF:

```lisp
:depends-on (#:alexandria)
```

and either qualify the symbol:

```lisp
(alexandria:when-let (user (find-user id))
  (notify user))
```

or import it:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:alexandria
                #:when-let))
```

Then use:

```lisp
(when-let (user (find-user id))
  (notify user))
```

| Dependency layer       | Example                                |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| ASDF system dependency | `:depends-on (#:alexandria)`           |
| Package access         | `:import-from #:alexandria #:when-let` |
| Source use             | `(when-let ...)`                       |
| Quicklisp acquisition  | `(ql:quickload :example)`              |

**Common Pitfall**

Loading a dependency manually:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :alexandria)
```

then forgetting:

```lisp
:depends-on (#:alexandria)
```

The project will fail in a fresh image.

**Professional rule:** loading is not dependency declaration. Declare dependencies in the `.asd` file.



### H.14 File dependency and `:serial t`

For small projects, `:serial t` is simple:

```lisp
:serial t
:components
((:file "packages")
 (:module "src"
  :components
  ((:file "users")
   (:file "config")
   (:file "main"))))
```

This means files are loaded in order.

For larger systems, explicit dependencies may be clearer:

```lisp
:components
((:file "packages")
 (:module "src"
  :components
  ((:file "users" :depends-on ("../packages"))
   (:file "config" :depends-on ("../packages"))
   (:file "main" :depends-on ("users" "config")))))
```

Exact ASDF path/dependency forms can become more complex. Many projects use `:serial t` within modules for readability.

| Strategy               | Good for                     | Caveat                        |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| `:serial t`            | Small/medium linear systems  | Can hide unnecessary ordering |
| Explicit `:depends-on` | Larger systems               | More verbose                  |
| Split systems          | Optional components/features | More ASDF complexity          |
| Separate test system   | Almost always good           | Needs test setup              |

**Professional rule:** encode load order in ASDF, not in a README instruction saying “load these files manually.”



### H.15 Multi-package project template

For larger systems:

```text
example/
  example.asd
  packages.lisp
  src/
    users.lisp
    config.lisp
    rendering.lisp
    storage.lisp
    main.lisp
  test/
    packages.lisp
    users-test.lisp
    config-test.lisp
```

Package file:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.users
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:make-user
           #:user-p
           #:user-name
           #:user-email))

(defpackage #:example.config
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:make-config
           #:config-host
           #:config-port
           #:invalid-config
           #:invalid-config-source
           #:invalid-config-reason))

(defpackage #:example.rendering
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:example.users
                #:user-name
                #:user-email)
  (:export #:render-to-stream
           #:rendering-error))

(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:example.users
                #:make-user
                #:user-p
                #:user-name
                #:user-email)
  (:import-from #:example.config
                #:make-config
                #:config-host
                #:config-port)
  (:import-from #:example.rendering
                #:render-to-stream)
  (:export #:make-user
           #:user-p
           #:user-name
           #:user-email
           #:make-config
           #:config-host
           #:config-port
           #:render-to-stream
           #:main))
```

Here `example` can act as a facade package that re-exports selected APIs from subpackages.

| Package             | Role               |
| ------------------- | ------------------ |
| `example.users`     | User domain        |
| `example.config`    | Config domain      |
| `example.rendering` | Rendering protocol |
| `example`           | Public facade      |

**When multi-package is useful:**

| Use multi-package when           | Avoid multi-package when       |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Subsystems have distinct APIs    | Project is tiny                |
| Dependencies should be separated | Boundaries are artificial      |
| Internal names conflict          | Package overhead dominates     |
| Public facade is useful          | Everything is one small module |
| Extension protocols differ       | Refactoring still fluid        |

**Professional rule:** add packages to clarify subsystem boundaries, not to imitate file/module structures mechanically.



### H.16 Application vs library layout

A library and an application have different entry-point expectations.

Library-style project:

```text
my-library/
  my-library.asd
  packages.lisp
  src/
    protocol.lisp
    implementation.lisp
  test/
    ...
```

Library focus:

| Concern                   | Library               |
| ------------------------- | --------------------- |
| Public API                | Central               |
| Export list               | Carefully stable      |
| Entry point               | Usually none          |
| Conditions                | Public and structured |
| Generic functions         | Extension protocols   |
| Side effects at load time | Avoid                 |
| Compatibility             | Important             |

Application-style project:

```text
my-app/
  my-app.asd
  packages.lisp
  src/
    config.lisp
    service.lisp
    main.lisp
  test/
    ...
```

Application focus:

| Concern                | Application         |
| ---------------------- | ------------------- |
| `main`                 | Central             |
| Config                 | Boundary validation |
| Logging                | Important           |
| Deployment             | Important           |
| Conditions             | Often app-specific  |
| External I/O           | More common         |
| Load-time side effects | Still avoid         |

Application entry point:

```lisp
(defun main (&key config-path)
  (let ((config (load-config config-path)))
    (start-service config)))
```

Library API:

```lisp
(defgeneric encode-to-stream (object stream &key pretty))
```

**Professional rule:** libraries define reusable operations; applications define explicit startup behavior.



### H.17 CLI-oriented template

For command-line-like programs, keep CLI parsing at the edge.

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.cli
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:main)
  (:export #:run-cli))
```

```lisp
(in-package #:example.cli)

(defun parse-arguments (args)
  "Parse command-line ARGS into keyword arguments for EXAMPLE:MAIN."
  ;; Simplified example.
  (destructuring-bind (&key (host "localhost") (port "8080") &allow-other-keys)
      args
    (list :host host
          :port (parse-integer port :junk-allowed nil))))

(defun run-cli ()
  (let ((args (uiop:command-line-arguments)))
    ;; Real code should use a proper option parser or robust parsing.
    (apply #'main (parse-arguments args))))
```

This example is deliberately minimal. Real command-line parsing should handle flags, errors, usage text, and exit status explicitly.

| Layer        | Responsibility                          |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------- |
| CLI package  | Parse strings, show usage, exit         |
| Config layer | Validate domain config                  |
| Core app     | Run with typed/configured values        |
| Tests        | Test parsing separately from core logic |

**Professional rule:** CLI arguments are strings. Convert them to domain values at the boundary.



### H.18 Internal package template

For implementation details:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example.internal
  (:use #:cl)
  (:import-from #:example
                #:invalid-config
                #:invalid-config-source
                #:invalid-config-reason)
  (:export
   ;; Usually export nothing unless internal subpackages need it.
   ))
```

Internal source:

```lisp
(in-package #:example.internal)

(defun %normalize-host (host)
  (string-downcase (string-trim " " host)))
```

Public code may call internal helper only within the system:

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(defun make-config (&key host port (source :unknown))
  (let ((host (example.internal::%normalize-host host)))
    ...))
```

This uses `::`, but inside the same project this can be acceptable. For cleaner boundaries, import internal symbols into relevant implementation packages or keep helpers in the same package.

| Internal access                                 | Acceptable? |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ----------- |
| Same system implementation uses internal helper | Usually yes |
| External user uses `example.internal::helper`   | No, brittle |
| Tests use internals deliberately                | Sometimes   |
| Public documentation mentions internal helper   | Usually no  |

**Professional rule:** internal packages are for project organization, not for stable external APIs.



### H.19 README template

See README Tutorial.

### H.20 `.asd` style checklist

| Check | Good |
|---|---|
| System name matches file convention | `example.asd` defines `#:example` |
| Dependencies declared | `:depends-on (...)` |
| Package file loaded first | `(:file "packages")` before source files |
| Test system separate | `#:example/test` |
| Test dependencies isolated | FiveAM only in test system |
| Load order encoded | `:serial t` or explicit dependencies |
| No manual load instructions required | ASDF handles it |
| System metadata present | description, author, license, version |
| Optional features separated | separate systems if needed |
| No load-time app startup | entry point only |



### H.21 Package style checklist

| Check | Good |
|---|---|
| Package names meaningful | `example.config`, not random abbreviations |
| Uses `#:package-name` style | Avoids accidental interning in current package |
| `:use #:cl` explicit | Common default |
| Imports selected external names | `:import-from` |
| Exports stable API only | No helper leakage |
| Condition readers exported if condition public | Programmatic handling |
| Generic functions exported if extension public | Method definition by users |
| No excessive broad `:use` | Avoid conflicts |
| Shadowing rare and explicit | Avoid hiding CL names |
| Test package separate | Clean tests |



### H.22 Source file checklist

| Check | Good |
|---|---|
| Starts with `in-package` | Clear namespace |
| Package already defined by ASDF order | No reader/load error |
| Definitions grouped by concept | Readability |
| Top-level forms define, not run | No surprise side effects |
| Public functions have docstrings | API clarity |
| Boundary functions validate input | Robustness |
| Conditions used for public errors | Structured handling |
| Internal helpers unexported | Refactoring freedom |
| Macros documented and tested | Expansion discipline |
| Implementation-specific code isolated | Portability |



### H.23 Test system checklist

| Check | Good |
|---|---|
| Separate test system | `example/test` |
| Test dependencies not in core | `#:fiveam` only in test system |
| Test package separate | `example.test` |
| Tests import public API | Black-box default |
| Conditions tested by type/slots | Not message strings only |
| Multiple values tested when relevant | Presence/status |
| Invalid input tested | Boundary behavior |
| Fresh-image runnable | Reproducibility |
| ASDF `test-op` configured | One command test |
| Internal tests labeled | Avoid accidental API dependence |



### H.24 Common project-structure mistakes

| Mistake | Consequence | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No `.asd` file | Manual loading only | Add ASDF system |
| `in-package` before `defpackage` loaded | Package error | Load `packages.lisp` first |
| Quickloaded dependency not in `:depends-on` | Fresh image failure | Declare dependency |
| Test library in core system | Runtime dependency pollution | Separate test system |
| Exports everything | No internal boundary | Export stable API only |
| Uses `pkg::internal` externally | Brittle coupling | Export supported API |
| Starts server at load time | Loading has side effects | Define explicit `main` |
| Tests depend on current REPL state | Non-reproducible | Fresh-image ASDF tests |
| One huge source file | Hard navigation | Split by conceptual area |
