| Too many tiny packages | Noise and complexity | Add packages only for real boundaries |
| String-only public errors | Hard to handle | Define condition classes |
| Package and system assumed identical | Name confusion | Treat separately |



### H.25 Final small-library template

Directory:

```text
small-lib/
  small-lib.asd
  packages.lisp
  src/
    core.lisp
  test/
    packages.lisp
    core-test.lisp
````

`small-lib.asd`:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:small-lib
  :description "Small example library."
  :author "Your Name"
  :license "MIT"
  :version "0.1.0"
  :depends-on ()
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:file "packages")
   (:module "src"
    :components
    ((:file "core")))))

(asdf:defsystem #:small-lib/test
  :depends-on (#:small-lib #:fiveam)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:module "test"
    :components
    ((:file "packages")
     (:file "core-test"))))
  :perform (asdf:test-op (op c)
             (declare (ignore op c))
             (symbol-call '#:fiveam '#:run! '#:small-lib-tests)))
```

`packages.lisp`:

```lisp
(defpackage #:small-lib
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:normalize-name
           #:invalid-name
           #:invalid-name-value))
```

`src/core.lisp`:

```lisp
(in-package #:small-lib)

(define-condition invalid-name (error)
  ((value :initarg :value :reader invalid-name-value))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid name: ~S"
                     (invalid-name-value condition)))))

(defun normalize-name (name)
  "Return NAME trimmed and lowercased.

Signals INVALID-NAME if NAME is not a nonempty string."
  (unless (and (stringp name)
               (plusp (length name)))
    (error 'invalid-name :value name))
  (string-downcase
   (string-trim '(#\Space #\Tab #\Newline) name)))
```

`test/packages.lisp`:

```lisp
(defpackage #:small-lib.test
  (:use #:cl #:fiveam)
  (:import-from #:small-lib
                #:normalize-name
                #:invalid-name
                #:invalid-name-value))
```

`test/core-test.lisp`:

```lisp
(in-package #:small-lib.test)

(def-suite small-lib-tests)
(in-suite small-lib-tests)

(test normalize-name-test
  (is (string= "ada"
               (normalize-name " Ada "))))

(test normalize-name-invalid-test
  (handler-case
      (progn
        (normalize-name "")
        (fail "Expected INVALID-NAME."))
    (invalid-name (condition)
      (is (string= "" (invalid-name-value condition))))))
```



### H.26 Final application template

Directory:

```text
my-app/
  my-app.asd
  packages.lisp
  src/
    config.lisp
    service.lisp
    main.lisp
  test/
    packages.lisp
    config-test.lisp
```

`my-app.asd`:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:my-app
  :description "Example application."
  :author "Your Name"
  :license "MIT"
  :version "0.1.0"
  :depends-on (#:alexandria)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:file "packages")
   (:module "src"
    :components
    ((:file "config")
     (:file "service")
     (:file "main")))))

(asdf:defsystem #:my-app/test
  :depends-on (#:my-app #:fiveam)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:module "test"
    :components
    ((:file "packages")
     (:file "config-test"))))
  :perform (asdf:test-op (op c)
             (declare (ignore op c))
             (symbol-call '#:fiveam '#:run! '#:my-app-tests)))
```

`packages.lisp`:

```lisp
(defpackage #:my-app
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:main
           #:make-config
           #:config-host
           #:config-port
           #:invalid-config
           #:invalid-config-reason))
```

`src/config.lisp`:

```lisp
(in-package #:my-app)

(define-condition invalid-config (error)
  ((reason :initarg :reason :reader invalid-config-reason))
  (:report (lambda (condition stream)
             (format stream "Invalid config: ~A"
                     (invalid-config-reason condition)))))

(defstruct (%config (:constructor %make-config))
  host
  port)

(defun make-config (&key (host "localhost") (port 8080))
  (unless (stringp host)
    (error 'invalid-config :reason "HOST must be a string."))
  (unless (typep port '(integer 1 65535))
    (error 'invalid-config :reason "PORT must be a valid port."))
  (%make-config :host host :port port))

(defun config-host (config)
  (%config-host config))

(defun config-port (config)
  (%config-port config))
```

`src/service.lisp`:

```lisp
(in-package #:my-app)

(defun start-service (config)
  "Start the service described by CONFIG."
  (format t "Service starting on ~A:~D~%"
          (config-host config)
          (config-port config))
  config)
```

`src/main.lisp`:

```lisp
(in-package #:my-app)

(defun main (&key
               (host "localhost")
               (port 8080))
  "Start the application."
  (let ((config (make-config :host host :port port)))
    (start-service config)))
```

**Key point:** loading `my-app` defines `main`; it does not call `main`.



### H.27 Final project decision tree

```text
DESIGNING PROJECT STRUCTURE
├── Is this a single experiment?
│   └── One file may be enough, but move durable code to ASDF soon.
│
├── Is this a reusable library?
│   ├── ASDF system
│   ├── public package
│   ├── careful exports
│   ├── structured conditions
│   └── test system
│
├── Is this an application?
│   ├── ASDF system
│   ├── config boundary
│   ├── explicit MAIN
│   ├── no load-time startup
│   └── deployment plan
│
├── Are there distinct subsystems?
│   ├── maybe multiple packages
│   └── maybe facade package
│
├── Are there optional features?
│   └── consider separate ASDF systems
│
├── Are tests needed?
│   └── always use separate test system for serious code
│
└── Does it work only in the current REPL?
    └── fix ASDF/package/dependency source of truth
```

### H.28 Final compact project checklist

| Requirement                    | Minimum professional answer          |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| Loadable from fresh image      | `(asdf:load-system :project)` works  |
| Testable from fresh image      | `(asdf:test-system :project)` works  |
| Package defined before use     | `packages.lisp` first                |
| Dependencies declared          | `:depends-on` complete               |
| Public API visible             | `:export` intentional                |
| Internals hidden               | no accidental exports                |
| Tests separate                 | `project/test` system                |
| Test dependencies isolated     | FiveAM/Rove/etc. not in core         |
| Entry point explicit           | `main`, not top-level startup        |
| Conditions structured          | public errors use `define-condition` |
| External input validated       | config/CLI/files parsed at boundary  |
| Load-time side effects avoided | loading defines, does not run        |
| README usable                  | load/test/basic usage included       |

### H.29 Final rule

**Final conclusion:** a professional Common Lisp project is not defined by directory layout alone. It is defined by reproducibility. ASDF records how the system is loaded, packages record what names mean, tests prove source-defined behavior, and entry points make execution explicit. The REPL may be the development environment, but the ASDF system and source files are the durable program.

## Appendix I — Runtime, Image, Compile/Load, and Fresh-Image Debugging

### I.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **runtime and image-model diagnostic reference**.

Common Lisp is often developed in a live image: functions can be redefined, methods can be added, classes can change, packages can be created, macros can be tested, and objects can remain alive while the program evolves. This is one of Common Lisp’s major strengths.

It is also a major source of confusion.

A program can work in the current REPL because the image contains definitions, packages, variables, or loaded systems that are not actually present in the source-controlled project.

**Core rule:** the REPL image is the working laboratory; the source files plus ASDF system are the durable program.



### I.2 The core pipeline

A Common Lisp program passes through several layers:

```text
source text
  -> reader
  -> Lisp objects
  -> macro expansion
  -> compilation or interpretation
  -> loading into image
  -> runtime execution
  -> debugger/restarts/redefinition
```

| Layer             | What happens                           | Typical mistake                       |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Source text       | Characters in files or REPL input      | Thinking text equals symbols directly |
| Reader            | Text becomes Lisp objects              | Package/readtable confusion           |
| Macro expansion   | Macro forms become ordinary forms      | Runtime/compile-time confusion        |
| Compilation       | Forms become compiled code             | Ignoring compiler warnings            |
| Loading           | Definitions enter the image            | Top-level side effects surprise       |
| Runtime           | Functions execute and objects allocate | Equality/mutation/condition bugs      |
| Image interaction | Redefinition and debugging happen live | Image/source drift                    |

**Professional habit:** when a bug appears, first locate its layer. A package reader error, a macro expansion bug, a load-order bug, and a runtime logic bug require different tools.



### I.3 Source file vs image

A source file is durable text.

An image is the live Lisp world currently running.

| Source file                                  | Image                                 |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Persistent                                   | Mutable                               |
| Version-controlled                           | Session-dependent                     |
| Loaded into image                            | Contains loaded definitions           |
| Does not automatically erase old image state | May retain stale definitions          |
| Defines intended program                     | May contain experiments               |
| Reproducible if ASDF-managed                 | May be impossible to recreate exactly |

Example:

```lisp
(defun greet ()
  "hello")
```

After evaluating this in the REPL, the image has a function binding for `greet`.

If this definition was never saved to a source file, it disappears when the image restarts.

**Failure-first explanation: REPL success is not source success**

The tempting model is:

```text
It works in the REPL, so the project works.
```

The failure appears after restarting Lisp and loading the project. The function, macro, package, variable, or method may not exist.

**Professional rule:** every durable definition must exist in source and be loaded through ASDF.



### I.4 Compile time, load time, and runtime

Common Lisp distinguishes several phases.

| Phase                | Meaning                                   | Example                           |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Read time            | Text becomes Lisp objects                 | symbols, lists, reader macros     |
| Macro expansion time | Macros produce code                       | `defmacro`, `macroexpand`         |
| Compile time         | Compiler processes forms                  | declarations, compiler macros     |
| Load time            | Compiled/source file is loaded into image | top-level definitions take effect |
| Runtime              | Function body executes                    | ordinary calls, object allocation |
| Debug time           | Live system inspected/restarted           | debugger, restarts                |

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro when-positive (x &body body)
  `(when (> ,x 0)
     ,@body))
```

The macro runs during macro expansion. The comparison `>`, however, appears in the generated code and runs at runtime.

Call:

```lisp
(when-positive score
  (print score))
```

Expansion:

```lisp
(when (> score 0)
  (print score))
```

**Professional rule:** ask whether the code is being read, expanded, compiled, loaded, or executed.



### I.5 `eval`, `compile-file`, `load`, and ASDF

Common Lisp provides several ways to process code.

| Operation          | Meaning                                 | Common use                            |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `eval`             | Evaluate a form in an environment       | REPL/tools; rare in application logic |
| `compile`          | Compile a function object or definition | Advanced/runtime compilation          |
| `compile-file`     | Compile a source file into a FASL       | Usually managed by ASDF               |
| `load`             | Load source or compiled file into image | Manual/simple loading                 |
| `asdf:load-system` | Load system and dependencies            | Professional project loading          |
| `ql:quickload`     | Acquire/load system through Quicklisp   | Development dependency loading        |

Manual compile/load:

```lisp
(compile-file "src/users.lisp")
(load "src/users.fasl")
```

Professional project loading:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
```

Quicklisp-assisted loading:

```lisp
(ql:quickload :example)
```

**Important distinction:**

| Tool               | Does                                         |
| ------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| `load`             | Loads one file                               |
| `asdf:load-system` | Loads a system graph                         |
| `ql:quickload`     | Finds/acquires systems and asks ASDF to load |
| `in-package`       | Changes package context                      |
| `defpackage`       | Defines package namespace                    |
| `:depends-on`      | Declares ASDF system dependency              |

**Professional rule:** for projects, use ASDF. Manual file loading is acceptable for experiments, but fragile for systems.



### I.6 FASL files

A FASL is an implementation-specific compiled file.

```text
source.lisp -> compile-file -> source.fasl
```

Exact extension and format are implementation-specific.

| Property                | Meaning                                                            |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Implementation-specific | SBCL FASL is not portable to another implementation                |
| Version-sensitive       | May depend on compiler/runtime version                             |
| Loadable                | `(load "file.fasl")` loads compiled definitions                    |
| Generated artifact      | Usually not source-controlled unless project policy says otherwise |
| Managed by ASDF         | ASDF compiles and caches as needed                                 |

**Common Pitfall**

A common mistake is debugging an old compiled artifact when the source has changed but the system was not properly recompiled. ASDF usually manages this, but stale artifacts or manual loading can still confuse diagnosis.

**Professional rule:** if behavior looks stale, force recompilation or clean ASDF output/cache for the system.



### I.7 Top-level forms

Top-level forms are processed specially by the compiler and loader.

Typical good top-level forms:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:main))
```

```lisp
(in-package #:example)
```

```lisp
(defun main ()
  ...)
```

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ...)
```

```lisp
(defgeneric render (object stream))
```

Risky top-level effects:

```lisp
(start-server)
```

```lisp
(connect-to-database)
```

```lisp
(delete-file "/tmp/output")
```

```lisp
(pushnew :loaded *global-state*)
```

| Top-level form type              |      Good? | Reason                      |
| -------------------------------- | ---------: | --------------------------- |
| Definitions                      |        Yes | Establish program objects   |
| Package setup                    |        Yes | Required namespace setup    |
| Declarations                     |        Yes | Compiler/global policy      |
| Constant/static data definitions |    Usually | If no hidden mutation       |
| Registry mutation                |      Maybe | Must be idempotent          |
| File/network/database effects    | Usually no | Loading becomes execution   |
| Starting threads/services        | Usually no | Reloading becomes dangerous |

**Professional rule:** top-level forms should define. Explicit entry points should run.



### I.8 Load-time side effects

Load-time side effects make a system hard to test, reload, compile, or inspect.

Bad:

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(start-service)
```

Now loading the file starts the service.

Better:

```lisp
(in-package #:example)

(defun start-service-command ()
  (start-service))
```

Application entry point:

```lisp
(defun main (&key config-path)
  (let ((config (load-config config-path)))
    (start-service config)))
```

| Side effect             | Better location                           |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Start server            | `main` or explicit `start` function       |
| Open database           | runtime initialization                    |
| Read config             | startup function                          |
| Create files            | command function                          |
| Start background thread | lifecycle manager                         |
| Register plugin         | top-level only if idempotent and intended |

Some top-level registration is acceptable:

```lisp
(register-handler :json #'handle-json)
```

But it must be designed to be safe on reload.

Safer:

```lisp
(defun register-default-handlers ()
  (register-handler :json #'handle-json)
  (register-handler :text #'handle-text))
```

**Professional rule:** if reloading a file twice causes bad behavior, the top-level side effect is suspect.



### I.9 `defvar`, `defparameter`, and reload behavior

`defvar` and `defparameter` both define special variables, but reload behavior differs.

```lisp
(defvar *cache* (make-hash-table))
```

`defvar` initializes only if the variable is unbound.

```lisp
(defparameter *timeout* 30)
```

`defparameter` assigns the value every time the form is evaluated.

| Form           | Re-evaluation behavior            | Use                                              |
| -------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| `defvar`       | Does not overwrite existing value | Long-lived mutable state, user-tuned variables   |
| `defparameter` | Overwrites value                  | Configurable default that should reset on reload |
| `defconstant`  | Defines constant                  | True constants, but redefinition is delicate     |

Example:

```lisp
(defvar *counter* 0)
```

If the REPL changes:

```lisp
(setf *counter* 100)
```

and the file is reloaded, `*counter*` remains `100`.

With:

```lisp
(defparameter *counter* 0)
```

reloading resets it to `0`.

**Common Pitfall**

A developer expects reload to reset a `defvar`, but it does not.

**Professional rule:** use `defvar` when preserving interactive value is intended; use `defparameter` when reloading should reset the binding.



### I.10 Redefining functions

Functions can be redefined in a live image.

```lisp
(defun score (x)
  (+ x 1))
```

Later:

```lisp
(defun score (x)
  (+ x 10))
```

Future calls use the new function definition.

But existing references can complicate things:

```lisp
(defparameter *old-score* #'score)
```

Depending on implementation and function object semantics, a saved function object may refer to an earlier function object or function binding behavior may vary by context. Avoid depending on subtle live redefinition semantics for architecture.

**Common workflow:**

1. Edit function.
2. Compile/evaluate definition into image.
3. Test interactively.
4. Save source.
5. Run test system from clean load.

**Professional rule:** live function redefinition is a development tool, not a replacement for clean testing.



### I.11 Redefining macros

Macro redefinition is more dangerous than function redefinition.

```lisp
(defmacro my-when (test &body body)
  `(if ,test (progn ,@body)))
```

If code has already been compiled using this macro, the compiled code contains the old expansion.

Later:

```lisp
(defmacro my-when (test &body body)
  `(when ,test
     (format t "Running body.~%")
     ,@body))
```

Previously compiled users of `my-when` do not automatically change. They must be recompiled.

| Change                              | Required action                            |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Function body changes               | Recompile/reload function                  |
| Macro body changes                  | Recompile macro and users                  |
| Macro syntax changes                | Update all callers                         |
| Macro helper changes                | Recompile macro/users as needed            |
| Definition-generating macro changes | Regenerate/recompile generated definitions |

**Failure-first explanation: macro changes are not retroactive**

The macro runs during expansion. Once code has expanded and compiled, changing the macro does not rewrite existing compiled code.

**Professional rule:** after changing a macro, recompile dependent files or reload the system cleanly.



### I.12 Redefining classes

CLOS supports class redefinition in a live image, but existing instances and slot changes can require care.

Initial class:

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :accessor user-name)))
```

Later:

```lisp
(defclass user ()
  ((name :initarg :name :accessor user-name)
   (email :initarg :email :accessor user-email :initform nil)))
```

Existing instances may be updated according to CLOS class redefinition rules when accessed. Advanced customization is possible, but should not be used casually.

| Class change              | Risk                                     |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Add slot with `:initform` | Existing instances can get default       |
| Add slot without default  | Existing instances may have unbound slot |
| Remove slot               | Old accessors/code may fail              |
| Rename slot               | Existing data migration issue            |
| Change inheritance        | Method dispatch may change               |
| Change accessor export    | API compatibility issue                  |

**Professional rule:** class redefinition is powerful in development. For persistent data or production systems, design explicit migration.



### I.13 Redefining methods and generic functions

Methods can be added or redefined live.

```lisp
(defgeneric render (object stream))

(defmethod render ((object string) stream)
  (write-string object stream))
```

Later:

```lisp
(defmethod render ((object user) stream)
  ...)
```

Now `render` can handle users.

Changing a generic function lambda list is more serious:

```lisp
(defgeneric render (object stream &key pretty))
```

Existing methods must be congruent with the generic function’s lambda list.

| Change                     | Risk                                            |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Add method                 | Usually safe                                    |
| Redefine method            | Future dispatch changes                         |
| Remove method              | Old method may remain unless removed explicitly |
| Change generic lambda list | Method congruence errors                        |
| Add `:around` method       | May alter control flow                          |
| Change method specializers | Dispatch changes                                |

**Common Pitfall**

Deleting a `defmethod` form from source and reloading the file may not remove the old method from the image. It may remain until the image is restarted or the method is explicitly removed.

**Professional rule:** fresh-image testing catches stale method problems that reloading alone may hide.



### I.14 Stale definitions

A stale definition exists in the image even though the current source no longer defines it.

Example:

1. Source once contained:

```lisp
(defun old-helper ()
  ...)
```

1. This was loaded into the image.
2. Source file is edited to remove `old-helper`.
3. File is reloaded.

The function binding may still exist in the image.

Diagnostic:

```lisp
(fboundp 'old-helper)
```

Potential stale objects:

| Stale thing      | Example                 |
| ---------------- | ----------------------- |
| Function         | old `defun`             |
| Macro            | old `defmacro`          |
| Method           | old `defmethod`         |
| Class            | old `defclass`          |
| Package          | old `defpackage` result |
| Special variable | old `defvar`            |
| Symbol           | old interned symbol     |
| Registry entry   | old top-level mutation  |
| Object instance  | old runtime data        |

**Professional rule:** deleting source does not necessarily delete image state. Restart the image to verify real source-defined behavior.



### I.15 Package state and stale symbols

Packages are live objects too.

If a package once exported a symbol:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:old-api))
```

and later source changes to:

```lisp
(defpackage #:example
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:new-api))
```

Reloading may not fully produce the package state one expects, depending on package redefinition behavior, conflicts, and implementation details. Old symbols may remain interned.

Potential issues:

| Package state issue         | Symptom                            |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| Old symbol still interned   | `find-symbol` still finds it       |
| Old export still visible    | external users can still access it |
| Package conflict            | reload signals name conflict       |
| Old imports remain          | symbol origin confusing            |
| `use-package` conflict      | inherited names collide            |
| Deleted package not removed | stale package exists               |

Diagnostics:

```lisp
(find-package "EXAMPLE")
```

```lisp
(find-symbol "OLD-API" "EXAMPLE")
```

```lisp
(package-use-list (find-package "EXAMPLE"))
```

```lisp
(package-used-by-list (find-package "EXAMPLE"))
```

**Professional rule:** package changes should be tested in a fresh image.



### I.16 ASDF and source-of-truth discipline

ASDF describes how a system is loaded.

A healthy project can be tested by:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

from a fresh image.

ASDF file:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:example
  :depends-on (#:alexandria)
  :serial t
  :components
  ((:file "packages")
   (:module "src"
    :components
    ((:file "users")
     (:file "config")
     (:file "main")))))
```

| Source-of-truth item | Where it belongs                        |
| -------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Dependency           | `.asd` `:depends-on`                    |
| File order           | `.asd` components                       |
| Package definition   | `packages.lisp`                         |
| Public API           | `defpackage :export`                    |
| Entry point          | source function, e.g. `main`            |
| Tests                | test system                             |
| Test dependencies    | test system `:depends-on`               |
| Runtime startup      | explicit function, not load side effect |

**Professional rule:** if a step must be done manually after loading, ask whether ASDF, package definitions, or an explicit startup function should encode it.



### I.17 Fresh-image debugging

A fresh image means a newly started Lisp process with no manually evaluated project definitions.

Minimum clean test:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

Fresh-image debugging catches:

| Hidden problem            | Example                                       |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Missing source definition | Function only existed in REPL                 |
| Missing ASDF dependency   | Library manually quickloaded earlier          |
| Package not defined       | `in-package` relies on old package            |
| Macro phase problem       | Macro helper unavailable during compilation   |
| Stale method              | Method deleted from source but still in image |
| Stale export              | Package old state remains                     |
| Load-time side effect     | Service starts during load                    |
| `defvar` preserved state  | Reload did not reset state                    |
| Uncompiled macro users    | Old macro expansion remains                   |
| Test pollution            | Test dependency used by core                  |

**Fresh-image workflow:**

```text
1. Start new Lisp process.
2. Load Quicklisp/ASDF setup if needed.
3. Load project through ASDF.
4. Run test system.
5. Call minimal public API smoke test.
6. If deployment exists, run deployment artifact.
```

**Professional rule:** if a bug disappears only in a warm image, the image is part of the bug.



### I.18 Clean-load checklist

Use this checklist before considering a project healthy.

| Check             | Question                                                  |
| ----------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| ASDF load         | Does `(asdf:load-system :project)` work in a fresh image? |
| Test load         | Does the test system load in a fresh image?               |
| Test run          | Does `(asdf:test-system :project)` work?                  |
| Dependencies      | Are all libraries in `:depends-on`?                       |
| Package order     | Is `packages.lisp` loaded first?                          |
| Macros            | Are macro definitions loaded before use?                  |
| Macro helpers     | Are expansion-time helpers available when compiling?      |
| File order        | Does ASDF encode component order?                         |
| Top-level effects | Does loading only define, not start?                      |
| Warnings          | Are compiler warnings understood?                         |
| Stale definitions | Does restart remove hidden dependencies?                  |
| Public API        | Can public calls be made with package prefixes?           |
| Tests             | Do tests import only intended symbols?                    |



### I.19 Compile/load phase diagnostic table

| Symptom                              | Likely phase                 | Likely cause                                   | First check                 |
| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Reader error: package does not exist | Read time                    | package prefix before package defined          | ASDF order, `defpackage`    |
| Symbol not external                  | Read time/package            | used `pkg:symbol` for internal symbol          | export list                 |
| Undefined function while compiling   | Compile time                 | function not defined/declared/loaded           | file order, dependency      |
| Macro undefined                      | Compile time/macro expansion | macro definition not available                 | ASDF order                  |
| Macro helper undefined               | Macro expansion              | helper unavailable at compile time             | `eval-when`                 |
| Works interpreted, fails compiled    | Compile/phase                | compile-time availability or declaration issue | warnings, macro expansion   |
| Changed macro but behavior unchanged | Expansion/compile            | dependent code not recompiled                  | recompile users             |
| Deleted method still runs            | Image state                  | stale method remains                           | fresh image                 |
| Reload does not reset variable       | Load/image                   | used `defvar`                                  | use `defparameter` or reset |
| Load starts server                   | Load time                    | top-level side effect                          | move to `main`              |
| Fresh image fails, REPL works        | Image/source drift           | manual definitions/dependencies                | ASDF/source audit           |



### I.20 `eval-when`

`eval-when` controls when forms are evaluated.

```lisp
(eval-when (:compile-toplevel :load-toplevel :execute)
  ...)
```

Common use: make helper functions available to macros at compile time.

```lisp
(eval-when (:compile-toplevel :load-toplevel :execute)
  (defun normalize-option-name (name)
    (intern (string-upcase name) :keyword)))

(defmacro define-option (name)
  `(defparameter ,(normalize-option-name name) nil))
```

| Situation                                     | Need `eval-when`?                                      |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Ordinary runtime function                     | Usually no                                             |
| Macro definition before use in same file      | Sometimes handled by top-level semantics, but be aware |
| Macro helper called during expansion          | Often yes                                              |
| Constant data used by macro at expansion time | Maybe                                                  |
| Runtime helper called by generated code       | No, needs runtime definition                           |
| Top-level registry used by macro              | Dangerous; phase-sensitive                             |

**Common Pitfall**

Adding `eval-when` around large blocks without understanding why makes phase behavior harder to reason about.

**Professional rule:** use `eval-when` narrowly, and only when a compile/load/execute phase requirement is real.



### I.21 `load-time-value`

`load-time-value` computes a value at load time and embeds or arranges it for use in compiled code.

Example:

```lisp
(defparameter *table*
  (load-time-value (make-hash-table)))
```

This is advanced and should not be used as a casual initialization tool.

| Use                                  | Caution                    |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| Precompute object at load time       | Understand object lifetime |
| Avoid recomputation                  | Load-time behavior matters |
| Constants needing runtime allocation | Subtle with mutability     |
| Compiler/runtime control             | Advanced phase tool        |

**Professional rule:** most initialization belongs in `defparameter`, constructors, or explicit startup functions. Use `load-time-value` only when load-time semantics are specifically needed.



### I.22 `compile-file` and macros in the same file

A common question: can a macro be defined and used later in the same file?

Usually top-level `defmacro` forms are recognized by the compiler so later forms can use the macro. But macro helper functions and nonstandard arrangements can create phase problems.

Example:

```lisp
(defmacro my-when (test &body body)
  `(if ,test (progn ,@body)))

(defun f (x)
  (my-when x
    (print x)))
```

This normal pattern is fine.

Problem:

```lisp
(defun helper (x)
  ...)

(defmacro m (x)
  (helper x))
```

During compilation, `helper` may not be available for macro expansion unless phase handling is correct.

Safer:

```lisp
(eval-when (:compile-toplevel :load-toplevel :execute)
  (defun helper (x)
    ...))

(defmacro m (x)
  (helper x))
```

**Professional rule:** macro definitions are usually fine at top level; helper functions used during macro expansion require phase awareness.



### I.23 Runtime initialization vs load-time initialization

Distinguish these:

| Initialization kind      | Example                       | Best location         |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------- | --------------------- |
| Define default variable  | `(defparameter *timeout* 30)` | top level             |
| Create static table      | `defparameter` or function    | top level if harmless |
| Read user config         | file I/O                      | startup function      |
| Open network socket      | external resource             | startup/lifecycle     |
| Start worker thread      | runtime lifecycle             | explicit start        |
| Register internal syntax | maybe top level               | must be idempotent    |
| Load plugin files        | deployment/runtime policy     | explicit function     |
| Connect to DB            | external resource             | explicit function     |

Bad:

```lisp
(defparameter *config* (load-config "/etc/app.conf"))
```

This reads external files during load.

Better:

```lisp
(defparameter *default-config*
  (make-config :host "localhost" :port 8080))

(defun load-application-config (path)
  (parse-config-file path))

(defun main (&key config-path)
  (let ((config (if config-path
                    (load-application-config config-path)
                    *default-config*)))
    (start-service config)))
```

**Professional rule:** top-level initialization should be deterministic, local, and safe to repeat.



### I.24 Image saving and deployment caution

Some implementations allow saving an image or building an executable.

Conceptually:

```text
load systems -> initialize image -> save image/executable
```

Risk: the saved image may contain accidental state.

| Accidentally captured state | Example                          |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Debug variables             | `*debug*` left true              |
| Test data                   | objects created during tests     |
| Open resources              | streams, sockets, DB connections |
| Stale definitions           | old functions/methods            |
| Local paths                 | developer machine paths          |
| Modified packages           | REPL experiments                 |
| Caches                      | non-reproducible runtime data    |
| Threads                     | unsafe saved state               |

Deployment image discipline:

```text
1. Start fresh image.
2. Load production systems.
3. Initialize only intended state.
4. Build/save artifact.
5. Run smoke test.
```

**Professional rule:** never save a long-lived development image as a production artifact without a controlled build script.



### I.25 Debugger and restarts in a live image

When a condition is unhandled, the debugger may appear. The debugger can inspect stack frames and offer restarts.

Example:

```lisp
(error "Something failed.")
```

In the debugger, possible actions may include aborting, retrying, using a value, skipping, or other restarts established by code.

This is powerful because the developer can:

| Action            | Meaning                         |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------- |
| inspect variables | understand runtime state        |
| invoke restart    | recover without killing process |
| redefine function | patch behavior                  |
| continue          | resume execution                |
| abort             | unwind to REPL/top-level        |

But it can hide source problems.

**Failure-first explanation: debugger recovery is not a permanent fix**

If a restart supplies a missing value interactively, the program may continue. But unless source/configuration is corrected, the same failure returns in a clean run.

**Professional rule:** every debugger recovery should lead to a source, config, or test change if it reveals a real defect.



### I.26 Trace, inspect, and macroexpand in image debugging

Useful tools:

| Tool            | Use                                                           |
| --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `trace`         | Observe function calls                                        |
| `untrace`       | Stop tracing                                                  |
| `inspect`       | Inspect object interactively, implementation/editor-dependent |
| `describe`      | Print object description                                      |
| `macroexpand-1` | See one macro expansion step                                  |
| `macroexpand`   | Expand top-level macro form fully                             |
| `fboundp`       | Check function binding                                        |
| `boundp`        | Check variable binding                                        |
| `find-symbol`   | Check package symbol                                          |
| `class-of`      | Inspect class of object                                       |
| `type-of`       | Inspect type                                                  |
| `documentation` | Read docstring                                                |

Examples:

```lisp
(trace parse-config)
```

```lisp
(untrace parse-config)
```

```lisp
(describe user)
```

```lisp
(macroexpand-1
 '(when-let (user (find-user id))
    (notify user)))
```

```lisp
(fboundp 'old-helper)
```

```lisp
(find-symbol "OLD-API" "EXAMPLE")
```

**Professional rule:** use image tools to diagnose, then verify diagnosis through source and fresh load.



### I.27 Interactive patching workflow

A disciplined live-debugging workflow:

```text
1. Reproduce bug in current image.
2. Inspect runtime state.
3. Trace or macroexpand if needed.
4. Patch function/method/macro interactively.
5. Confirm behavior.
6. Apply the patch to source files.
7. Recompile/reload affected system.
8. Run tests.
9. Restart fresh image.
10. Load and test again.
```

| Patch type                  | Extra step                            |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Function patch              | Recompile function and tests          |
| Macro patch                 | Recompile macro users                 |
| Class patch                 | Consider existing instances/migration |
| Method patch                | Check old methods not stale           |
| Package patch               | Fresh image required                  |
| ASDF patch                  | Reload system from clean state        |
| Dependency patch            | Fresh image required                  |
| Top-level side-effect patch | Test reload twice                     |

**Professional rule:** the interactive patch is a hypothesis. The source patch plus fresh test is the fix.



### I.28 Fresh-image smoke tests

A smoke test is a minimal test that proves the system loads and basic public API works.

Example smoke file or REPL sequence:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)

(let ((user (example:make-user :name "Ada"
                               :email "ada@example.com")))
  (assert (example:user-p user))
  (assert (string= "Ada" (example:user-name user))))

(example:main :host "localhost" :port 8080)
```

For a library:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :small-lib)

(assert (string= "ada"
                 (small-lib:normalize-name " Ada ")))
```

For tests:

```lisp
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

**Professional rule:** every serious project should have a documented fresh-image smoke path.



### I.29 Clean-image CI concept

Even without a full CI system, the concept is simple:

```text
fresh environment
  -> install/load dependencies
  -> load ASDF system
  -> run tests
  -> optionally build artifact
  -> run smoke test
```

CI catches the same problems as manual fresh-image testing, but consistently.

| CI step              | Catches                    |
| -------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Fresh Lisp startup   | image-state dependency     |
| Quicklisp/dist setup | dependency assumptions     |
| ASDF load            | system graph errors        |
| Test system load     | test dependency issues     |
| Test run             | behavior regressions       |
| Build artifact       | deployment-specific issues |
| Smoke command        | startup/config issues      |

**Professional rule:** the stronger the REPL workflow, the more important clean-image verification becomes.



### I.30 Common image-related anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern                                | Problem                           | Better                      |
| ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Manual load order in REPL                   | Non-reproducible                  | ASDF components             |
| Function exists only in REPL                | Lost on restart                   | Save to source              |
| Deleted source but old function still works | Stale image                       | Fresh image test            |
| Macro changed but users not recompiled      | Old expansion                     | Recompile dependents        |
| Package edited but old symbols remain       | Stale package state               | Fresh image                 |
| Top-level starts service                    | Load-time side effect             | Explicit `main`             |
| `defvar` expected to reset                  | It preserves existing value       | Use `defparameter` or reset |
| Saved dev image deployed                    | Captures accidental state         | Controlled build image      |
| Broad handler hides load errors             | Debugging hard                    | Specific handlers/tests     |
| Ignoring compiler warnings                  | Hidden undefined/stale references | Fix warnings                |
| Tests depend on prior REPL evals            | Non-reproducible                  | ASDF test system            |
| Internal dependency quickloaded manually    | Missing `:depends-on`             | Declare dependency          |



### I.31 Runtime/image diagnostic table

| Symptom                                   | Likely cause                     | Diagnostic                            | Fix                                   |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Works before restart, fails after restart | Definition only in image         | Search source, ASDF load              | Save definition to source             |
| Works in REPL, fails in ASDF load         | Missing dependency/order         | `.asd`, compiler output               | Add `:depends-on` or component order  |
| Deleted function still callable           | Stale function binding           | `(fboundp 'name)` in warm/fresh image | Restart or unbind; rely on fresh load |
| Deleted method still dispatches           | Stale method object              | inspect generic methods               | Fresh image or remove method          |
| Changed macro has no effect               | Old expansion                    | `macroexpand-1`, recompile users      | Recompile dependent files             |
| Variable does not reset on reload         | `defvar`                         | inspect value after reload            | use `defparameter` or explicit reset  |
| Loading starts app                        | top-level side effect            | inspect source top level              | move to `main`                        |
| Package conflict appears only fresh       | package definition/order issue   | `find-package`, `find-symbol`         | fix `defpackage` and ASDF             |
| Compiler says undefined function          | file order/dependency            | ASDF order, package imports           | define/load earlier                   |
| Runtime no-applicable-method after reload | method not loaded or stale class | `class-of`, methods                   | fix method/system load                |
| Config read during compile/load           | top-level external I/O           | source audit                          | move to startup                       |
| Different behavior compiled vs REPL       | phase/declaration/macro issue    | warnings, macro expansion             | fix phase assumptions                 |



### I.32 Final runtime phase decision tree

```text
BUG OR SURPRISE
├── Reader/package error?
│   ├── Check *package*, defpackage, package prefixes.
│   └── Check ASDF loads packages.lisp first.
│
├── Macro-related?
│   ├── macroexpand-1
│   ├── check evaluation count/capture
│   └── recompile dependent code after macro changes.
│
├── Compile-time error?
│   ├── Check undefined functions/macros.
│   ├── Check ASDF component order.
│   └── Check eval-when for macro helpers.
│
├── Load-time surprise?
│   ├── Look for top-level side effects.
│   ├── Check defvar/defparameter behavior.
│   └── Ensure loading only defines.
│
├── Runtime logic bug?
│   ├── trace, inspect, describe
│   ├── check equality/nil/mutation
│   └── test behavior.
│
├── Works only in warm image?
│   ├── stale definition?
│   ├── manual dependency?
│   ├── package state?
│   └── restart fresh image.
│
└── Deployment/image issue?
    ├── build from fresh image
    ├── avoid saving accidental state
    └── run smoke test.
```

### I.33 Final clean-image protocol

Use this as a final project sanity check:

```text
1. Quit the current Lisp image.
2. Start a fresh Lisp image.
3. Load project only through ASDF or Quicklisp.
4. Do not manually define packages, functions, variables, or macros.
5. Run the test system.
6. Call one or two public API smoke tests.
7. Confirm no unexpected load-time side effects occurred.
8. Review compiler warnings.
9. If behavior differs from the warm REPL, trust the fresh image.
```

Minimal commands:

```lisp
(asdf:load-system :example)
(asdf:test-system :example)
```

Optional smoke call:

```lisp
(example:main :host "localhost" :port 8080)
```

### I.34 Final rule

**Final conclusion:** Common Lisp’s live image is a productivity engine, but not a proof of reproducibility. The professional workflow is to exploit the image for exploration, debugging, macro expansion, inspection, and recovery, then verify everything from source through ASDF in a fresh image. When in doubt, restart Lisp and load the system cleanly.

## Appendix J — Performance and Consing Checklist

### J.1 What this appendix is for

This appendix is a **practical performance checklist** for Common Lisp. It is not a replacement for profiling, implementation manuals, or compiler notes. Its purpose is to identify the most common sources of accidental cost:

| Cost source             | Typical cause                                          |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Consing                 | lists, temporary objects, intermediate sequences       |
| Generic arithmetic      | unknown numeric types                                  |
| Boxing                  | numeric values not represented unboxed                 |
| Generic dispatch        | CLOS calls in hot paths                                |
| Sequence copying        | `remove`, `substitute`, `copy-seq`, `append`           |
| Destructive operations  | mutation bugs or unexpected sharing                    |
| String allocation       | `format nil`, concatenation, temporary strings         |
| Hash-table cost         | wrong equality test, rehashing, poor key normalization |
| Condition overhead      | signaling in normal hot paths                          |
| FFI/subprocess overhead | too many small boundary crossings                      |

**Core rule:** optimize the representation and allocation pattern before lowering safety or adding declarations.



### J.2 Performance workflow

A disciplined Common Lisp performance workflow:

```text
1. Write clear correct code.
2. Add tests.
3. Identify realistic workload.
4. Profile time and allocation.
5. Read compiler warnings and notes.
6. Check data representation.
7. Remove accidental consing.
8. Add local type declarations only when true.
9. Re-measure.
10. Keep portability and maintainability boundaries explicit.
```

| Step                  | Purpose                                               |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Correctness first     | Fast wrong code is useless                            |
| Tests                 | Protect refactoring                                   |
| Workload              | Avoid optimizing imaginary cases                      |
| Profiling             | Locate actual cost                                    |
| Compiler notes        | Find type/allocation uncertainty                      |
| Representation review | Choose lists/vectors/hash tables/classes deliberately |
| Consing review        | Reduce avoidable allocation                           |
| Local declarations    | Help compiler where invariants are known              |
| Re-measurement        | Confirm improvement                                   |
| Boundary isolation    | Keep implementation-specific optimization controlled  |

**Professional rule:** never begin with `(optimize (speed 3) (safety 0))`. Begin with measurement and representation.



### J.3 The main cost categories

| Category               | Common examples                     | First diagnostic question                             |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Algorithmic complexity | repeated `append`, nested scans     | Is the algorithm accidentally quadratic?              |
| Allocation / consing   | `mapcar`, `list`, `format nil`      | Is the code creating avoidable temporary objects?     |
| Representation         | list random access, generic objects | Is the data structure suited to the access pattern?   |
| Numeric dispatch       | generic `+`, `*`, `/`               | Does the compiler know numeric types?                 |
| CLOS dispatch          | generic functions in tight loops    | Is runtime dispatch semantically needed here?         |
| Equality cost          | `equalp`, deep keys                 | Is the equality predicate too expensive or too loose? |
| I/O cost               | many small writes                   | Should output be buffered or batched?                 |
| Boundary crossing      | FFI, subprocess, network            | Are many tiny calls crossing a boundary?              |
| Concurrency cost       | locks, contention                   | Is shared mutable state forcing synchronization?      |
| Compilation/loading    | huge macros, generated code         | Is macro expansion or load-time work excessive?       |



### J.4 Consing

**Consing** usually means allocating cons cells, but in Common Lisp performance discussions it often refers more broadly to allocation pressure.

Common consing sources:

| Form/pattern               | Allocation                                            |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `(cons a b)`               | one cons cell                                         |
| `(list a b c)`             | one cons per element                                  |
| `mapcar`                   | result list                                           |
| `append`                   | copies earlier list structure                         |
| `copy-list`                | copied top-level cons cells                           |
| `copy-tree`                | copied cons tree                                      |
| `remove`, `substitute`     | result sequence/list                                  |
| `format nil`               | new string                                            |
| `with-output-to-string`    | generated string                                      |
| closure creation           | function/environment object, implementation-dependent |
| bignum/ratio arithmetic    | numeric heap objects                                  |
| generic sequence pipelines | intermediate objects                                  |

Example:

```lisp
(mapcar #'print xs)
```

This prints each element but also builds a result list that is probably ignored.

Better:

```lisp
(dolist (x xs)
  (print x))
```

or:

```lisp
(mapc #'print xs)
```

**Professional rule:** if a result list is ignored, do not use `mapcar`.



### J.5 Repeated `append`

Repeated `append` is a classic accidental quadratic pattern.

Bad:

```lisp
(defun collect-active-users (users)
  (let ((result nil))
    (dolist (user users result)
      (when (active-user-p user)
        (setf result (append result (list user)))))))
```

Each `append` traverses the current `result`. For many users, this becomes expensive.

Better:

```lisp
(defun collect-active-users (users)
  (let ((result nil))
    (dolist (user users (nreverse result))
      (when (active-user-p user)
        (push user result)))))
```

Or use `loop`:

```lisp
(defun collect-active-users (users)
  (loop for user in users
        when (active-user-p user)
          collect user))
```

| Pattern                             | Cost                                          |
| ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| repeated `(append result (list x))` | often quadratic                               |
| `push` + `nreverse`                 | linear                                        |
| `loop collect`                      | idiomatic and usually efficient               |
| `nconc`                             | efficient but destructive; requires ownership |

**Professional rule:** build lists with `push` + `nreverse`, `loop collect`, or controlled destructive accumulation. Avoid repeated append in loops.



### J.6 `push` and `nreverse`

Efficient list accumulation commonly uses `push`.

```lisp
(defun squares (xs)
  (let ((result nil))
    (dolist (x xs (nreverse result))
      (push (* x x) result))))
```

Why `nreverse` is safe here:

| Condition                                   | Status |
| ------------------------------------------- | ------ |
| `result` is freshly built                   | yes    |
| no external alias exists                    | yes    |
| destructive reversal cannot surprise caller | yes    |

Unsafe:

```lisp
(defun reverse-shared-list (xs)
  (nreverse xs))
```

This destructively modifies `xs`, which may be shared by the caller.

Safer:

```lisp
(defun reverse-copy (xs)
  (reverse xs))
```

**Professional rule:** destructive operations are efficient when the data is fresh or owned. They are dangerous when data is shared.



### J.7 `mapcar`, `mapc`, `dolist`, and `loop`

| Task                              | Prefer                            | Reason                         |
| --------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Transform list and keep result    | `mapcar`                          | Clear list mapping             |
| Traverse for side effects         | `dolist` or `mapc`                | Avoid unused result allocation |
| Accumulate with conditions        | `loop` or `push` + `nreverse`     | Clear and efficient            |
| Traverse general sequence         | `map`, `loop`, sequence functions | Depends on result type         |
| Complex iteration state           | `loop`, `do`, `let` + iteration   | Explicit control               |
| Performance-sensitive simple loop | `dotimes`, `dolist`, `loop`       | Compiler-friendly              |

Transform:

```lisp
(mapcar #'user-name users)
```

Side effects:

```lisp
(dolist (user users)
  (notify user))
```

Conditional collection:

```lisp
(loop for user in users
      when (active-user-p user)
        collect (user-name user))
```

Index loop:

```lisp
(dotimes (i (length vector))
  (process (aref vector i)))
```

**Professional rule:** choose an iteration form by result shape and side-effect policy.



### J.8 Lists vs vectors vs arrays

Lists are excellent for sequential traversal and symbolic structure. They are poor for random access.

| Data structure  | Good for                                                       | Bad for                       |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| List            | sequential traversal, stack-like construction, code/data trees | random access by index        |
| Vector          | indexed access, dense sequence, numeric-ish data               | cheap insertion at front      |
| Array           | multidimensional indexed data                                  | symbolic tree structure       |
| Hash table      | key-value lookup                                               | ordered traversal assumptions |
| Structure/class | named fields and domain objects                                | arbitrary sequence operations |

Bad:

```lisp
(dotimes (i (length xs))
  (process (nth i xs)))
```

For a list, `nth` is linear, so this can become quadratic.

Better for list:

```lisp
(dolist (x xs)
  (process x))
```

Better for indexed access:

```lisp
(dotimes (i (length vector))
  (process (aref vector i)))
```

**Professional rule:** lists are not arrays. If the algorithm needs frequent indexed access, use a vector or array.



### J.9 Copying and destructive sequence operations

Common Lisp has destructive and non-destructive operations. Performance and correctness depend on choosing deliberately.

| Non-destructive | Destructive counterpart    | Notes                             |
| --------------- | -------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `reverse`       | `nreverse`                 | `nreverse` may modify list        |
| `append`        | `nconc`                    | `nconc` links lists destructively |
| `remove`        | `delete`                   | `delete` may alter sequence       |
| `substitute`    | `nsubstitute`              | destructive substitution          |
| `sort`          | no non-destructive default | `sort` is destructive             |
| `copy-seq`      | —                          | shallow copy                      |
| `copy-list`     | —                          | shallow list copy                 |
| `copy-tree`     | —                          | cons tree copy                    |

Important: `sort` is destructive.

Bad if original order needed:

```lisp
(let ((sorted (sort users #'string< :key #'user-name)))
  ...)
```

This may reorder `users`.

Safer:

```lisp
(let ((sorted (sort (copy-seq users) #'string< :key #'user-name)))
  ...)
```

For lists:

```lisp
(let ((sorted (sort (copy-list users) #'string< :key #'user-name)))
  ...)
```

**Professional rule:** assume functions beginning with `n` may modify their arguments. Also remember `sort` is destructive even without an `n`.



### J.10 String allocation

String creation is often hidden.

| Pattern                     | Allocation                              |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| `(format nil "...")`        | new string                              |
| `(concatenate 'string a b)` | new string                              |
| repeated concatenation      | many strings                            |
| `with-output-to-string`     | growing string result                   |
| `string-upcase`             | new or copied string depending function |
| `nstring-upcase`            | destructive string modification         |
| `subseq` on string          | new sequence                            |

Bad repeated concatenation:

```lisp
(defun join-lines-slow (lines)
  (let ((result ""))
    (dolist (line lines result)
      (setf result (concatenate 'string result line #\Newline)))))
```

Better:

```lisp
(defun join-lines (lines)
  (with-output-to-string (stream)
    (dolist (line lines)
      (write-string line stream)
      (terpri stream))))
```

For direct output, avoid building a string at all:

```lisp
(defun write-lines (lines stream)
  (dolist (line lines)
    (write-string line stream)
    (terpri stream)))
```

**Professional rule:** if the final destination is a stream, write to the stream. Do not build a temporary string unless needed.



### J.11 `format` performance

`format` is powerful but can be costly in hot paths.

| Use                         | Judgment                     |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Human-readable output       | Excellent                    |
| Logging                     | Good, but consider frequency |
| Error reports               | Good                         |
| Tight numeric loop          | Usually avoid                |
| Building many small strings | May allocate heavily         |
| Complex format strings      | Can reduce readability       |

Example:

```lisp
(format nil "~A:~D" host port)
```

This allocates a string.

If writing to stream:

```lisp
(format stream "~A:~D" host port)
```

No result string is needed.

For very simple output, direct functions may be clearer:

```lisp
(write-string host stream)
(write-char #\: stream)
(princ port stream)
```

**Professional rule:** `format` is for clarity and formatting power. In hot output paths, consider simpler stream operations.



### J.12 Hash table performance and equality tests

Hash table performance depends heavily on key choice and equality test.

```lisp
(make-hash-table :test 'eql)
(make-hash-table :test 'equal)
(make-hash-table :test 'equalp)
```

| Key type                | Good test             | Notes                       |
| ----------------------- | --------------------- | --------------------------- |
| keywords                | `eq` or `eql`         | symbolic identity           |
| symbols                 | `eq` or `eql`         | identity semantics          |
| fixnums/chars           | `eql`                 | default works               |
| strings                 | `equal`               | content equality            |
| lists/S-expression keys | `equal`               | structural equality         |
| case-insensitive text   | normalize or `equalp` | normalization often clearer |
| complex objects         | custom key extraction | avoid deep object equality  |

Bad:

```lisp
(make-hash-table :test 'eq)
```

for string keys.

Good:

```lisp
(make-hash-table :test 'equal)
```

for string keys.

Avoid using `equalp` by habit:

```lisp
(make-hash-table :test 'equalp)
```

This may be too permissive and more expensive.

Normalize keys when possible:

```lisp
(defun normalize-user-id (id)
  (string-downcase id))
```

Then use `equal` on normalized strings.

**Professional rule:** choose hash table tests by key semantics. Normalize keys at boundaries when appropriate.



### J.13 Hash table growth and pre-sizing

Hash tables may resize as they grow. If size is predictable, specify initial size.

```lisp
(make-hash-table :test 'equal :size expected-count)
```

Example:

```lisp
(defun index-users (users)
  (let ((table (make-hash-table :test 'equal
                                :size (length users))))
    (dolist (user users table)
      (setf (gethash (user-id user) table) user))))
```

| Option              | Purpose                  |
| ------------------- | ------------------------ |
| `:size`             | Initial approximate size |
| `:rehash-size`      | Growth behavior          |
| `:rehash-threshold` | Load factor threshold    |
| `:test`             | Equality semantics       |

Do not obsess over these options before profiling. But for large known datasets, `:size` is a simple improvement.

**Professional rule:** pre-size large hash tables when the expected number of entries is known.



### J.14 Numeric exactness and accidental ratios

Common Lisp preserves exact arithmetic where possible.

```lisp
(/ 1 3)
;; => 1/3
```

This is an exact ratio, not a float.

Good for exact symbolic/math reasoning. Bad if unintentional in numeric performance code.

Floating-point:

```lisp
(/ 1.0d0 3.0d0)
```

Potential issue:

```lisp
(defun average (sum count)
  (/ sum count))
```

If `sum` and `count` are integers, this may produce a ratio.

If floating result is desired:

```lisp
(defun average (sum count)
  (/ (coerce sum 'double-float)
     (coerce count 'double-float)))
```

or ensure input types are already floats.

| Domain                   | Prefer                                             |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Exact rational math      | ratios are good                                    |
| Financial decimal policy | explicit representation; do not drift accidentally |
| Numeric simulation       | floats, often `double-float`                       |
| Statistical computation  | floats, usually `double-float`                     |
| Symbolic algebra         | exact integers/ratios                              |
| Performance numeric loop | declared numeric types and specialized arrays      |

**Professional rule:** decide exact vs floating arithmetic explicitly.



### J.15 Generic arithmetic

Common Lisp arithmetic is generic by default.

```lisp
(+ a b)
```

works across integers, floats, ratios, complexes, and more. That flexibility can cost in hot code if the compiler does not know types.

Generic arithmetic cost may include:

| Cost                 | Cause                                |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Type dispatch        | numeric types unknown                |
| Boxing/unboxing      | value representation not specialized |
| Allocation           | bignums, ratios, complexes           |
| Missed optimizations | compiler cannot infer exact type     |
| Safety checks        | high safety settings preserve checks |

Performance-sensitive example:

```lisp
(defun dot-product (a b)
  (declare (type (simple-array double-float (*)) a b)
           (optimize (speed 3) (safety 1)))
  (let ((sum 0.0d0))
    (declare (type double-float sum))
    (dotimes (i (length a) sum)
      (incf sum (* (aref a i)
                   (aref b i))))))
```

Key declarations:

| Declaration                                | Meaning                     |
| ------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- |
| `(type (simple-array double-float (*)) a)` | vector of double floats     |
| `(type double-float sum)`                  | accumulator is double float |
| `(optimize (speed 3) (safety 1))`          | local speed preference      |
| `0.0d0`                                    | double-float initial value  |

**Professional rule:** declarations should state true invariants. Do not lie to the compiler.



### J.16 Fixnums, bignums, and integer performance

Common Lisp integers are exact and can grow beyond machine word size.

| Type              | Meaning                                | Cost                    |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| fixnum            | small implementation-supported integer | efficient               |
| bignum            | arbitrary precision integer            | heap allocation, slower |
| integer           | exact integer, may be fixnum or bignum | flexible                |
| `(integer 0 100)` | constrained integer type               | helps compiler if true  |

Example:

```lisp
(defun count-items (xs)
  (let ((count 0))
    (declare (type fixnum count))
    (dolist (x xs count)
      (declare (ignore x))
      (incf count))))
```

Caution: if `count` exceeds fixnum range, behavior under low safety can become problematic depending on implementation and declarations.

**Professional rule:** use fixnum declarations only when range is truly bounded or acceptable under the implementation’s assumptions.



### J.17 Specialized arrays

For numeric code, specialized arrays matter.

Generic vector:

```lisp
(vector 1.0d0 2.0d0 3.0d0)
```

The implementation may still represent elements generically depending on construction and declarations.

Explicit specialized array:

```lisp
(make-array 1000 :element-type 'double-float
                 :initial-element 0.0d0)
```

Type declaration:

```lisp
(declare (type (simple-array double-float (*)) v))
```

| Array kind                | Use                                                       |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| general vector            | arbitrary objects                                         |
| simple vector             | general object vector                                     |
| specialized numeric array | numeric performance                                       |
| simple-array              | non-adjustable, no fill pointer, often easier to optimize |
| multidimensional array    | matrix/grid data                                          |

Example:

```lisp
(defun sum-double-vector (v)
  (declare (type (simple-array double-float (*)) v)
           (optimize (speed 3) (safety 1)))
  (let ((sum 0.0d0))
    (declare (type double-float sum))
    (dotimes (i (length v) sum)
      (incf sum (aref v i)))))
```

**Professional rule:** serious numeric code usually needs specialized arrays and accurate declarations.



### J.18 Type declarations

Declarations tell the compiler what is true.

```lisp
(defun add-fixnums (a b)
  (declare (type fixnum a b))
  (+ a b))
```

They do not parse or convert values.

Bad boundary code:

```lisp
(defun parse-count (x)
  (declare (type fixnum x))
  x)
```

This does not validate `x`.

Better:

```lisp
(defun parse-count (x)
  (unless (typep x '(integer 0 *))
    (error "Invalid count: ~S" x))
  x)
```

Then internal hot path can declare:

```lisp
(defun use-count (count)
  (declare (type fixnum count))
  ...)
```

| Declaration      | Use                          |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------- |
| `type`           | State variable/form type     |
| `ftype`          | State function type          |
| `inline`         | Suggest inlining             |
| `notinline`      | Prevent inlining             |
| `optimize`       | Local compiler policy        |
| `dynamic-extent` | State object does not escape |
| `ignore`         | Suppress unused warning      |
| `ignorable`      | May or may not be used       |

**Professional rule:** validate at boundaries; declare after invariants are established.



### J.19 `optimize` declarations

Optimization policy affects compiler behavior.

```lisp
(declare (optimize (speed 3)
                   (safety 1)
                   (debug 1)))
```

Common qualities:

| Quality             | Meaning                          |
| ------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `speed`             | favor faster code                |
| `safety`            | runtime checks and safe behavior |
| `debug`             | debuggability                    |
| `space`             | code/data size                   |
| `compilation-speed` | faster compilation               |

Bad global habit:

```lisp
(declaim (optimize (speed 3) (safety 0)))
```

This can make wrong declarations dangerous and debugging difficult.

Better local use:

```lisp
(defun hot-loop (v)
  (declare (optimize (speed 3) (safety 1)))
  ...)
```

For development:

```lisp
(declaim (optimize (safety 3) (debug 3) (speed 1)))
```

may be more useful.

**Professional rule:** keep aggressive optimization local to measured hot paths.



### J.20 Dynamic extent

`dynamic-extent` tells the compiler that an object will not escape the dynamic extent of the form.

Example:

```lisp
(defun call-with-temp-list (fn a b)
  (let ((args (list a b)))
    (declare (dynamic-extent args))
    (apply fn args)))
```

This may allow stack allocation or other optimizations.

Caution: the declaration must be true. Do not let the object escape.

Bad:

```lisp
(defun bad ()
  (let ((xs (list 1 2 3)))
    (declare (dynamic-extent xs))
    xs))
```

This returns `xs`, so it escapes.

| Use                            | Risk                    |
| ------------------------------ | ----------------------- |
| temporary rest/list structures | useful                  |
| temporary closures             | sometimes useful        |
| FFI temporary objects          | implementation-specific |
| returned objects               | invalid                 |
| stored objects                 | invalid                 |
| captured by closure            | invalid                 |

**Professional rule:** use `dynamic-extent` only when lifetime is obvious and local.



### J.21 CLOS dispatch cost

Generic function calls are powerful but not free.

```lisp
(render object stream)
```

If `render` is a generic function, CLOS dispatch occurs.

Potential costs:

| Cost source        | Explanation                              |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------- |
| method lookup      | determine applicable methods             |
| method combination | before/after/around/primary              |
| dynamic dispatch   | runtime types matter                     |
| slot access        | accessor generic function or slot lookup |
| allocation         | CLOS instances                           |
| abstraction layers | more calls and wrappers                  |

Usually this cost is acceptable. In hot loops, reconsider.

Bad hot path:

```lisp
(dotimes (i n)
  (incf sum (value-at object i)))
```

if `value-at` is a generic function and `n` is huge.

Possible alternative:

```lisp
(let ((vector (object-values object)))
  (dotimes (i n)
    (incf sum (aref vector i))))
```

or dispatch once outside:

```lisp
(defmethod sum-values ((object vector-backed-object))
  (let ((values (object-values object)))
    (sum-double-vector values)))
```

| Situation                      | Strategy                          |
| ------------------------------ | --------------------------------- |
| Open protocol                  | Use CLOS                          |
| Dispatch once, loop many times | Move dispatch outside loop        |
| Closed internal hot loop       | Ordinary function or local helper |
| Slot access repeated           | Extract slot once                 |
| Numeric data inside objects    | Use specialized arrays internally |

**Professional rule:** CLOS is for protocol extensibility. Avoid repeated generic dispatch inside inner loops when the dispatch result can be hoisted.



### J.22 Closures and retention

Closures can retain captured objects.

```lisp
(defun make-lookup-function (huge-table)
  (lambda (key)
    (gethash key huge-table)))
```

As long as the returned function is reachable, `huge-table` remains reachable.

| Closure pattern           | Risk                |
| ------------------------- | ------------------- |
| captures large table      | memory retained     |
| captures object graph     | unexpected lifetime |
| many closures in loop     | allocation          |
| captures mutable variable | shared hidden state |
| short callback            | usually fine        |

Example accidental retention:

```lisp
(defun make-filter (users)
  (let ((index (index-users users)))
    (lambda (id)
      (gethash id index))))
```

This may be intentional. But if `users` or `index` is huge, the closure’s lifetime matters.

**Professional rule:** closures are good for private behavior, but inspect what they capture when memory matters.



### J.23 Conditions in hot paths
